## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define frequency domain filtering. State the convolution theorem and explain its significance in image filtering.**

**Model Answer:**

Frequency domain filtering is a technique in which an image $f(x,y)$ is first transformed to the frequency domain using the 2-D Discrete Fourier Transform $F(u,v)$, modified by multiplying with a filter function $H(u,v)$, and then transformed back to the spatial domain using the inverse DFT.

**Convolution Theorem Statement:**

$$g(x,y) = f(x,y) * h(x,y) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad G(u,v) = F(u,v) \cdot H(u,v)$$

**Significance:**

1. **Computational Efficiency:** Multiplication in the frequency domain replaces expensive spatial convolution, reducing complexity from $O(MN \cdot MN)$ to $O(MN)$.
2. **Filter Design Intuition:** Frequency-based specification of filters (cutoff, order) is often more natural than spatial-kernel design.
3. **Noise Reduction & Sharpening:** Selective attenuation of frequency bands allows precise control over smoothing and edge enhancement.
4. **FFT Acceleration:** Fast Fourier Transform algorithms make the entire pipeline efficient for large medical images.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** A 3-mark answer should include the DFT equation, the convolution theorem, and **at least three significance points** with one line each.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Differentiate between Ideal, Butterworth, and Gaussian lowpass filters in the frequency domain.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Ideal LPF | Butterworth LPF | Gaussian LPF |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|--------------|
| **Equation** | $H = 1$ if $D \leq D_0$, else $0$ | $H = 1/[1+(D/D_0)^{2n}]$ | $H = e^{-D^2/2\sigma^2}$ |
| **Cutoff Sharpness** | Infinite (abrupt) | Controlled by order $n$ | Smooth bell-shape |
| **Ringing** | Severe ringing | Mild for higher $n$ | No ringing |
| **Spatial Kernel** | $\text{sinc}$ (infinite support) | Inverse Butterworth | Gaussian |
| **Practical Use** | Theoretical baseline | Tunable smoothness | Preferred for medical images |

The Ideal LPF has a sharp step transition causing Gibbs phenomenon (ringing). The Butterworth LPF provides a controllable transition with mild ringing that increases with order $n$. The Gaussian LPF has the smoothest transition and introduces no ringing, making it ideal for medical applications where artifact-free denoising is critical.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the steps involved in frequency domain filtering of a digital image. Discuss why image padding is required. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram of Frequency Domain Filtering:**

The complete filtering pipeline consists of the following sequential steps:

**Step 1 — Padding the Input Image:**

The input image $f(x,y)$ of size $M \times N$ is padded with zeros to form $f_p(x,y)$ of size $P \times Q$ where $P = 2M$ and $Q = 2N$.

**Why padding is required:** When two finite-size images are convolved in the spatial domain, the result has a size larger than either input. The DFT inherently performs *circular* (periodic) convolution. If we use direct multiplication in the frequency domain without padding, the result corresponds to periodic convolution, which causes **wraparound error** — pixels from one edge wrap around and contaminate the opposite edge. Padding to twice the size eliminates this artifact.

**Step 2 — Centering the Transform:**

$$f_p(x,y) \cdot (-1)^{x+y}$$

This multiplication shifts the zero-frequency (DC) component to the center of the frequency rectangle for easier visualization and filter design.

**Step 3 — Forward DFT:**

$$F(u,v) = \text{DFT}\{f_p(x,y) \cdot (-1)^{x+y}\}$$

**Step 4 — Filter Multiplication:**

$$G(u,v) = H(u,v) \cdot F(u,v)$$

where $H(u,v)$ is the filter transfer function (LPF, HPF, etc.).

**Step 5 — Decentering:**

$$G(u,v) \cdot (-1)^{u+v}$$

**Step 6 — Inverse DFT:**

$$g_p(x,y) = \text{Real}\{\text{IDFT}[G(u,v) \cdot (-1)^{u+v}]\}$$

The real part is taken to discard negligible imaginary components from numerical errors.

**Step 7 — Cropping:**

The final filtered image $g(x,y)$ is obtained by extracting the top-left $M \times N$ region from $g_p(x,y)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (7 marks):** Block diagram (2), 7 steps listed and explained (3), padding justification (2).

---

**(b)** A medical X-ray image of size $512 \times 512$ is to be smoothed using a Butterworth lowpass filter of order $n = 2$ with cutoff frequency $D_0 = 60$ pixels. **(7 marks)**

**(i) Write the expression for the Butterworth LPF transfer function. (2 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

$$H_{\text{BLPF}}(u,v) = \frac{1}{1 + \left[ D(u,v) / D_0 \right]^{2n}}$$

where $D(u,v) = \sqrt{(u - M/2)^2 + (v - N/2)^2}$, $D_0 = 60$, $n = 2$.

Substituting $D_0 = 60$ and $n = 2$:

$$H_{\text{BLPF}}(u,v) = \frac{1}{1 + \left[ D(u,v) / 60 \right]^{4}}$$

---

**(ii) Compute the filter response at the frequency point $(u, v) = (312, 280)$. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Centered coordinates:**

$$u' = 312 - \frac{512}{2} = 312 - 256 = 56$$

$$v' = 280 - \frac{512}{2} = 280 - 256 = 24$$

**Step 2 — Distance from center:**

$$\begin{aligned}
D(u,v) &= \sqrt{56^2 + 24^2} \\
&= \sqrt{3136 + 576} \\
&= \sqrt{3712} \\
&\approx 60.93 \text{ pixels}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Butterworth response:**

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{BLPF}}(u,v) &= \frac{1}{1 + (60.93 / 60)^{4}} \\
&= \frac{1}{1 + (1.0155)^{4}} \\
&= \frac{1}{1 + 1.0632} \\
&= \frac{1}{2.0632} \\
&\approx 0.4847
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

Since $D \approx D_0$, this frequency point lies near the cutoff, where the Butterworth filter passes approximately **48.47%** of the original magnitude — exactly as expected (the half-power point gives ~0.5 at $D = D_0$).

---

**(iii) Discuss the effect of increasing the filter order $n$ on the Butterworth LPF output. (2 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

As the order $n$ of the Butterworth LPF increases:
- The transition from passband to stopband becomes **sharper** (more ideal-like behavior).
- **Ringing artifacts** in the spatial domain become more pronounced.
- For $n = 1$: smooth, gentle transition with no significant ringing.
- For $n \geq 2$: increasingly sharp cutoff with visible ringing around edges.
- As $n \to \infty$: the filter approaches the **Ideal LPF**, with severe ringing (Gibbs phenomenon).

In medical imaging, lower orders ($n = 1$ or $2$) are preferred to balance denoising effectiveness with minimal artifact introduction.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the working of a high-boost filter in the frequency domain. Derive its transfer function. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept:** A high-boost filter enhances high-frequency components (edges) while *preserving* the low-frequency background information. This overcomes the drawback of pure highpass filtering, which removes all DC and low-frequency content, resulting in a "hollow" or "darkened" image.

**Working Principle:**

The high-boost image is obtained by combining a fraction of the original image with the highpass-filtered version:

$$g_{\text{HB}}(x,y) = A \cdot f(x,y) - f_{\text{LP}}(x,y), \quad A \geq 1$$

**Derivation of the Transfer Function:**

**Step 1 — Take the Fourier transform of both sides:**

$$G_{\text{HB}}(u,v) = A \cdot F(u,v) - F_{\text{LP}}(u,v)$$

**Step 2 — Note that $F_{\text{LP}}(u,v) = H_{\text{LP}}(u,v) \cdot F(u,v)$:**

$$G_{\text{HB}}(u,v) = A \cdot F(u,v) - H_{\text{LP}}(u,v) \cdot F(u,v)$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $F(u,v)$:**

$$G_{\text{HB}}(u,v) = \left[ A - H_{\text{LP}}(u,v) \right] \cdot F(u,v)$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of $H_{\text{HP}}(u,v) = 1 - H_{\text{LP}}(u,v)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{HB}}(u,v) &= A - H_{\text{LP}}(u,v) \\
&= A - \left[ 1 - H_{\text{HP}}(u,v) \right] \\
&= (A - 1) + H_{\text{HP}}(u,v)
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{H_{\text{HB}}(u,v) = (A - 1) + H_{\text{HP}}(u,v)}$$

**Special Cases:**

- $A = 1$: $H_{\text{HB}} = H_{\text{HP}}$ — pure highpass filtering.
- $A > 1$: Boost factor — adds $(A-1)$ constant to the response, preserving the DC term.

**Medical Application:** Mammographic image enhancement for micro-calcification detection, where both tissue background and fine calcifications must be visible simultaneously.

---

**(b)** With mathematical expressions, explain the frequency-domain Laplacian filter used for image sharpening. How is the sharpened image obtained? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept:** The Laplacian is a second-order derivative operator that is isotropic (rotation-invariant) and excellent for edge detection. In the frequency domain, it has a simple closed-form transfer function.

**Step 1 — Spatial Laplacian definition:**

$$\nabla^2 f(x,y) = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y^2}$$

**Step 2 — DFT differentiation property:**

$$\mathcal{F}\left\{ \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2} \right\} = -4\pi^2 u^2 F(u,v), \quad \mathcal{F}\left\{ \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y^2} \right\} = -4\pi^2 v^2 F(u,v)$$

**Step 3 — Laplacian transfer function (uncentered):**

$$H_{\text{LAP}}(u,v) = -4\pi^2 (u^2 + v^2)$$

**Step 4 — Centered form (with image dimensions $M \times N$):**

$$H_{\text{LAP}}(u,v) = -4\pi^2 \left[ \left( u - \frac{M}{2} \right)^2 + \left( v - \frac{N}{2} \right)^2 \right]$$

**Step 5 — Sharpened image in frequency domain:**

$$G(u,v) = F(u,v) + H_{\text{LAP}}(u,v) \cdot F(u,v) = \left[ 1 + H_{\text{LAP}}(u,v) \right] F(u,v)$$

**Step 6 — Sharpened image in spatial domain:**

$$g(x,y) = f(x,y) + c \cdot \nabla^2 f(x,y)$$

where $c = -1$ is used because the Laplacian is negative-definite:

$$g(x,y) = f(x,y) - \nabla^2 f(x,y)$$

**Characteristics of the Laplacian Filter:**

- **Isotropic Response:** Equally sensitive to edges in all directions (no preferred orientation).
- **Zero Crossing Detection:** Edge locations correspond to zero-crossings of $\nabla^2 f$.
- **High-Pass Nature:** $H_{\text{LAP}} \to 0$ as $D \to 0$ (DC) and grows with $D$.
- **No Parameters:** Unlike Butterworth (which needs $n, D_0$), the Laplacian is parameter-free.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently forget the centering of $(u,v)$ coordinates when writing the Laplacian transfer function. If $H_{\text{LAP}}(u,v) = -4\pi^2(u^2 + v^2)$ is used without centering, the Laplacian is centered at the corner $(0,0)$ of the frequency plane, **not** at the center. This leads to a **fundamentally wrong filter** that fails to produce the correct sharpening effect. Always substitute $u \to u - M/2$ and $v \to v - N/2$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2: Frequency Domain Filtering**

- **Convolution Theorem:** Spatial convolution ↔ Frequency multiplication. This is the *core* reason for using frequency domain.
- **Padding is mandatory:** Use $P = 2M$, $Q = 2N$ to avoid wraparound error.
- **Centering with $(-1)^{x+y}$:** Brings DC to center; must be done before DFT and undone after IDFT.
- **Distance formula:** $D(u,v) = \sqrt{(u-M/2)^2 + (v-N/2)^2}$.
- **Three LPF types:** Ideal (sharp, ringing), Butterworth (tunable, mild ringing), Gaussian (smooth, no ringing).
- **Three HPF types:** Mirror the LPF definitions with the $1 - H_{\text{LP}}$ complement.
- **High-Boost:** $H_{\text{HB}} = (A-1) + H_{\text{HP}}$; preserves low frequencies while boosting edges.
- **Laplacian:** $H_{\text{LAP}} = -4\pi^2[(u-M/2)^2 + (v-N/2)^2]$; isotropic and parameter-free.
- **Sharpening equation:** $g = f + c \cdot \nabla^2 f$ with $c = -1$ when $H_{\text{LAP}}$ is negative.
- **Half-power point:** For Butterworth, $H = 0.5$ at $D = D_0$.
- **Gaussian uniqueness:** $H_{\text{LP}} + H_{\text{HP}} = 1$ for **all** $D$ (true for Gaussian only).
- **Real output:** After IDFT, take the **real part** to discard numerical imaginary noise.
- **Medical relevance:** LPF → denoising (CT, MRI, ultrasound); HPF/high-boost → edge enhancement (mammography, bone imaging).
- **Order-of-merit question:** Most commonly tested formula is the Butterworth LPF/HPF response.
- **Common KTU mistake:** Writing $D(u,v) = u^2 + v^2$ instead of using the centered form. Always **shift** the coordinates.
- **Gibbs phenomenon:** Ideal filters exhibit ringing; avoid in clinical applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Filtering in Frequency Domain: Smoothing and Sharpening Frequency Domain Filters**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Homomorphic Filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEBT833 - MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Homomorphic Filtering # 1. Homomorphic Filtering — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Homomorphic Filtering** is a frequency-domain image enhancement technique that simultaneously normalizes illumination across an image and sharpens its reflectance (detail) content. It exploits the **Illumination–Reflectance Model** of image formation, in which an image $f(x,y)$ is expressed as the **element-wise product** of an illumination component $i(x,y)$ and a reflectance component $r(x,y)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Illumination–Reflectance Model (Canonical Form):** > $$f(x,y) = i(x,y) \cdot r(x,y)$$ > where $0 < i(x,y) < \infty$ (illumination) and $0 < r(x,y) < 1$ (reflectance). Because the two components occupy **different regions of the frequency spectrum**, a carefully designed filter can compress the low-frequency illumination while amplifying the high-frequency reflectance, yielding an image with **simultaneous brightness equalization and contrast enhancement**. ## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — "The Studio Light Trick" Imagine you are photographing a document in a room with a **bright lamp on one side** (non-uniform illumination). Some parts of the page look dark, others washed out, even though the *ink marks* (true reflectance) are uniform. - **Illumination** = the uneven lamplight (slowly varying, like a soft shadow gradient). - **Reflectance** = the actual ink/text (rapidly varying, the detail you care about). - **Homomorphic filtering** is like a smart studio retoucher: it *removes the uneven lighting gradient* and *boosts the fine ink contrast*, all in one pass. In **medical imaging**, this translates beautifully to: - Enhancing **chest X-rays** where rib shadows obscure nodules. - Improving **MRI** slices where the bias field causes smooth intensity drift. - Boosting **mammograms** where dense tissue creates uneven brightness. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OEEBT833 – Module 2):** Homomorphic filtering is grouped under *Frequency Domain Enhancement* and is assessed regularly as a Part B (14-mark) problem in End Semester Examinations. Students must master the block diagram, the log–DFT–filter–IDFT–exp pipeline, and the Gaussian high-pass filter design. ## 1.3 Why It Is "Homomorphic" The word *homomorphic* comes from abstract algebra: a transformation that **maps one algebraic operation (multiplication) to another simpler one (addition)**, processes it, and maps back. Here: | Original Domain | After Logarithm | After Filtering | After Exponential | |----------------|-----------------|-----------------|-------------------| | Multiplication $f = i \cdot r$ | Addition $\ln f = \ln i + \ln r$ | Linear filtering | Multiplication restored | The log–exp pair acts as a **homomorphism between multiplicative and additive groups**. ## 1.4 Physical/Spectral Constants & Boundaries - **Illumination** component dominates the **low-frequency** band (large wavelength gradients). - **Reflectance** component dominates the **high-frequency** band (edges, textures, micro-detail). - The **cut-off frequency** $D_0$ of the homomorphic filter must lie *between* these two bands — typically chosen in the range of $0.1 \le D_0 \le 0.5$ (in normalized frequency, with image size = 1). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logic The complete homomorphic filter is built from **five stages** executed sequentially: **Step 1 — Logarithmic Transformation** Convert the multiplicative model to an additive one: $$\ln f(x,y) = \ln i(x,y) + \ln r(x,y)$$ This is the algebraic key that unlocks linear filtering of a non-linear signal. **Step 2 — Forward Fourier Transform (DFT)** Project the log-image into the frequency domain. By linearity of the DFT: $$F\{ \ln f(x,y) \} = F\{ \ln i(x,y) \} + F\{ \ln r(x,y) \}$$ Denoting $\mathcal{F}\{\cdot\}$ as the DFT operator: $$F(u,v) = I(u,v) + R(u,v)$$ where $I(u,v)$ is concentrated near the origin (low frequencies) and $R(u,v)$ is spread outward (high frequencies). **Step 3 — Apply High-Pass Filter $H(u,v)$** A homomorphic filter is, by construction, a **high-pass filter** (often with a controlled gain $\gamma_L < 1$ and $\gamma_H > 1$): $$H(u,v) \cdot F(u,v) = H(u,v) \cdot I(u,v) + H(u,v) \cdot R(u,v)$$ **Step 4 — Inverse Fourier Transform (IDFT)** $$g(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{ H(u,v) \cdot F(u,v) \}$$ This returns the filtered log-image to the spatial domain. **Step 5 — Exponential Transformation** Restore the original multiplicative algebra: $$g'(x,y) = e^{g(x,y)}$$ The result is the enhanced image. > [!NOTE] > **Examination Tip:** The five steps (Log → DFT → Filter → IDFT → Exp) constitute the **canonical block diagram** expected in every 14-mark KTU question on homomorphic filtering. Memorize the order, the equation at each node, and the *purpose* of each block. ## 2.2 The Generalized Homomorphic Filter Function The most widely used form (Gonzalez & Woods convention adopted by KTU textbooks) is a **modified Gaussian high-pass filter**: $$H(u,v) = (\gamma_H - \gamma_L) \cdot \left[ 1 - e^{-c \cdot \left(\dfrac{D(u,v)}{D_0}\right)^{2n}} \right] + \gamma_L$$ where the parameters are defined below. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cheat Sheet — Master These Before the Exam** | Symbol / Term | Meaning | Typical Value / Range | Units | |---------------|---------|------------------------|-------| | $f(x,y)$ | Original 2-D image | Continuous / discrete | Intensity (gray-levels) | | $i(x,y)$ | Illumination component | $0 < i < \infty$ | Intensity | | $r(x,y)$ | Reflectance component | $0 \le r \le 1$ | Dimensionless (ratio) | | $D(u,v)$ | Distance from origin in freq. domain: $\sqrt{u^2 + v^2}$ | $\vert u \vert \le M/2$, $\vert v \vert \le N/2$ | Pixels / cycles | | $D_0$ | Cut-off frequency | $0.1 \le D_0 \le 0.5$ | Normalized to image size | | $\gamma_L$ | Low-frequency gain (illumination control) | $0 < \gamma_L < 1$ | Dimensionless | | $\gamma_H$ | High-frequency gain (reflectance boost) | $\gamma_H > 1$ | Dimensionless | | $c$ | Sharpness/roll-off constant of Gaussian | $0.5 \le c \le 2.0$ | Dimensionless | | $n$ | Filter order (Butterworth-like) | $n = 1, 2$ | Dimensionless | | $H(u,v)$ | Homomorphic filter transfer function | $\gamma_L \le H \le \gamma_H$ | Dimensionless | ## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Medical Relevance - **Computed Radiography / X-Ray:** Removes the **Heel Effect** (anode-side intensity drop) in X-ray beams. - **MRI Bias-Field Correction:** Homomorphic filtering approximates the low-frequency multiplicative bias field, separating it from true tissue contrast. - **Dermatoscopy & Retinal Imaging:** Suppresses vignetting artefacts in fundus photographs. - **Pathology Slide Scanning:** Corrects uneven Kohler illumination in whole-slide scanners. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — From Multiplicative Model to Linear Filter We begin with the image formation model: $$f(x,y) = i(x,y) \cdot r(x,y)$$ **Step A — Apply Natural Logarithm** $$\ln\big[f(x,y)\big] = \ln\big[i(x,y) \cdot r(x,y)\big]$$ Using the logarithm product rule $\ln(ab) = \ln a + \ln b$: $$\ln\big[f(x,y)\big] = \ln\big[i(x,y)\big] + \ln\big[r(x,y)\big]$$ Let us denote $\tilde{f} = \ln f$, $\tilde{i} = \ln i$, $\tilde{r} = \ln r$: $$\tilde{f}(x,y) = \tilde{i}(x,y) + \tilde{r}(x,y)$$ **Step B — Forward 2-D Discrete Fourier Transform** The 2-D DFT is defined as: $$F(u,v) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x,y) \, e^{-j2\pi\!\left(\dfrac{u x}{M} + \dfrac{v y}{N}\right)}$$ By linearity, the DFT of a sum equals the sum of DFTs: $$\mathcal{F}\{\tilde{f}\} = \mathcal{F}\{\tilde{i}\} + \mathcal{F}\{\tilde{r}\}$$ $$F(u,v) = I(u,v) + R(u,v)$$ **Step C — Multiply by High-Pass Filter $H(u,v)$** $$S(u,v) = H(u,v) \cdot F(u,v)$$ $$S(u,v) = H(u,v) \cdot I(u,v) + H(u,v) \cdot R(u,v)$$ **Step D — Inverse 2-D DFT** $$s(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{S(u,v)\} = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{H \cdot I\} + \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{H \cdot R\}$$ $$s(x,y) = h(x,y) * \tilde{i}(x,y) + h(x,y) * \tilde{r}(x,y)$$ **Step E — Exponential to Restore Multiplicative Algebra** $$g(x,y) = e^{s(x,y)}$$ $$g(x,y) = e^{h * \tilde{i}} \cdot e^{h * \tilde{r}} = i'(x,y) \cdot r'(x,y)$$ where the prime $(')$ denotes the *filtered* components. The output is a new image whose illumination has been compressed and whose reflectance has been amplified. ## 3.2 Derivation — The Gaussian Homomorphic Transfer Function Start from the standard Gaussian high-pass filter: $$H_{\text{GHP}}(u,v) = 1 - e^{-c \cdot \left(\dfrac{D^2(u,v)}{D_0^{\,2}}\right)}$$ We *bias* this function between $\gamma_L$ and $\gamma_H$ so that low frequencies can be **attenuated** (not fully removed) and high frequencies can be **amplified** (not just preserved). Linear remap of the interval $[0, 1]$ to $[\gamma_L, \gamma_H]$: $$H(u,v) = \gamma_L + (\gamma_H - \gamma_L) \cdot H_{\text{GHP}}(u,v)$$ Substituting the Gaussian kernel: $$H(u,v) = \gamma_L + (\gamma_H - \gamma_L) \cdot \left[ 1 - e^{-c \cdot \left(\dfrac{D^2(u,v)}{D_0^{\,2}}\right)} \right]$$ Rearranging by distributing $(\gamma_H - \gamma_L)$: $$H(u,v) = \gamma_L + (\gamma_H - \gamma_L) - (\gamma_H - \gamma_L) \cdot e^{-c \cdot \left(\dfrac{D^2(u,v)}{D_0^{\,2}}\right)}$$ $$H(u,v) = \gamma_H - (\gamma_H - \gamma_L) \cdot e^{-c \cdot \left(\dfrac{D^2(u,v)}{D_0^{\,2}}\right)}$$ Final, KTU-board-preferred form: $$\boxed{H(u,v) = (\gamma_H - \gamma_L) \left[ 1 - e^{-c \left(\dfrac{D(u,v)}{D_0}\right)^{2n}} \right] + \gamma_L}$$ **Verification of boundary values:** - At $D(u,v) = 0$ (DC / origin): exponential term $= 1$, so $H = (\gamma_H - \gamma_L)(0) + \gamma_L = \gamma_L$. ✓ - At $D(u,v) \to \infty$: exponential term $\to 0$, so $H \to (\gamma_H - \gamma_L)(1) + \gamma_L = \gamma_H$. ✓ - Thus the filter transitions monotonically from $\gamma_L$ (low freq) to $\gamma_H$ (high freq). ✓ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameter Tuning | Parameter | Increase Effect | Decrease Effect | KTU Typical Choice | |-----------|-----------------|-----------------|--------------------| | $\gamma_L$ | Brighter background, less contrast in dark regions | Darker, more uniform shadows | $0.3$ to $0.5$ | | $\gamma_H$ | Sharper edges, noisier appearance | Smoother, softer edges | $1.5$ to $2.5$ | | $D_0$ | More low-freq content passes, smoother result | More high-freq content passes, more detail/noise | $0.15$ to $0.4$ | | $c$ | Sharper roll-off (more selective) | Gentler roll-off (smoother) | $1.0$ to $2.0$ | | $n$ | Steeper transition (Butterworth effect) | Smoother transition | $n = 1$ or $2$ | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of Homomorphic Filtering Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Input Image
f(x,y)"] --> B["Logarithm
ln f(x,y)"] B --> C["Forward DFT
F(u,v)"] C --> D["High-Pass Filter
H(u,v) * F(u,v)"] D --> E["Inverse DFT
s(x,y)"] E --> F["Exponential
exp s(x,y)"] F --> G["Enhanced Output
g(x,y)"] subgraph LP["Low-Frequency Suppression"] D end subgraph RP["Restoration Stage"] E F end ``` ## 4.2 Frequency-Domain Decomposition Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph IMG["Spatial Image f(x,y)"] I1["Illumination i(x,y)"] I2["Reflectance r(x,y)"] end IMG --> M["Multiplication
f = i * r"] M --> LOG["Logarithm
ln f = ln i + ln r"] LOG --> FT["2-D DFT
F = I + R"] FT --> LF["Low-Freq Region
Center of Spectrum
ILow = I near origin"] FT --> HF["High-Freq Region
Outer Spectrum
RHigh = R outer bands"] LF --> ATT["Attenuate by gamma_L
Multiply by gamma_L"] HF --> AMP["Amplify by gamma_H
Multiply by gamma_H"] ATT --> SUM["Combine Filt F"] AMP --> SUM SUM --> IFT["Inverse DFT"] IFT --> EXP["Exponential"] EXP --> OUT["Enhanced Image g(x,y)"] ``` ## 4.3 Filter Response Curve — $H(u,v)$ vs. $D(u,v)$ ```mermaid flowchart LR A["D = 0
H = gamma_L
e.g. 0.4"] --> B["D = D0
H near midpoint
~ 0.5 * gamma_L + gamma_H"] B --> C["D large
H approaches gamma_H
e.g. 2.0"] C --> D["High-frequency detail boosted
Low-frequency illumination suppressed"] ``` ## 4.4 Component Separation Map ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ORIG["ORIGINAL IMAGE f(x,y)"] A1["i(x,y): Illumination
Slowly varying
LOW FREQUENCY"] A2["r(x,y): Reflectance
Rapidly varying
HIGH FREQUENCY"] end ORIG --> DECOMP["Homomorphic Filter
Separates i and r"] DECOMP --> OUT1["i_prime = e to the h * ln i
Compressed illumination"] DECOMP --> OUT2["r_prime = e to the h * ln r
Enhanced reflectance"] ``` ## 4.5 Medical Imaging Application Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Acquired X-ray
Uneven illumination"] --> B["Log Transform"] B --> C["DFT to Frequency Domain"] C --> D["Apply Homomorphic
Gaussian HPF"] D --> E["IDFT to Spatial Domain"] E --> F["Exponential Transform"] F --> G["Enhanced X-ray
Uniform brightness
Sharper lesions"] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State the Illumination–Reflectance model of an image and explain how homomorphic filtering is used to separate illumination and reflectance components.** **Model Answer:** The illumination–reflectance model expresses an image as a product of two components: $$f(x,y) = i(x,y) \cdot r(x,y)$$ where $i(x,y)$ is the illumination (slowly varying, low frequency) and $r(x,y)$ is the reflectance (rapidly varying, high frequency). Homomorphic filtering separates them by: 1. Taking the **logarithm** to convert multiplication to addition: $\ln f = \ln i + \ln r$. 2. Applying the **DFT**: $F(u,v) = I(u,v) + R(u,v)$. 3. Multiplying by a **high-pass filter** $H(u,v)$ that attenuates $I$ and boosts $R$. 4. Taking the **inverse DFT** and the **exponential** to recover an enhanced image. ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Why is a high-pass filter used in homomorphic filtering instead of a low-pass filter?** **Model Answer:** A high-pass filter is used because: - The **illumination component** occupies the **low-frequency** band and is generally unwanted (it causes non-uniform brightness). - The **reflectance component** (true image details such as edges, textures, lesions) occupies the **high-frequency** band and is the desired content. - A high-pass filter *attenuates* low-frequency illumination and *preserves/amplifies* high-frequency reflectance, achieving simultaneous brightness normalization and contrast enhancement. A low-pass filter would have the opposite, undesirable effect. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the concept of **homomorphic filtering** in image enhancement. Discuss the role of the logarithm and exponential transformations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for the homomorphic filter transfer function $H(u,v)$ using a Gaussian high-pass filter with parameters $\gamma_L$, $\gamma_H$, $c$, and $D_0$. Show that $H(0,0) = \gamma_L$ and $H(u,v) \to \gamma_H$ as $D(u,v) \to \infty$. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution (a):** The block diagram is: **Input $f(x,y)$ → ln → DFT → High-Pass Filter $H(u,v)$ → IDFT → exp → Output $g(x,y)$** **Role of Logarithm:** The image is modeled as $f(x,y) = i(x,y) \cdot r(x,y)$. Multiplication is **non-linear** and cannot be separated by linear filters. The natural logarithm converts the product into a sum: $$\ln f(x,y) = \ln i(x,y) + \ln r(x,y)$$ This allows linear frequency-domain filtering of each component independently. **Role of Exponential:** After filtering, the inverse log (exponential) is applied: $$g(x,y) = \exp\big[ \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{ H(u,v) \cdot F(u,v) \} \big]$$ This restores the multiplicative algebra of the original image and produces an enhanced output $g(x,y) = i'(x,y) \cdot r'(x,y)$, where the illumination has been compressed and reflectance has been enhanced. #### **Model Solution (b):** Starting with the **standard Gaussian high-pass filter**: $$H_{\text{GHP}}(u,v) = 1 - e^{-c \cdot \left(\dfrac{D^2(u,v)}{D_0^{\,2}}\right)}$$ **Linear remap** from $[0, 1]$ to $[\gamma_L, \gamma_H]$: $$H(u,v) = \gamma_L + (\gamma_H - \gamma_L) \cdot \left[ 1 - e^{-c \left(\dfrac{D^2(u,v)}{D_0^{\,2}}\right)} \right]$$ **Verification at $D(u,v) = 0$:** $$H(0,0) = \gamma_L + (\gamma_H - \gamma_L) \cdot [1 - e^{0}] = \gamma_L + 0 = \gamma_L \quad \checkmark$$ **Verification as $D(u,v) \to \infty$:** $$H \to \gamma_L + (\gamma_H - \gamma_L) \cdot [1 - 0] = \gamma_L + \gamma_H - \gamma_L = \gamma_H \quad \checkmark$$ Thus $H(u,v)$ varies monotonically from $\gamma_L$ at the origin to $\gamma_H$ at infinity, providing the desired low-frequency suppression and high-frequency boost. --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Modified]` **(a)** Explain the **illumination–reflectance model** of an image. Why is the illumination component concentrated in the low-frequency region and the reflectance component in the high-frequency region? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A medical imaging system acquires a 2-D digital X-ray image. Design a homomorphic filter for this image with $\gamma_L = 0.4$, $\gamma_H = 1.8$, $D_0 = 30$ pixels, and $c = 1$. Compute the value of $H(u,v)$ at the points where $D(u,v) = 0$, $D(u,v) = D_0 = 30$, and $D(u,v) = 60$. Take $n = 1$. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution (a):** The illumination–reflectance model is: $$f(x,y) = i(x,y) \cdot r(x,y)$$ - **$i(x,y)$ — Illumination:** The amount of source light incident on the scene. It varies **slowly** across the image because light sources produce smooth gradients (e.g., a lamp in a corner creates a smooth fall-off). Therefore its energy is concentrated at **low spatial frequencies**, near the origin of the frequency spectrum. - **$r(x,y)$ — Reflectance:** The fraction of light reflected by the object. It varies **rapidly** at object boundaries, edges, and texture changes. Its energy is concentrated at **high spatial frequencies**, in the outer regions of the spectrum. This spectral separation is the **physical justification** for using a high-pass filter in homomorphic filtering — to suppress $i$ and enhance $r$. #### **Model Solution (b):** The homomorphic filter equation is: $$H(u,v) = (\gamma_H - \gamma_L) \left[ 1 - e^{-c \left(\dfrac{D(u,v)}{D_0}\right)^{2n}} \right] + \gamma_L$$ Given: $\gamma_L = 0.4$, $\gamma_H = 1.8$, $D_0 = 30$, $c = 1$, $n = 1$. So $\gamma_H - \gamma_L = 1.8 - 0.4 = 1.4$. **Case 1: $D(u,v) = 0$** $$H = 1.4 \cdot [1 - e^{0}] + 0.4 = 1.4 \cdot 0 + 0.4 = \mathbf{0.4}$$ **Case 2: $D(u,v) = D_0 = 30$** $$\frac{D}{D_0} = 1, \quad e^{-1 \cdot 1^{2}} = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$$ $$H = 1.4 \cdot [1 - 0.3679] + 0.4 = 1.4 \cdot 0.6321 + 0.4 \approx 0.885 + 0.4 = \mathbf{1.285}$$ **Case 3: $D(u,v) = 60$** $$\frac{D}{D_0} = 2, \quad e^{-1 \cdot 4} = e^{-4} \approx 0.0183$$ $$H = 1.4 \cdot [1 - 0.0183] + 0.4 = 1.4 \cdot 0.9817 + 0.4 \approx 1.374 + 0.4 = \mathbf{1.774}$$ **Summary:** | $D(u,v)$ | $H(u,v)$ | Interpretation | |-----------|-----------|----------------| | $0$ | $0.400$ | Illumination suppressed | | $30$ | $1.285$ | Mid-frequency boosted | | $60$ | $1.774$ | High-frequency strongly amplified | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the additive $\gamma_L$** in the final expression — many students write $H = (\gamma_H - \gamma_L)[1 - e^{(\cdot)}]$ and lose 2 marks. > 2. **Using $D^2/D_0$** instead of $(D/D_0)^2$ in the exponent — both forms are algebraically equivalent for $n=1$, but the $(D/D_0)^{2n}$ form is the KTU textbook convention. > 3. **Skipping the block diagram** in 7-mark questions — KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks for the diagram alone. > 4. **Confusing the order of exp and IDFT** — the exponential is the *last* step, applied after the IDFT to restore multiplicative algebra. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] The **Illumination–Reflectance Model** is: $f(x,y) = i(x,y) \cdot r(x,y)$. - [x] **Illumination** is low-frequency (smooth gradients); **reflectance** is high-frequency (edges, texture). - [x] The **5-step pipeline** is mandatory: **Log → DFT → Filter → IDFT → Exp**. - [x] Logarithm converts **multiplication to addition**; exponential restores it. - [x] The filter must be a **high-pass filter** with low-frequency gain $\gamma_L < 1$ and high-frequency gain $\gamma_H > 1$. - [x] Canonical filter: $H(u,v) = (\gamma_H - \gamma_L)[1 - e^{-c(D/D_0)^{2n}}] + \gamma_L$. - [x] **Boundary values:** $H(0,0) = \gamma_L$; $H \to \gamma_H$ as $D \to \infty$. - [x] **Medical applications:** X-ray brightness correction, MRI bias-field removal, mammogram enhancement, retinal image de-vignetting. - [x] **Key parameters to memorize:** $\gamma_L \in [0.3, 0.5]$, $\gamma_H \in [1.5, 2.5]$, $D_0 \in [0.15, 0.4]$ (normalized), $c \in [1, 2]$, $n = 1$ or $2$. - [x] **Don't confuse** homomorphic filtering with simple histogram equalization — homomorphic operates in the *frequency* domain and targets *multiplicative* degradations. - [x] For **14-mark answers**, always include: block diagram, all 5 equations, derivation, parameter table, and one numerical example.

topics.title: Image Degradation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT833 - MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Image Degradation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🩻 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Image Degradation?

**Image Degradation** is the process by which an original (ideal) image loses quality, clarity, or information due to imperfections in the imaging system, environmental conditions, or random noise. In medical imaging, this manifests as blur, low contrast, grainy textures, or loss of fine anatomical detail.

The formal mathematical model used universally in KTU syllabus is:

$$g(x,y) = H[f(x,y)] + \eta(x,y)$$

Where:
- $f(x,y)$ = **Original (undegraded) input image**
- $g(x,y)$ = **Observed (degraded) output image**
- $H$ = **Degradation operator** (a linear, shift-invariant system describing the blurring/PSF effect)
- $\eta(x,y)$ = **Additive noise term** (stochastic component)
- $(x,y)$ = **Spatial coordinates** in the image plane

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The fundamental goal of *image restoration* (covered later) is to recover an estimate $\hat{f}(x,y)$ of the original image, given $g(x,y)$ and some knowledge of $H$ and $\eta$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition 🏥

> **Real-World Analogy — "The Smudged Eyeglass Lens"**
>
> Imagine you are a radiologist looking at a chest X-ray. The X-ray is your **original image** $f(x,y)$. But the hospital's old scanner has a slightly smudged lens and the room has a flickering fluorescent light:
>
> - The **smudged lens** causes *blur* (system imperfections) → this is the degradation operator $H$.
> - The **flickering light** causes random grainy speckles → this is the **noise** $\eta(x,y)$.
> - What you actually see on the screen is $g(x,y)$ — the smudged, grainy version.
>
> Image degradation is the mathematical description of "smudges + speckles." To restore the X-ray, you need to mathematically reverse both the smudge (deconvolution) and reduce the speckles (denoising).

---

## 1.3 Categories of Degradation

| Category | Cause | Medical Example |
|----------|-------|-----------------|
| **Blur (Deterministic)** | Motion, defocus, scattering | Patient movement during CT scan |
| **Atmospheric Turbulence** | Random refractive index variations | Long-range ultrasound |
| **Sensor Noise** | Electronic, thermal, photon statistics | Low-dose X-ray quantum noise |
| **Quantization Noise** | Limited bit-depth of ADC | MRI with 8-bit depth |
| **Geometric Distortion** | Lens aberration, sensor tilt | Wide-field fundus camera |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Degradation Function (H):** A mathematical operator that describes how the imaging system distorts the true signal. When $H$ is linear and position-invariant, it can be fully characterized by its **Point Spread Function (PSF)** in the spatial domain, or equivalently by its transfer function $H(u,v)$ in the frequency domain.

---

## 1.4 The Two Pillars of the Degradation Model

### Pillar 1 — Spatial Domain Convolution Model

When the system is **Linear and Shift-Invariant (LSI)**, the noise-free degraded image is the convolution of the original image with the system's **Point Spread Function** $h(x,y)$:

$$g(x,y) = f(x,y) * h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

Here, $h(x,y)$ is the impulse response (PSF) of the degradation system. The asterisk $*$ denotes 2D convolution.

### Pillar 2 — Frequency Domain Multiplication Model

Applying the **2D Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** to the convolution model, convolution becomes multiplication:

$$G(u,v) = F(u,v) \cdot H(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

Where uppercase letters denote the DFT of their spatial counterparts. This is the foundation of frequency-domain restoration filters (Wiener, inverse, etc.).

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters clinically:** In CT/MRI reconstruction, the degradation function $H(u,v)$ is known as the **Modulation Transfer Function (MTF)**. Manufacturers specify scanner MTF to describe sharpness. Image restoration filters aim to "undo" the MTF.

---

## 1.5 Additive Noise — The Universal Noise Model

The most common assumption in KTU syllabus is that noise is **additive** and **statistically independent** of the image signal:

$$g(x,y) = f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

In the frequency domain:

$$G(u,v) = F(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

Noise is characterized by its **Probability Density Function (PDF)** and statistical parameters (mean $\mu$, variance $\sigma^2$, power spectrum $S_\eta$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Degradation Function — Detailed Taxonomy

The degradation function $h(x,y)$ (PSF) can arise from multiple physical phenomena. KTU syllabus emphasizes the following canonical models:

### 2.1.1 Motion Blur (Uniform Linear Motion)

When there is relative motion between the object and the camera/sensor during exposure, the PSF for uniform linear motion over $T$ seconds in the $x$-direction is:

$$h(x,y) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{T} & \text{if } 0 \le x \le T \text{ and } y = 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

In the frequency domain:

$$H(u,v) = \frac{\sin(\pi u T)}{\pi u T} \cdot e^{-j\pi u T}$$

This is a **sinc function**, which is why motion-blurred images have characteristic ringing patterns when naively restored.

### 2.1.2 Atmospheric Turbulence Blur

Caused by long-exposure imaging through turbulent air. The Gaussian PSF model is:

$$H(u,v) = e^{-k \left( u^2 + v^2 \right)^{5/6}}$$

Where $k$ is a turbulence constant depending on atmospheric conditions. **k ≈ 0.0025** is a typical reference value used in KTU textbook examples.

### 2.1.3 Out-of-Focus Blur (Uniform Disk PSF)

$$h(x,y) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{\pi R^2} & \text{if } x^2 + y^2 \le R^2 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

$$H(u,v) = \frac{J_1(2\pi R \rho)}{\pi R \rho}, \quad \rho = \sqrt{u^2 + v^2}$$

Where $J_1$ is the Bessel function of the first kind, order 1, and $R$ is the radius of the defocus circle.

---

## 2.2 Noise Models — The Six Canonical PDFs (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Noise Model | PDF $p(z)$ | Mean $\mu$ | Variance $\sigma^2$ | Physical Origin |
|---|-------------|-----------|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Gaussian** | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma} e^{-(z-\mu)^2 / 2\sigma^2}$ | $\mu$ | $\sigma^2$ | Electronic sensor noise, thermal noise |
| 2 | **Rayleigh** | $\dfrac{2}{b}(z-a)e^{-(z-a)^2/b}$ for $z \ge a$ | $a + \sqrt{\pi b/4}$ | $\dfrac{b(4-\pi)}{4}$ | Range imaging, ultrasound speckle tails |
| 3 | **Erlang (Gamma)** | $\dfrac{a^b z^{b-1}}{(b-1)!} e^{-az}$ for $z \ge 0$ | $b/a$ | $b/a^2$ | X-ray photon counting, MRI noise |
| 4 | **Exponential** | $a e^{-az}$ for $z \ge 0$ | $1/a$ | $1/a^2$ | Laser imaging, specialized cases |
| 5 | **Uniform** | $\dfrac{1}{b-a}$ for $a \le z \le b$ | $(a+b)/2$ | $(b-a)^2/12$ | Quantization noise, dithering |
| 6 | **Impulse (Salt & Pepper)** | $\begin{cases} P_a & z=a \\ P_b & z=b \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | — | — | Defective pixels, transmission errors |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always write the PDF support range (e.g., $z \ge 0$) when asked to define a noise model. Many students lose marks by omitting the domain.

### Special Cases of Erlang/Gamma Noise
- **Exponential** noise = Erlang with $b = 1$.
- **Gamma** noise = Generalized Erlang.
- **Chi-square** = Special case of gamma (used in statistical image analysis).

---

## 2.3 Periodic Noise — The Spatial-Domain Sinusoid Model

Periodic noise appears as regular patterns (stripes, rings) often caused by electrical interference in the imaging chain. It is modeled as a 2D sinusoid:

$$\eta(x,y) = A \sin\left[ 2\pi u_0 x + 2\pi v_0 y + \phi \right]$$

In the frequency domain, this produces **two Dirac delta spikes** at $(u_0, v_0)$ and $(-u_0, -v_0)$:

$$N(u,v) = \frac{A}{2j}\left[ \delta(u-u_0, v-v_0) e^{j\phi} - \delta(u+u_0, v+v_0) e^{-j\phi} \right]$$

This is why periodic noise is so easily removed by **notch filtering** in the frequency domain.

---

## 2.4 Estimating the Degradation Function — Three Approaches

| # | Method | Description | When Used |
|---|--------|-------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Observation** | Visually inspect $g(x,y)$ and hypothesize $h(x,y)$ (e.g., 2D Gaussian blur radius) | Simple blur, no ground truth |
| 2 | **Experimentation** | Image a known point source/impulse (e.g., a pinhole) to obtain $h(x,y)$ directly | Lab calibration, controlled settings |
| 3 | **Mathematical Modeling** | Use physical/analytical models (motion, turbulence, defocus) with parameters | Theoretical analysis, simulation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Medical Imaging specifically**, the degradation function is often estimated from **phantom studies** (imaging a calibration object of known geometry) — this is an "Experimentation" approach using the **line spread function (LSF)** or **edge spread function (ESF)** to derive the PSF.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Parameter |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | General Degradation | $g(x,y) = H[f(x,y)] + \eta(x,y)$ | Linear operator $H$ |
| 2 | Convolution Model | $g(x,y) = f(x,y) * h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$ | PSF $h(x,y)$ |
| 3 | Frequency Model | $G(u,v) = F(u,v)H(u,v) + N(u,v)$ | Transfer function $H(u,v)$ |
| 4 | Motion Blur PSF | $h(x,y) = 1/T$ for $0 \le x \le T, y=0$ | Exposure $T$ |
| 5 | Motion $H(u,v)$ | $\dfrac{\sin(\pi u T)}{\pi u T} e^{-j\pi u T}$ | sinc function |
| 6 | Turbulence $H(u,v)$ | $e^{-k(u^2+v^2)^{5/6}}$ | $k \approx 0.0025$ |
| 7 | Gaussian Noise PDF | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma}e^{-(z-\mu)^2/2\sigma^2}$ | $\mu, \sigma$ |
| 8 | Rayleigh Noise PDF | $\dfrac{2}{b}(z-a)e^{-(z-a)^2/b}$ | $a, b$ |
| 9 | Erlang Noise PDF | $\dfrac{a^b z^{b-1}}{(b-1)!}e^{-az}$ | $a, b$ integer |
| 10 | Exponential Noise PDF | $a e^{-az}$ | $a > 0$ |
| 11 | Uniform Noise PDF | $\dfrac{1}{b-a}$ | $a, b$ bounds |
| 12 | Impulse Noise PDF | $P_a, P_b$ at $a, b$ | Salt ($b$) and Pepper ($a$) |
| 13 | Periodic Noise | $A\sin[2\pi u_0 x + 2\pi v_0 y + \phi]$ | $A, u_0, v_0, \phi$ |
| 14 | SNR (dB) | $10\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\sum f^2}{\sum \eta^2}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio |
| 15 | Noise Power Spectrum | $S_{\eta\eta}(u,v) = \vert N(u,v)\vert^2$ | Frequency noise power |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **SNR** is the gold-standard metric in radiology for image quality. A chest X-ray typically requires **SNR > 40 dB**; a mammogram needs **SNR > 55 dB** for microcalcification detection. Restoration filters are designed to maximize output SNR.

---

## 2.6 The Critical Assumption — Linearity and Shift-Invariance

Almost all KTU problems assume $H$ is a **Linear Spatially Invariant (LSI)** operator. This assumption:

1. **Linearity:** $H[a f_1 + b f_2] = a H[f_1] + b H[f_2]$
2. **Shift-Invariance:** $H[f(x-a, y-b)] = g(x-a, y-b)$

These two properties allow the system to be characterized entirely by a single PSF $h(x,y)$ and to use convolution/Fourier methods. When violated (e.g., in nonlinear MRI distortion), special methods are needed.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Spatial Degradation to Frequency Domain

We start with the spatial LSI model and derive its frequency-domain equivalent step-by-step.

### Step 1 — Write the Spatial Convolution Model
By the LSI assumption, the noise-free degraded image is a 2D convolution:

$$g_0(x,y) = f(x,y) * h(x,y)$$

where $g_0$ denotes the noise-free degraded image and $h$ is the PSF.

### Step 2 — Apply the 2D Fourier Transform
Take the Fourier transform of both sides. We use the **convolution theorem** which states that convolution in space becomes multiplication in frequency:

$$G_0(u,v) = F(u,v) \cdot H(u,v)$$

**Conversion Logic:** The Fourier transform of a convolution is the product of the individual Fourier transforms. This is one of the most powerful properties in signal processing.

### Step 3 — Add the Additive Noise Component
The complete model in space is:

$$g(x,y) = f(x,y) * h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

Since noise is additive, we apply Fourier transform to the sum:

$$G(u,v) = F(u,v) \cdot H(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

**Conversion Logic:** Fourier transform is linear, so $\mathcal{F}[a+b] = \mathcal{F}[a] + \mathcal{F}[b]$. The noise $N(u,v)$ is simply the Fourier transform of $\eta(x,y)$.

### Step 4 — Solve for the Original Spectrum (Theoretical)
If we could ignore noise and invert exactly:

$$F(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

This is the **Inverse Filter** concept, and it fails when $H(u,v) \approx 0$ (division by very small numbers amplifies noise catastrophically).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Motion Blur PSF → Transfer Function

We derive $H(u,v)$ for uniform linear motion.

### Step 1 — Define the Spatial PSF
A point moving uniformly in the $x$-direction during exposure time $T$ smears its energy uniformly along a line segment of length $T$:

$$h(x,y) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{T} & 0 \le x \le T, \, y = 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The factor $\frac{1}{T}$ normalizes the total energy to unity (energy conservation).

### Step 2 — Express as a Rectangular Function
The PSF can be written compactly as:

$$h(x,y) = \frac{1}{T} \cdot \text{rect}\left(\frac{x - T/2}{T}\right) \delta(y)$$

where $\text{rect}(u/T)$ is the rectangle function of width $T$ centered at origin, and $\delta(y)$ restricts it to the $x$-axis.

### Step 3 — Take the 2D Fourier Transform
The transform of $\text{rect}(x/T)$ is $T \cdot \text{sinc}(uT)$ (using frequency $u$ in cycles/sample), and $\delta(y)$ contributes a constant in the $v$-direction:

$$H(u,v) = \frac{1}{T} \cdot T \cdot \text{sinc}(uT) \cdot 1 = \text{sinc}(uT)$$

### Step 4 — Add the Linear Phase Term
Because the rectangle is centered at $x = T/2$ (not at the origin), the **shift property** introduces a linear phase:

$$H(u,v) = \text{sinc}(uT) \cdot e^{-j\pi u T}$$

Expanded explicitly:

$$H(u,v) = \frac{\sin(\pi u T)}{\pi u T} \cdot e^{-j\pi u T}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The exponential $e^{-j\pi u T}$ corresponds to a delay of $T/2$ pixels in the spatial domain. This phase factor is critical for accurate restoration.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Rayleigh Noise — Mean and Variance

The Rayleigh PDF is:

$$p(z) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{2}{b}(z-a)e^{-(z-a)^2/b} & z \ge a \\ 0 & z < a \end{cases}$$

### Step 1 — Mean Calculation
$$\mu = \int_{a}^{\infty} z \cdot \frac{2}{b}(z-a) e^{-(z-a)^2/b} \, dz$$

Substitute $w = (z-a)^2/b$, so $dw = \frac{2(z-a)}{b}dz$:

$$\mu = a + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-w} \sqrt{bw} \, dw = a + \sqrt{b} \cdot \Gamma(3/2)$$

Since $\Gamma(3/2) = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$:

$$\mu = a + \sqrt{\pi b / 4}$$

### Step 2 — Variance Calculation
$$\sigma^2 = \int_{a}^{\infty} (z - \mu)^2 \cdot \frac{2}{b}(z-a) e^{-(z-a)^2/b} \, dz$$

After integration (using $\Gamma$ identities):

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{b(4 - \pi)}{4}$$

This is a frequently tested KTU result.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)

For an $M \times N$ image, the **average signal power** and **average noise power** are:

$$P_f = \frac{1}{MN} \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} [f(x,y)]^2$$

$$P_\eta = \frac{1}{MN} \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} [\eta(x,y)]^2$$

The SNR in decibels:

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{P_f}{P_\eta} \right) = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{\sum \sum f^2}{\sum \sum \eta^2} \right)$$

**Conversion Logic:** Decibels compress a wide dynamic range into a manageable number. A 20 dB increase = 100× power ratio.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Gaussian Noise PDF Evaluation

**Problem:** A medical sensor has Gaussian noise with $\mu = 0, \sigma = 10$ (grayscale units). Find the probability of a noise sample with intensity in $[-15, +15]$.

**Step 1 — Compute the standardized variable:**
For $z_1 = -15, z_2 = +15$:

$$z_{\text{low}} = \frac{-15 - 0}{10} = -1.5$$

$$z_{\text{high}} = \frac{+15 - 0}{10} = +1.5$$

**Step 2 — Use the standard normal table:**
From tables, $P(Z \le 1.5) \approx 0.9332$, so $P(-1.5 \le Z \le 1.5) = 2(0.9332) - 1 = 0.8664$.

**Step 3 — Interpret:** Approximately **86.64%** of noise samples lie within $\pm 1.5\sigma$ — useful for setting denoising thresholds in CT/MRI.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Noise Model

| Imaging Modality | Dominant Noise | Suggested Model |
|------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| **X-ray (low dose)** | Quantum (Poisson) ≈ Gaussian | Gaussian |
| **MRI** | Rician (Gaussian in real & imag) | Rayleigh-like |
| **Ultrasound** | Speckle (multiplicative) | Rayleigh / Exponential |
| **PET** | Poisson counting | Approximated as Gaussian at high counts |
| **Digital Radiography** | Electronic readout | Gaussian + Impulse |
| **Fundus Photography** | Sensor + quantization | Gaussian + Uniform |

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the general model of image degradation and explain the role of the degradation function $H$ and the noise term $\eta(x,y)$.**

**Model Answer:**

The general model of image degradation is expressed as:

$$g(x,y) = H[f(x,y)] + \eta(x,y)$$

- $f(x,y)$ is the **original undegraded image**.
- $H[\cdot]$ is the **degradation operator**, typically representing a linear shift-invariant system characterized by its Point Spread Function (PSF) $h(x,y)$.
- $\eta(x,y)$ is the **additive noise**, statistically independent of the signal, modeled by a random process (e.g., Gaussian, Rayleigh).
- $g(x,y)$ is the **observed degraded image** at the output.

For an LSI system, this becomes a convolution:

$$g(x,y) = f(x,y) * h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

The goal of restoration is to estimate $\hat{f}(x,y)$ from $g(x,y)$ using known or estimated $H$ and noise statistics.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any four important noise probability density functions used in image processing. Give the PDF of Gaussian noise.**

**Model Answer:**

Four important noise PDFs in KTU syllabus are:

1. **Gaussian (Normal)** — for electronic/sensor noise
2. **Rayleigh** — for range imaging and ultrasound
3. **Erlang (Gamma)** — for X-ray photon counting
4. **Impulse (Salt-and-Pepper)** — for defective pixels

**Gaussian Noise PDF:**

$$p(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma} e^{-(z-\mu)^2 / (2\sigma^2)}$$

Where $\mu$ is the mean (often 0) and $\sigma$ is the standard deviation. Approximately **70%** of samples lie within $\pm\sigma$ and **95%** within $\pm 2\sigma$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the process of image degradation. Derive the relationship $g(x,y) = f(x,y) \ast h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$ and state its frequency-domain equivalent. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** For **uniform linear motion blur** with exposure $T = 0.4$ s, derive the transfer function $H(u,v)$ and explain why simple inverse filtering fails. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q. (a):**

**Step 1 — Block Diagram Description:**
The degradation process has three components: original image, degradation system, additive noise, and observed output (refer to SECTION 4.1 diagram).

**Step 2 — Define the Degradation Model:**
The general equation in continuous space:

$$g(x,y) = H[f(x,y)] + \eta(x,y)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Linearity and Shift-Invariance:**
For an LSI system, the response to any input can be computed using the system's impulse response (PSF) $h(x,y)$. If we denote convolution by $*$, the noise-free output is:

$$g_0(x,y) = f(x,y) * h(x,y)$$

This is the **2D convolution integral**:

$$g_0(x,y) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(\alpha,\beta) \, h(x-\alpha, y-\beta) \, d\alpha \, d\beta$$

**Step 4 — Add Noise:**
Since the noise is additive and independent:

$$g(x,y) = f(x,y) * h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

**Step 5 — Frequency Domain Equivalent:**
Apply the 2D Fourier Transform $\mathcal{F}$ and use the convolution theorem:

$$G(u,v) = F(u,v) \cdot H(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

This form is far more useful for designing restoration filters because multiplication is invertible (up to numerical issues).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q. (b):**

**Step 1 — Define Motion Blur PSF:**
For uniform motion of a point source along the $x$-axis during time $T$:

$$h(x,y) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{T} & 0 \le x \le T, \, y = 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Transfer Function:**
Using the Fourier transform of a shifted rectangle function (derivation in Section 3.2):

$$H(u,v) = \frac{\sin(\pi u T)}{\pi u T} \cdot e^{-j\pi u T}$$

Substituting $T = 0.4$ s:

$$H(u,v) = \frac{\sin(0.4 \pi u)}{0.4 \pi u} \cdot e^{-j 0.4 \pi u}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Zeros of $H(u,v)$:**
$H(u,v) = 0$ when $\sin(\pi u T) = 0$, i.e., when $u = n/T = n/0.4$ for integer $n \neq 0$. At these frequencies, $H = 0$ exactly.

**Step 4 — Explain Failure of Inverse Filtering:**
Inverse filtering computes:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)} = F(u,v) + \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

Near the zeros of $H(u,v)$, the term $1/H(u,v)$ becomes **infinitely large**, which means the noise component $N(u,v)/H(u,v)$ explodes and completely dominates. Even small noise levels produce unacceptable output, making inverse filtering unusable for noisy medical images without regularization.

---

### **QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Q. (a)** Derive the probability density function of **Rayleigh noise** and obtain expressions for its mean and variance. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** An 8-bit medical image of size $256 \times 256$ is corrupted by additive Gaussian noise with $\mu = 0$ and $\sigma = 12$. Compute the SNR in dB if the original image has pixel value sum of squares = $1.2 \times 10^8$ and noise sum of squares = $8.5 \times 10^5$. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q. (a):**

**Step 1 — Define Rayleigh PDF:**
The Rayleigh distribution arises when two orthogonal Gaussian components (real and imaginary parts of a complex signal) are combined. Its PDF is:

$$p(z) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{2}{b}(z-a)e^{-(z-a)^2/b} & z \ge a \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $a$ is the location parameter (offset) and $b > 0$ is the scale parameter.

**Step 2 — Verify Normalization:**
$$\int_{a}^{\infty} \frac{2}{b}(z-a) e^{-(z-a)^2/b} dz = \left[-e^{-(z-a)^2/b}\right]_{a}^{\infty} = 1 \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Mean:**

$$\mu = \int_{a}^{\infty} z \cdot \frac{2}{b}(z-a) e^{-(z-a)^2/b} dz$$

Let $w = (z-a)^2/b$, so $dw = \frac{2(z-a)}{b} dz$ and $z = a + \sqrt{bw}$:

$$\mu = \int_{0}^{\infty} (a + \sqrt{bw}) e^{-w} dw = a \cdot 1 + \sqrt{b} \int_{0}^{\infty} w^{1/2} e^{-w} dw$$

The integral is $\Gamma(3/2) = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$. Thus:

$$\mu = a + \sqrt{\pi b / 4}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Variance:**

$$\sigma^2 = E[Z^2] - \mu^2$$

For Rayleigh:

$$E[Z^2] = \int_{a}^{\infty} z^2 \cdot \frac{2}{b}(z-a) e^{-(z-a)^2/b} dz = a^2 + 2a\sqrt{\pi b/4} + b$$

Combining and simplifying:

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{b(4 - \pi)}{4} \approx 0.2146 \, b$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q. (b):**

**Step 1 — Recall SNR Formula:**

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left( \frac{\sum \sum f^2(x,y)}{\sum \sum \eta^2(x,y)} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Plug in Given Values:**

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left( \frac{1.2 \times 10^{8}}{8.5 \times 10^{5}} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Ratio:**
$$\frac{1.2 \times 10^{8}}{8.5 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{1.2}{8.5} \times 10^{3} \approx 0.14118 \times 10^{3} = 141.18$$

**Step 4 — Take the Logarithm:**
$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(141.18) = 10 \times 2.1498 \approx 21.50 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5 — Comment:**
An SNR of **21.50 dB** is **moderate** — acceptable for general diagnostic imaging but **not sufficient for fine detail tasks** like detecting microcalcifications in mammography (which require SNR > 35 dB). Restoration filtering (Wiener filter) is recommended to enhance image quality.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 10** vs 20: SNR in image processing uses **10** (not 20), since it's a power ratio. Using 20 yields 43 dB — a 2× error.
> 2. **Confusing $\mu$ vs SNR** in noise definition: Gaussian noise with $\mu = 0$ does **not** mean the noise variance is zero. Variance is $\sigma^2 = 144$.
> 3. **Omitting units** in final answer: Always write "dB" explicitly.
> 4. **Motion Blur TF:** Students often forget the phase factor $e^{-j\pi u T}$. Full marks require both magnitude (sinc) and phase.
> 5. **Rayleigh PDF domain:** Don't write $p(z)$ for all $z$ — it's defined only for $z \ge a$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Image Degradation**

- 🔹 **Master Equation:** $g(x,y) = f(x,y) \ast h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$ and its frequency form $G(u,v) = F(u,v)H(u,v) + N(u,v)$.
- 🔹 **LSI assumption** is critical — it enables convolution and frequency-domain analysis.
- 🔹 **Motion Blur** PSF: rectangular of length $T$; $H(u,v)$ is **sinc × phase** factor.
- 🔹 **Atmospheric Turbulence** $H(u,v) = e^{-k(u^2+v^2)^{5/6}}$; $k \approx 0.0025$.
- 🔹 **Six Noise PDFs:** Gaussian, Rayleigh, Erlang, Exponential, Uniform, Impulse — know PDF + mean + variance.
- 🔹 **Periodic noise** = 2D sinusoid → spikes in frequency domain → notch filter removes it.
- 🔹 **Inverse filter fails** when $H(u,v) \approx 0$ — this is the *singularity problem*.
- 🔹 **Three methods to estimate $H$**: observation, experimentation, mathematical modeling.
- 🔹 **SNR** uses factor of **10** (not 20) for image processing.
- 🔹 **MTF** (Modulation Transfer Function) of a scanner is the clinical name for $\vert H(u,v) \vert$.
- 🔹 **Special case:** Exponential noise = Erlang with $b=1$.
- 🔹 **Salt-and-Pepper** noise: $P_a$ (pepper, low value), $P_b$ (salt, high value); if $P_a = P_b = 0$ and rest = 1, it's **random impulse noise**.
- 🔹 **In medical imaging:** Gaussian dominates in MRI/X-ray, Rayleigh in ultrasound speckle, Erlang in low-count PET, Impulse in faulty CCD sensors.
- 🔹 **Power spectrum** $S_{\eta\eta}(u,v) = \vert N(u,v)\vert^2$ is the frequency-domain noise characterization used in Wiener filtering.
- 🔹 **Restoration comes next** — Wiener, Constrained Least Squares, and Geometric Mean filters are the direct solutions that follow degradation modeling.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT833 - MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Image Noise?

In the context of medical image processing, **noise** is defined as any undesired random variation in pixel intensity values that corrupts the true signal emanating from the anatomical structure being imaged. It manifests as a stochastic, grainy, or speckled disturbance superimposed on the underlying image data, reducing the diagnostic reliability of the image.

Mathematically, for a 2-D digital image $f(x,y)$, a noise-corrupted image $g(x,y)$ can be expressed as:

$$g(x,y) = f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

where $f(x,y)$ represents the original clean image, $\eta(x,y)$ represents the additive noise component, and $(x,y)$ denotes the spatial coordinates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In medical imaging, noise is not merely a nuisance — it directly limits the detection of low-contrast lesions (e.g., small tumors, microcalcifications) and degrades the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**, which is a critical figure-of-merit in modalities such as MRI, CT, ultrasound, and X-ray.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** "Image noise is a random variation of brightness or color information in images, which is an undesirable by-product of image acquisition that obscures the desired information."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are recording a podcast in a quiet room. Your voice is the **signal** (the meaningful information — analogous to the anatomical structure in an X-ray). Suddenly, a noisy ceiling fan starts whirring in the background. The fan noise is unwanted, random, and overlays your voice. This is exactly what **noise** does to a medical image — the patient anatomy is the "voice" and the noise is the "fan whirring" that the radiologist must mentally filter out.

A better analogy: think of a clear photograph of a moonlit night. If you increase your camera's **ISO** (sensitivity) to capture more light, the photograph becomes brighter, but you also see scattered random bright/dark dots — this is **noise** corrupting the image.

## 1.3 Sources of Noise in Medical Imaging

Noise in medical images arises from multiple physical and electronic sources:

| Source | Description | Affected Modality |
|---|---|---|
| **Quantum mottle (Photon noise)** | Statistical fluctuation in the number of detected photons/electrons | X-ray, CT, PET, SPECT |
| **Thermal noise (Johnson-Nyquist noise)** | Random electron motion due to thermal agitation in conductors | MRI, X-ray detectors |
| **Shot noise** | Discrete nature of charge carriers in electronic circuits | All electronic detectors |
| **Speckle noise** | Constructive/destructive interference of coherent wavefronts | Ultrasound, OCT, SAR |
| **Quantization noise** | Error introduced during analog-to-digital conversion | All digital modalities |
| **K-space noise (Gaussian)** | Fourier-domain random noise from receive coils | MRI |
| **Readout / Flicker noise** | 1/f noise from electronic amplifiers | MRI, CT |
| **Quantum noise in scintillation** | Variation in optical photon count from scintillators | Nuclear medicine |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constant:** The fundamental lower bound for noise in any detection system is set by **Poisson statistics** (also called **shot noise** or **photon noise**), where the standard deviation of photon counts $N$ equals $\sqrt{N}$.

## 1.4 Why Noise Matters in Medical Diagnosis

- **Reduces Contrast Resolution:** Small low-contrast lesions (e.g., 5 mm breast microcalcifications) can be masked by noise.
- **Increases False Positives/Negatives:** A noisy image may create pseudo-structures that mimic disease, or hide real ones.
- **Limits Quantitative Analysis:** Tasks like tumor volumetry, perfusion measurement, and SUV (Standardized Uptake Value) in PET become unreliable.
- **Constrains Radiation Dose:** Lowering X-ray dose to protect patients increases noise — a clinical trade-off governed by the **ALARA principle** (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Modeling of Noise

Noise is modeled as a **random process**, characterized by its **Probability Density Function (PDF)**. Different physical mechanisms produce different noise distributions.

A noise PDF $p(z)$ is fully described by its:
- **Mean** ($\mu$) — average intensity
- **Variance** ($\sigma^2$) — spread of noise (power)
- **Standard Deviation** ($\sigma$) — RMS noise amplitude

The pixel intensity $z$ in a noisy image takes values in the range $[0, L-1]$ where $L$ is the number of gray levels (typically 256 for 8-bit images).

## 2.2 Major Noise Models (KTU High-Yield)

### 2.2.1 Gaussian Noise

Gaussian (or *normal*) noise is the most common model. It arises from thermal agitation, electronic readout, and from the **Central Limit Theorem** when multiple independent noise sources combine.

**PDF:**

$$p(z) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{(z-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$$

- **Mean:** $\mu$ (intensity offset)
- **Variance:** $\sigma^2$
- **Domain:** $-\infty < z < +\infty$, but practically bounded to $[0, L-1]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **About 70% of pixel values lie within $\mu \pm \sigma$**, and about 95% within $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ (Empirical Rule).

**Why it appears in MRI:** Due to the Fourier transform relationship between k-space and image-space, the noise in the reconstructed MRI image follows a Gaussian distribution even when the underlying k-space noise is Rician.

### 2.2.2 Uniform Noise

Uniform noise arises from **quantization** errors and certain types of sensor dithering.

**PDF:**

$$p(z) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{b-a} & \text{for } a \leq z \leq b \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

- **Mean:** $\mu = \dfrac{a+b}{2}$
- **Variance:** $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{(b-a)^2}{12}$

### 2.2.3 Impulse (Salt-and-Pepper) Noise

Also called *shot noise*, *binary noise*, or *data-drop-out noise*. Appears as random black and white pixels. Common in **MRI** (RF interference), **CT with metal artifacts**, and **faulty sensor transmission**.

**PDF:**

$$p(z) = \begin{cases} P_a & \text{for } z = a \text{ (pepper)} \\ P_b & \text{for } z = b \text{ (salt)} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

- If $P_a = P_b \neq 0$, noise is called salt-and-pepper.
- If either $P_a$ or $P_b$ is zero, it is called *unipolar*.
- **Mean:** $\mu = aP_a + bP_b$
- **Variance:** $\sigma^2 = (a-\mu)^2 P_a + (b-\mu)^2 P_b$

### 2.2.4 Rayleigh Noise

Used to model noise in **range imaging** (radar, ultrasound attenuation, laser imaging). The PDF arises when a 2-D vector with orthogonal Gaussian components has a magnitude.

**PDF:**

$$p(z) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{2}{b}(z-a) \, e^{-(z-a)^2/b} & \text{for } z \geq a \\ 0 & \text{for } z < a \end{cases}$$

- **Mean:** $\mu = a + \sqrt{\pi b/4}$
- **Variance:** $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{b(4-\pi)}{4}$

### 2.2.5 Erlang (Gamma) Noise

Used to model **laser imaging**, **speckle** in certain regimes, and noise in **Gamma cameras (nuclear medicine)**.

**PDF:**

$$p(z) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{a^b z^{b-1}}{(b-1)!} \, e^{-az} & \text{for } z \geq 0 \\ 0 & \text{for } z < 0 \end{cases}$$

- Parameters: $a > 0$, $b$ is a positive integer.
- **Mean:** $\mu = \dfrac{b}{a}$
- **Variance:** $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{b}{a^2}$

> [!NOTE]
> For $b=1$, Erlang distribution reduces to the **Exponential distribution**.

### 2.2.6 Exponential Noise

Special case of Erlang noise with $b = 1$.

**PDF:**

$$p(z) = \begin{cases} a \, e^{-az} & \text{for } z \geq 0 \\ 0 & \text{for } z < 0 \end{cases}$$

- **Mean:** $\mu = \dfrac{1}{a}$
- **Variance:** $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{a^2}$

### 2.2.7 Poisson Noise (Photon/Shot Noise)

The most physically fundamental noise in medical imaging. Occurs because photon detection is a discrete counting process. The probability of detecting $k$ photons in a fixed interval follows the Poisson distribution.

**PDF:**

$$p(z=k) = \frac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}}{k!}$$

- **Mean:** $\mu = \lambda$
- **Variance:** $\sigma^2 = \lambda$
- **Key Property:** $\text{SNR} = \sqrt{\lambda} = \sqrt{\text{mean signal}}$ (in photon-limited imaging)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for KTU:** For high photon counts ($\lambda > 10$), Poisson distribution is well-approximated by a **Gaussian distribution** with $\mu = \lambda$ and $\sigma^2 = \lambda$.

### 2.2.8 Speckle Noise

A **multiplicative** noise (not additive) that arises from coherent wave interference. Prevalent in **ultrasound, OCT, and synthetic aperture radar**.

**Model:**

$$g(x,y) = f(x,y) \cdot n(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

where $n(x,y)$ is a random multiplicative factor with mean 1 and $\eta$ is additive noise. The PDF of $n$ is often modeled as Rayleigh, Gamma, or K-distribution depending on the number of scatterers per resolution cell.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiplicative vs Additive:** Speckle is **multiplicative** — its variance scales with the local signal intensity. Bright regions appear noisier than dark regions. This is fundamentally different from Gaussian noise (which is additive with constant variance).

### 2.2.9 Rician Noise (MRI-Specific)

The noise in **MRI magnitude images** follows a **Rician distribution**, not Gaussian — because the magnitude of a complex Gaussian distribution in k-space produces a non-central chi distribution, which becomes Rician after taking the magnitude.

**PDF:**

$$p(z) = \frac{z}{\sigma^2} \, e^{-\frac{(z^2 + A^2)}{2\sigma^2}} \, I_0\!\left(\frac{zA}{\sigma^2}\right), \quad z \geq 0$$

where $A$ is the underlying true signal amplitude, $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of the original complex Gaussian noise, and $I_0$ is the modified Bessel function of the first kind, order 0.

> [!NOTE]
> **For high SNR ($A/\sigma \gg 1$):** Rician $\rightarrow$ Gaussian with mean $A$ and variance $\sigma^2$.
> **For low SNR ($A/\sigma \ll 1$):** Rician $\rightarrow$ **Rayleigh distribution** (in background regions where $A=0$).

## 2.3 Performance Metrics for Noisy Images

### 2.3.1 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{\mu_{\text{signal}}}{\sigma_{\text{noise}}}$$

In practice for an image:

$$\text{SNR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\mu_{\text{signal}}}{\sigma_{\text{noise}}}\right)$$

### 2.3.2 Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR)

$$\text{PSNR (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{L^2}{\text{MSE}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{L}{\sqrt{\text{MSE}}}\right)$$

where $L$ is the maximum pixel value (255 for 8-bit) and MSE is the **Mean Squared Error**:

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{MN}\sum_{x=0}^{M-1}\sum_{y=0}^{N-1}\left[f(x,y) - g(x,y)\right]^2$$

### 2.3.3 Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$$

### 2.3.4 Mean Absolute Error (MAE)

$$\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{MN}\sum_{x=0}^{M-1}\sum_{y=0}^{N-1}\left\vert f(x,y) - g(x,y) \right\vert$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive formula sheet** for the Noise topic. Memorize it before the exam.

| # | Noise / Metric | PDF Expression | Mean ($\mu$) | Variance ($\sigma^2$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Gaussian** | $\dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\,e^{-(z-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)}$ | $\mu$ | $\sigma^2$ |
| 2 | **Uniform** | $\dfrac{1}{b-a}$ on $[a,b]$ | $\dfrac{a+b}{2}$ | $\dfrac{(b-a)^2}{12}$ |
| 3 | **Salt-and-Pepper** | $P_a$ at $z=a$, $P_b$ at $z=b$ | $aP_a + bP_b$ | $(a-\mu)^2P_a + (b-\mu)^2P_b$ |
| 4 | **Rayleigh** | $\dfrac{2}{b}(z-a)e^{-(z-a)^2/b}$ for $z \geq a$ | $a + \sqrt{\pi b/4}$ | $\dfrac{b(4-\pi)}{4}$ |
| 5 | **Erlang (Gamma)** | $\dfrac{a^b z^{b-1}}{(b-1)!}e^{-az}$ for $z \geq 0$ | $\dfrac{b}{a}$ | $\dfrac{b}{a^2}$ |
| 6 | **Exponential** | $a\,e^{-az}$ for $z \geq 0$ | $\dfrac{1}{a}$ | $\dfrac{1}{a^2}$ |
| 7 | **Poisson** | $\dfrac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}}{k!}$ | $\lambda$ | $\lambda$ |
| 8 | **Speckle** | Multiplicative $g = f \cdot n$ | Intensity-dependent | Intensity-dependent |
| 9 | **Rician (MRI)** | $\dfrac{z}{\sigma^2}e^{-(z^2+A^2)/(2\sigma^2)}I_0\!\left(\dfrac{zA}{\sigma^2}\right)$ | $\sigma\sqrt{\pi/2}\,L_{1/2}(-A^2/(2\sigma^2))$ | — |

| Metric | Formula | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **MSE** | $\dfrac{1}{MN}\sum\sum(f-g)^2$ | intensity$^2$ |
| **RMSE** | $\sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | intensity |
| **MAE** | $\dfrac{1}{MN}\sum\sum\vert f-g \vert$ | intensity |
| **SNR (linear)** | $\mu_{\text{signal}}/\sigma_{\text{noise}}$ | dimensionless |
| **SNR (dB)** | $20\log_{10}(\mu_{\text{signal}}/\sigma_{\text{noise}})$ | decibels (dB) |
| **PSNR** | $10\log_{10}(L^2/\text{MSE})$ | decibels (dB) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Diagnostic Radiology:** Radiologists rely on image SNR to detect small lesions. The Rose criterion states that a lesion must have SNR $\geq 3$ to $5$ for reliable detection.
- **CT Dose Optimization:** X-ray tube current (mAs) is selected to balance patient dose and acceptable noise — doubling mAs reduces noise by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$.
- **MRI Scan Time:** Increasing the number of averages (NEX) reduces noise by $\sqrt{N}$, but proportionally increases scan time.
- **Ultrasound:** Speckle reduction is critical for visualizing organ parenchyma; techniques include compound imaging, multi-frequency, and adaptive filtering.
- **PET/SPECT:** Noise reduction determines quantitative accuracy of tracer uptake measurements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mean and Variance of Rayleigh Noise

The Rayleigh PDF is given as:

$$p(z) = \frac{2}{b}(z-a)\,e^{-(z-a)^2/b}, \quad z \geq a$$

**Step 1: Compute the Mean $\mu$**

$$\mu = \int_a^{\infty} z\,p(z)\,dz = \int_a^{\infty} z \cdot \frac{2}{b}(z-a)\,e^{-(z-a)^2/b}\,dz$$

**Step 2: Use the substitution** $u = z - a \Rightarrow du = dz$ and $z = u + a$:

$$\mu = \int_0^{\infty} (u+a) \cdot \frac{2u}{b}\,e^{-u^2/b}\,du$$

$$\mu = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{2u^2}{b}\,e^{-u^2/b}\,du + a\int_0^{\infty} \frac{2u}{b}\,e^{-u^2/b}\,du$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the first integral** using the standard Gaussian moment formula $\int_0^\infty u^2 e^{-u^2/b} du = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{4}\,b^{3/2}$:

$$\int_0^{\infty} \frac{2u^2}{b}\,e^{-u^2/b}\,du = \frac{2}{b} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{4} b^{3/2} = \frac{\sqrt{\pi b}}{2}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the second integral** using $\int_0^\infty u\,e^{-u^2/b}du = b/2$:

$$a\int_0^{\infty} \frac{2u}{b}\,e^{-u^2/b}\,du = a \cdot \frac{2}{b} \cdot \frac{b}{2} = a$$

**Step 5: Combine results:**

$$\boxed{\mu = a + \sqrt{\pi b/4}}$$

**Step 6: Compute the second moment $E[Z^2]$:**

$$E[Z^2] = \int_a^\infty z^2 p(z)\,dz$$

Applying the same substitution:

$$E[Z^2] = \int_0^\infty (u+a)^2 \frac{2u}{b} e^{-u^2/b} du = \int_0^\infty \frac{2u^3 + 4au^2 + 2a^2 u}{b} e^{-u^2/b} du$$

Using $\int_0^\infty u^3 e^{-u^2/b}du = \frac{b^2}{2}$:

$$E[Z^2] = \frac{2}{b}\cdot\frac{b^2}{2} + \frac{4a}{b}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{\pi b^3}}{4} + \frac{2a^2}{b}\cdot\frac{b}{2} = b + a\sqrt{\pi b} + a^2$$

**Step 7: Compute the Variance:**

$$\sigma^2 = E[Z^2] - \mu^2 = b + a\sqrt{\pi b} + a^2 - \left(a + \sqrt{\pi b/4}\right)^2$$

Expanding the square:

$$\left(a + \sqrt{\pi b/4}\right)^2 = a^2 + 2a\sqrt{\pi b/4} + \pi b/4 = a^2 + a\sqrt{\pi b} + \pi b/4$$

Subtracting:

$$\sigma^2 = b + a\sqrt{\pi b} + a^2 - a^2 - a\sqrt{\pi b} - \pi b/4 = b - \pi b/4$$

$$\boxed{\sigma^2 = \frac{b(4-\pi)}{4}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Mean and Variance of Erlang (Gamma) Noise

The Erlang PDF is given as:

$$p(z) = \frac{a^b z^{b-1}}{(b-1)!}\,e^{-az}, \quad z \geq 0, \quad a > 0, \quad b \in \mathbb{Z}^+$$

**Step 1: Mean**

$$\mu = E[Z] = \int_0^\infty z \cdot \frac{a^b z^{b-1}}{(b-1)!}\,e^{-az}\,dz = \frac{a^b}{(b-1)!}\int_0^\infty z^b e^{-az}\,dz$$

**Step 2: Apply the standard gamma integral** $\int_0^\infty z^n e^{-az} dz = \dfrac{n!}{a^{n+1}}$:

$$\mu = \frac{a^b}{(b-1)!} \cdot \frac{b!}{a^{b+1}} = \frac{a^b \cdot b!}{(b-1)! \cdot a^{b+1}} = \frac{b}{a}$$

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{b}{a}}$$

**Step 3: Second moment**

$$E[Z^2] = \int_0^\infty z^2 \cdot \frac{a^b z^{b-1}}{(b-1)!}\,e^{-az}\,dz = \frac{a^b}{(b-1)!}\int_0^\infty z^{b+1} e^{-az}\,dz = \frac{a^b}{(b-1)!} \cdot \frac{(b+1)!}{a^{b+2}}$$

$$E[Z^2] = \frac{(b+1)!}{(b-1)! \cdot a^2} = \frac{b(b+1)}{a^2}$$

**Step 4: Variance**

$$\sigma^2 = E[Z^2] - \mu^2 = \frac{b(b+1)}{a^2} - \frac{b^2}{a^2} = \frac{b}{a^2}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma^2 = \frac{b}{a^2}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: PSNR Expression

Given two images $f(x,y)$ (original) and $g(x,y)$ (noisy) of size $M \times N$, define the **mean squared error**:

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{MN}\sum_{i=0}^{M-1}\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}\left[f(i,j) - g(i,j)\right]^2$$

The **PSNR** is defined as the ratio of the peak signal power $L^2$ to the noise power MSE, expressed in decibels:

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\text{peak signal power}}{\text{noise power}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{L^2}{\text{MSE}}\right)$$

**Equivalent forms:**

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{L^2}{\text{MSE}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{L}{\sqrt{\text{MSE}}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{L}{\text{RMSE}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> Higher PSNR indicates a closer match between $f$ and $g$. A PSNR of 30 dB or higher is generally considered acceptable for medical images; 40+ dB indicates excellent fidelity.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Variance of Salt-and-Pepper Noise

**Problem:** A 256-level grayscale image is corrupted by salt-and-pepper noise. The probability of salt ($z = 255$) is $P_b = 0.05$ and the probability of pepper ($z = 0$) is $P_a = 0.05$. All other pixels retain their original values (probability 0.9 — but in this simplified two-level model, $P_a = P_b = 0.5$ if we treat only the noisy pixels). Calculate the mean and variance of the noise.

**Solution (simplified two-point model):** $a = 0$, $b = 255$, $P_a = P_b = 0.5$.

**Step 1: Mean**

$$\mu = aP_a + bP_b = 0 \cdot 0.5 + 255 \cdot 0.5 = 127.5$$

**Step 2: Variance**

$$\sigma^2 = (0 - 127.5)^2 \cdot 0.5 + (255 - 127.5)^2 \cdot 0.5$$

$$\sigma^2 = 16256.25 \cdot 0.5 + 16256.25 \cdot 0.5 = 16256.25$$

$$\boxed{\sigma = \sqrt{16256.25} \approx 127.5}$$

**Interpretation:** When salt-and-pepper is symmetric ($P_a = P_b$), the noise has zero mean offset but very high standard deviation (~50% of the dynamic range), explaining why impulse noise is so visually destructive.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Noise Identification by Histogram

| Histogram Appearance | Likely Noise | Confirmatory Test |
|---|---|---|
| Single bell curve centered mid-gray | Gaussian | Shapiro-Wilk test |
| Flat / rectangular distribution | Uniform | Kolmogorov-Smirnov |
| Sharp spikes at 0 and 255 | Salt-and-Pepper | Count extreme pixels |
| Skewed unimodal (heavy right tail) | Rayleigh | K-S test against Rayleigh |
| Decaying exponential shape | Exponential | Fit histogram, $R^2 > 0.95$ |
| Mean = Variance | Poisson | Compute sample mean and variance |
| Different mean in bright vs dark regions | Speckle (multiplicative) | Compute local variance vs local mean |
