| "Rice" shape in MRI background | Rician | Compare $\sigma_{\text{background}}$ between magnitude/phase |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Noise Modeling Workflow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Clean Image f x,y] --> B[Acquisition System]
    B --> C[Detector Circuit]
    C --> D[Amplifier]
    D --> E[Analog-to-Digital Converter]
    E --> F[Reconstruction Engine]
    F --> G[Digital Image g x,y]
    
    H[Photon Source X-ray RF US] --> B
    I[Thermal Noise Source] --> D
    J[Quantization Error] --> E
    K[Coherent Interference] --> F
    
    L[Noise Model Selection] --> M{Choose PDF}
    M --> N1[Gaussian]
    M --> N2[Poisson]
    M --> N3[Speckle Multiplicative]
    M --> N4[Impulse S and P]
    M --> N5[Rician MRI]
    M --> N6[Rayleigh]
    
    N1 --> O[Simulated Noisy Image]
    N2 --> O
    N3 --> O
    N4 --> O
    N5 --> O
    N6 --> O
    
    O --> P[Apply Noise Filter]
    P --> Q[Restored Image]
    Q --> R[Quality Metrics PSNR SNR MSE]
```

## 4.2 Noise Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[Image Noise] --> A1[Additive Noise]
    ROOT --> A2[Multiplicative Noise]
    
    A1 --> B1[Gaussian Thermal]
    A1 --> B2[Poisson Shot Photon]
    A1 --> B3[Impulse Salt and Pepper]
    A1 --> B4[Uniform Quantization]
    A1 --> B5[Rician MRI]
    
    A2 --> C1[Speckle Ultrasound]
    A2 --> C2[Film Grain Noise]
    
    B1 --> D1[Independent of Signal]
    B2 --> D2[Variance equals Mean]
    B3 --> D3[Extreme Black White Spikes]
    B4 --> D4[Constant Power Spectrum]
    B5 --> D5[Non Central Chi Distribution]
    
    C1 --> E1[Variance Scales with Intensity]
    C2 --> E2[Common in Analog Film]
```

## 4.3 SNR vs. Diagnostic Detectability (Engineering Trade-off)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Increase Dose mAs / NEX] --> B[More Photons Acquired]
    B --> C[Higher Signal Mean]
    C --> D[Noise Sigma grows as sqrt Mean]
    D --> E[SNR improves as sqrt Dose]
    E --> F[Better Lesion Detectability]
    
    G[Decrease Dose] --> H[Fewer Photons]
    H --> I[Lower Signal]
    I --> J[Patient Safety Benefit]
    J --> K[Lower SNR Risk]
    K --> L[Diagnostic Quality Loss]
    
    F --> M[Optimal Balance ALARA Principle]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Clinical Decision]
```

## 4.4 Spatial vs Frequency Domain View of Noise

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Image Domain x y] --> B[Noise Acts Locally]
    B --> B1[Impulse Noise isolated pixels]
    B --> B2[Gaussian Noise everywhere]
    
    A --> C[Fourier Domain u v]
    C --> D[Noise Power Spectrum N u v]
    D --> D1[White Noise Flat Spectrum]
    D --> D2[Pink Noise 1 over f]
    D --> D3[Brownian Noise 1 over f squared]
```

## 4.5 Noise Generation Pipeline for Simulation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Select Noise Type] --> B[Estimate Parameters]
    B --> B1[Mean mu from ROI]
    B --> B2[Variance sigma2 from ROI]
    B --> B3[Photon count lambda]
    
    B1 --> C[Generate Random Samples]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    
    C --> D[Box Muller for Gaussian]
    C --> E[Inverse CDF for others]
    C --> F[Knuth Algorithm for Poisson]
    
    D --> G[Add to Image f plus eta]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    
    G --> H[Clamp to 0 L-1]
    H --> I[Save Noisy Image]
```

## 4.6 Comparative PDF Plots (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[PDF Shape Comparison] --> A1[Gaussian Bell Symmetric]
    A --> A2[Uniform Flat Top]
    A --> A3[Exponential Decay]
    A --> A4[Rayleigh Skewed]
    A --> A5[Erlang Multi Modal Light]
    A --> A6[Salt Pepper Two Spikes]
    
    A1 --> P1[Peak at mu, Symmetric, Tails]
    A2 --> P2[Constant from a to b, Zero elsewhere]
    A3 --> P3[Max at z=0, Decays as e to minus az]
    A4 --> P4[Zero at z equals a, Peaks at a plus sqrt b over 2]
    A5 --> P5[For b greater than 1, Has a mode at b minus 1 over a]
    A6 --> P6[Spikes only at z equals 0 and z equals L minus 1]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Image Noise. List the different types of noise that affect medical images. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Image noise is defined as the random, unwanted variation in pixel intensity values that corrupts the true signal in a digital image. Mathematically, an image corrupted by additive noise is expressed as $g(x,y) = f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$, where $\eta(x,y)$ is the noise component.

**Types of noise affecting medical images:**

1. **Gaussian Noise** — thermal/electronic noise; MRI background, X-ray detectors.
2. **Poisson Noise (Photon/Shot Noise)** — photon-counting statistics; dominant in X-ray, CT, PET.
3. **Salt-and-Pepper (Impulse) Noise** — RF interference in MRI, transmission errors.
4. **Speckle Noise** — coherent wave interference; ultrasound, OCT.
5. **Rician Noise** — magnitude reconstruction of complex Gaussian MRI data.
6. **Uniform Noise** — quantization error in A/D conversion.
7. **Rayleigh Noise** — range imaging artifacts.
8. **Erlang/Gamma Noise** — laser imaging, Gamma camera noise.

---

### Question 2: Explain Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) and PSNR with formulas. Why are they important in medical imaging? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio):** It is the ratio of the mean signal intensity to the standard deviation of the noise.

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{\mu_{\text{signal}}}{\sigma_{\text{noise}}} \quad ; \quad \text{SNR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\mu_{\text{signal}}}{\sigma_{\text{noise}}}\right)$$

**PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio):** It measures the ratio of peak signal power to noise power (MSE) in decibels.

$$\text{PSNR (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{L^2}{\text{MSE}}\right)$$

where $L$ is the maximum pixel value (255) and MSE is the mean squared error between original and corrupted images.

**Importance in medical imaging:**
- Determines the detectability of small low-contrast lesions.
- Used to compare performance of different denoising filters.
- Guides dose optimization (ALARA principle) — lower dose → lower SNR.
- Quantifies the trade-off between image quality and patient safety.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**a)** Derive the mean and variance of **Rayleigh noise**. State its PDF and mention one medical imaging scenario where it occurs. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Explain **Speckle noise** in detail. How does it differ from Gaussian noise? Mention two speckle-reduction techniques used in ultrasound imaging. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**PDF of Rayleigh Noise:**

$$p(z) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{2}{b}(z-a)\,e^{-(z-a)^2/b}, & z \geq a \\[4pt] 0, & z < a \end{cases}$$

**Derivation of Mean:**

$$\mu = \int_a^\infty z \cdot \frac{2}{b}(z-a)\,e^{-(z-a)^2/b}\,dz$$

Substituting $u = z - a$:

$$\mu = \int_0^\infty \frac{2u(u+a)}{b}\,e^{-u^2/b}\,du$$

$$= \int_0^\infty \frac{2u^2}{b}\,e^{-u^2/b}\,du + a\int_0^\infty \frac{2u}{b}\,e^{-u^2/b}\,du$$

Using standard Gaussian moments:

$$= \frac{\sqrt{\pi b}}{2} + a = a + \sqrt{\pi b/4}$$

$$\boxed{\mu = a + \sqrt{\pi b/4}}$$

**Derivation of Variance:**

Second moment:

$$E[Z^2] = b + a\sqrt{\pi b} + a^2$$

Variance:

$$\sigma^2 = E[Z^2] - \mu^2 = b + a\sqrt{\pi b} + a^2 - \left(a^2 + a\sqrt{\pi b} + \frac{\pi b}{4}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\sigma^2 = \frac{b(4-\pi)}{4}}$$

**Medical imaging scenario:** Rayleigh noise occurs in **ultrasound imaging due to back-scattered signal envelope detection** and in **laser range imaging** used in optical coherence tomography (OCT).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Speckle Noise:**

Speckle is a granular, multiplicative noise that arises when coherent waves (ultrasound, laser) interact with tissue microstructures smaller than the resolution cell. The constructive and destructive interference of back-scattered wavelets produces random intensity variations.

**Mathematical model:**

$$g(x,y) = f(x,y) \cdot n(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

where $n(x,y)$ is the multiplicative component with mean 1 and $\eta$ is the additive thermal noise.

**Differences from Gaussian Noise:**

| Property | Speckle | Gaussian |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | **Multiplicative** | **Additive** |
| Variance | Scales with local intensity | Constant |
| Source | Coherent wave interference | Thermal/electronic |
| Appearance | Grainy texture, intensity-dependent | Uniform grain |
| Common in | Ultrasound, OCT, SAR | X-ray, MRI background, CT |

**Two Speckle-Reduction Techniques:**

1. **Multi-Look / Spatial Compounding:** Acquire images from different angles and average them. Speckle patterns are angle-dependent, so averaging decorrelates speckle while preserving structure.

2. **Adaptive Filtering (Lee / Frost / Kuan filters):** These filters compute local statistics within a moving window and apply smoothing that adapts to the local coefficient of variation, preserving edges while smoothing homogeneous regions.

3. **(Bonus) Wavelet-based despeckling:** Decompose the log-transformed image into wavelet sub-bands, apply soft-thresholding to high-frequency sub-bands, and reconstruct.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a)** Discuss the sources of noise in medical imaging modalities (X-ray, CT, MRI, Ultrasound). List the dominant noise type for each modality. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Derive the mean and variance of **Erlang (Gamma) noise**. Under what condition does it reduce to the exponential distribution? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Modality | Source of Noise | Dominant Noise Type |
|---|---|---|
| **X-ray** | Photon-counting statistics at the detector; film grain | **Poisson (Photon) Noise** |
| **CT** | Photon-counting in detector array; electronic noise in DAS | **Poisson Noise** (with Gaussian approximation at high counts) |
| **MRI** | Thermal noise in receive coils; complex Gaussian noise in k-space → magnitude image | **Rician Noise** (in magnitude images) |
| **Ultrasound** | Coherent wave interference from tissue scatterers | **Speckle (Multiplicative) Noise** |
| **PET** | Coincidence detection statistics; randoms and scatter | **Poisson Noise** |
| **SPECT** | Photon-counting in Gamma camera | **Poisson + Uniform Quantization** |
| **Mammography** | Low-dose photon statistics | **Quantum Mottle (Poisson)** |

**Detailed explanation:**

- **X-ray / CT:** X-ray imaging relies on counting photons transmitted through tissue. The number of detected photons per pixel follows Poisson statistics. Standard deviation $\sigma = \sqrt{N}$, so SNR = $\sqrt{N}$. Reducing dose reduces $N$ and degrades SNR — hence the dose-quality trade-off.

- **MRI:** Raw k-space data contains complex Gaussian noise. The image is reconstructed via Fourier transform, and the **magnitude operation** introduces a non-linear transformation, yielding a **Rician distribution** in the magnitude image. In high-SNR regions, Rician → Gaussian; in low-SNR (background) regions, it → Rayleigh.

- **Ultrasound:** Speckle arises from coherent interference. Its variance is proportional to local signal intensity, so bright structures appear noisier. It is multiplicative and not constant-power.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Erlang (Gamma) PDF:**

$$p(z) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{a^b z^{b-1}}{(b-1)!}\,e^{-az}, & z \geq 0 \\[4pt] 0, & z < 0 \end{cases}$$

where $a > 0$ and $b$ is a positive integer.

**Derivation of Mean:**

$$\mu = \int_0^\infty z \cdot \frac{a^b z^{b-1}}{(b-1)!}\,e^{-az}\,dz = \frac{a^b}{(b-1)!}\int_0^\infty z^b e^{-az}\,dz$$

Using $\int_0^\infty z^n e^{-az}dz = \dfrac{n!}{a^{n+1}}$:

$$\mu = \frac{a^b}{(b-1)!} \cdot \frac{b!}{a^{b+1}} = \frac{b}{a}$$

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{b}{a}}$$

**Derivation of Variance:**

Second moment:

$$E[Z^2] = \int_0^\infty z^2 \cdot \frac{a^b z^{b-1}}{(b-1)!}\,e^{-az}\,dz = \frac{a^b}{(b-1)!} \int_0^\infty z^{b+1} e^{-az}\,dz$$

$$= \frac{a^b}{(b-1)!} \cdot \frac{(b+1)!}{a^{b+2}} = \frac{b(b+1)}{a^2}$$

Variance:

$$\sigma^2 = E[Z^2] - \mu^2 = \frac{b(b+1)}{a^2} - \frac{b^2}{a^2} = \frac{b}{a^2}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma^2 = \frac{b}{a^2}}$$

**Reduction to Exponential:** Setting $b = 1$ in the Erlang PDF:

$$p(z) = \frac{a^1 z^{0}}{0!}\,e^{-az} = a\,e^{-az}, \quad z \geq 0$$

This is the **exponential distribution** with mean $\mu = 1/a$ and variance $\sigma^2 = 1/a^2$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Confusing Poisson noise with Gaussian noise — Poisson is discrete-count based, Gaussian is continuous.
> 2. Treating speckle as additive (it is multiplicative — variance scales with intensity).
> 3. Forgetting the constant $1/(b-1)!$ in the Erlang PDF normalization.
> 4. Stating Rayleigh mean as "$\sqrt{b}$" — it is actually $a + \sqrt{\pi b/4}$ (not zero-centered).
> 5. Using $|x|$ (vertical bars) inside markdown table cells breaks the table — use $\vert$ or $\mid$.
> 6. Mixing up PSNR formula — PSNR uses $10\log_{10}$, not $20\log_{10}$, because it is a power ratio, not amplitude ratio.
> 7. Forgetting that in MRI, **Gaussian noise in k-space → Rician noise in image space** (a frequently tested KTU question).
> 8. Not mentioning the ALARA principle when discussing dose vs. noise trade-off in X-ray/CT.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Noise**

- **Additive Noise Model:** $g(x,y) = f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$ — applies to Gaussian, Poisson (approx), Impulse, Uniform, Rayleigh, Rician.
- **Multiplicative Noise Model:** $g(x,y) = f(x,y) \cdot n(x,y)$ — applies to **Speckle**.
- **Gaussian PDF:** $\mu$ = mean, $\sigma^2$ = variance; bell-shaped, dominant in MRI/Electronic systems.
- **Poisson:** Mean = Variance = $\lambda$; dominant in photon-limited imaging (X-ray, CT, PET, SPECT).
- **Salt-and-Pepper:** Spikes at 0 and $L-1$; reduce with **median filter**.
- **Rician:** In **MRI magnitude images**; reduces to Gaussian (high SNR) or Rayleigh (zero signal).
- **Speckle:** Multiplicative in **ultrasound**; reduce with Lee, Frost, Kuan, or wavelet filters.
- **Rayleigh Mean:** $a + \sqrt{\pi b/4}$; **Variance:** $b(4-\pi)/4$.
- **Erlang Mean:** $b/a$; **Variance:** $b/a^2$; reduces to Exponential at $b=1$.
- **Uniform Mean:** $(a+b)/2$; **Variance:** $(b-a)^2/12$.
- **SNR (dB):** $20 \log_{10}(\mu/\sigma)$; **PSNR (dB):** $10 \log_{10}(L^2/\text{MSE})$.
- **MSE:** $\dfrac{1}{MN}\sum\sum(f-g)^2$.
- **Dose-SNR Trade-off:** Doubling X-ray dose (mAs) → noise decreases by factor $\sqrt{2}$ → SNR increases by $\sqrt{2}$.
- **MRI Averaging:** $N$ acquisitions averaged → noise $\sigma$ reduced by $\sqrt{N}$ → scan time $\propto N$.
- **ALARA Principle:** As Low As Reasonably Achievable — governs dose vs. quality trade-off in radiology.
- **Rose Criterion:** Lesion SNR $\geq 3$–$5$ required for reliable detection.
- **Log-transform trick for Speckle:** Apply $\log g = \log f + \log n$ to convert multiplicative to additive noise.
- **Histogram Inspection:** Symmetric bell → Gaussian; spikes at extremes → S&P; skewed → Rayleigh/Exponential.
- **Always state whether noise is additive or multiplicative** — this is the first thing KTU examiners look for.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** "**G**aussian, **P**oisson, **S**peckle, **S**alt-Pepper, **R**ician, **R**ayleigh, **E**rlang, **U**niform, **E**xponential" — *"GPS-SS-RRE-UE"* — the nine canonical noise types you must know for KTU 2024 ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blurring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT833 - MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Blurring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Medical Image Processing (OEEBT833)**, **Blurring** (also referred to as image smoothing, low-pass filtering, or spatial filtering) is a fundamental spatial-domain operation that reduces the high-frequency content of an image, thereby suppressing sharp intensity transitions, fine details, and stochastic noise. Mathematically, blurring is realized as the **2D convolution** of the original image $f(x,y)$ with a blurring kernel (also called a *Point Spread Function* or PSF) $h(x,y)$, producing a degraded image $g(x,y)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology (Must Use Verbatim):**
> Blurring is formally defined as the convolution of an image $f(x,y)$ with a low-pass filter kernel $h(x,y)$, such that:
> $$g(x,y) = f(x,y) \ast h(x,y)$$
> This is a **linear, shift-invariant (LSI)** degradation model and forms the foundation for both image enhancement and image restoration modules in the OEEBT833 syllabus.

In medical imaging, blurring can occur in two fundamentally different contexts:
1. **Intentional Blurring (Pre-processing):** Applied deliberately to reduce noise, suppress irrelevant details, or prepare images for downstream tasks (e.g., segmentation, edge detection).
2. **Unintentional Blurring (Degradation):** Occurs due to physical limitations of the imaging system, such as patient motion, optical defocus, atmospheric turbulence, or sensor limitations — requiring **image restoration** techniques to invert the blur.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "Looking Through a Frosted Glass Window"**
> Imagine you are standing outside a window and trying to read a newspaper inside the room. If the glass is perfectly clear, you can see every letter crisply (this is a sharp, unblurred image). If the glass becomes **frosted** or smeared with grease, all the sharp edges of the letters become soft, the fine print disappears, and only the large, bold headlines remain visible. This is precisely what blurring does to a digital image — it **smooths out rapid intensity changes** while preserving the broad, low-frequency structure (gradients, large organ boundaries, average tissue intensity).

Another intuitive analogy: **A moving camera**. When you snap a photo while rapidly panning your camera, the result is a **motion-blurred** image where edges stretch into streaks. This is exactly how a medical scanner behaves when a patient breathes or moves during a CT/MRI scan.

## 1.3 Why Blurring Matters in Medical Imaging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Relevance — KTU High-Yield Point:**
> - **Noise Suppression:** Medical images (CT, MRI, X-ray, Ultrasound) are inherently corrupted by quantum noise, thermal noise, and speckle noise. Blurring acts as a **low-pass filter** that suppresses this noise.
> - **Anatomical Scale Selection:** Radiologists focus on structures at specific scales (e.g., tumors at 5 mm, vessels at 1 mm). Blurring controls the *scale* of visible features — analogous to the **Gaussian scale-space** theory (Lindeberg, 1994).
> - **Pre-processing for Segmentation:** Region-growing and thresholding algorithms perform poorly on noisy images. A pre-blurring step improves segmentation accuracy by **5–20%** in typical CT organ delineation tasks.
> - **Foundation for Deconvolution:** The blur PSF is the kernel that Wiener deconvolution, blind deconvolution, and Richardson-Lucy algorithms attempt to **invert** during image restoration.

## 1.4 Types of Blur Covered in OEEBT833 Module 3

| Blur Type | Physical Cause | PSF Shape |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|
| **Gaussian Blur** | Diffraction-limited optics, general system blur | 2D Gaussian bell |
| **Averaging / Box Blur** | Uniform sensor averaging | Rectangular pulse |
| **Motion Blur** | Patient/equipment motion during exposure | Linear streak |
| **Defocus Blur** | Lens misfocus (out-of-plane tissue) | Disk (pillbox) |
| **Atmospheric Blur** | Turbulence in optical imaging (endoscopy) | Gaussian with long tail |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Image Degradation / Restoration Model

The blurring process in medical imaging is mathematically modeled as a **linear, shift-invariant (LSI) system** with additive noise:

$$g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

Where:
- $f(x,y)$ — Original (undegraded) medical image, size $M \times N$
- $h(x,y)$ — **Point Spread Function (PSF)**, the blur kernel
- $\ast$ — 2D convolution operator
- $\eta(x,y)$ — Additive noise (often modeled as zero-mean Gaussian)
- $g(x,y)$ — Observed (degraded) image

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Convolution?** Because the imaging system (scanner + optics + patient) is linear and shift-invariant, the output for any input can be computed by convolving the input with the system's impulse response (the PSF). The PSF is the image of an ideal point source (e.g., a single X-ray photon emitter) after passing through the imaging system.

## 2.2 Frequency-Domain Perspective (Critical for KTU 2024)

Applying the **Convolution Theorem** (a key KTU high-yield concept), convolution in the spatial domain becomes multiplication in the frequency domain:

$$G(u,v) = H(u,v) \cdot F(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

Where capital letters denote the **2D Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** of the corresponding spatial-domain quantities. The function $H(u,v)$ is called the **Optical Transfer Function (OTF)**, and its magnitude $\vert H(u,v) \vert$ is the **Modulation Transfer Function (MTF)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Blurring is a **low-pass filter** in the frequency domain — it attenuates high spatial frequencies (fine details, edges, noise) while preserving low spatial frequencies (smooth gradients, large structures). This is why blurring "removes" noise: most noise is high-frequency.

## 2.3 Types of Blurring Kernels — Detailed Analysis

### 2.3.1 Gaussian Blur (Most Common in Medical Imaging)

The 2D isotropic Gaussian PSF is the most important blur model in medical imaging because it is:
- **Rotationally symmetric** (isotropic — no preferred direction)
- **Separable** (can be applied as two 1D convolutions, saving computation)
- **Smooth in the frequency domain** (also Gaussian — no ringing artifacts)

$$h(x,y) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2 + y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

The parameter $\sigma$ (standard deviation) controls the **amount** of blur. Larger $\sigma$ → more blur.

### 2.3.2 Box (Averaging) Filter

Replaces each pixel with the unweighted average of its neighborhood. The PSF is a simple rectangular pulse:

$$h(x,y) = \frac{1}{mn} \quad \text{for } -\tfrac{m}{2} \leq x \leq \tfrac{m}{2}, \; -\tfrac{n}{2} \leq y \leq \tfrac{n}{2}$$

This produces visible **ringing artifacts** in the frequency domain (sinc-shaped OTF).

### 2.3.3 Motion Blur (Linear)

Caused by uniform relative motion during exposure. The PSF is a 1D line segment along the motion direction:

$$h(x,y) = \frac{1}{L} \quad \text{for } 0 \leq x \leq L, \; y = 0$$

(where $L$ is the motion length in pixels). The OTF is a **sinc** function: $H(u,v) = \text{sinc}(Lu)$.

### 2.3.4 Defocus (Disk / Pillbox) Blur

Caused by an out-of-focus lens. The PSF is a uniform disk of radius $R$:

$$h(x,y) = \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \quad \text{for } x^2 + y^2 \leq R^2$$

The OTF is a **jinc** (Bessel-based) function.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all critical formulas** the KTU examiner may test for the "Blurring" topic. Memorize the boxed entries.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Degradation Model | $g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$ | Convolution in spatial domain |
| 2 | Frequency Model | $G(u,v) = H(u,v) \cdot F(u,v) + N(u,v)$ | Multiplication in frequency domain |
| 3 | 2D Gaussian PSF | $h(x,y) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | $\sigma$ in pixels, $\sigma > 0$ |
| 4 | 1D Gaussian (separable component) | $h(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | 2D Gaussian = outer product of two 1D Gaussians |
| 5 | Box/Averaging PSF (m×n) | $h(x,y) = \frac{1}{mn}$ inside the window | Zero elsewhere |
| 6 | Motion Blur PSF (length L) | $h(x,y) = \frac{1}{L}$ along $0 \leq x \leq L$ | Direction-dependent |
| 7 | Defocus PSF (radius R) | $h(x,y) = \frac{1}{\pi R^2}$ inside disk | OTF is a jinc function |
| 8 | Convolution Theorem | $\mathcal{F}\{f \ast h\} = F \cdot H$ | Foundation of freq-domain filtering |
| 9 | PSF Normalization | $\sum_{x,y} h(x,y) = 1$ | Preserves average image intensity |
| 10 | OTF of Gaussian | $H(u,v) = \exp\!\left(-2\pi^2\sigma^2(u^2+v^2)\right)$ | Also Gaussian — no ringing |
| 11 | OTF of Box Filter (size m×n) | $H(u,v) = \frac{\sin(\pi u m)}{m \sin(\pi u)} \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi v n)}{n \sin(\pi v)}$ | Sinc-shaped, causes ringing |
| 12 | FWHM of Gaussian | $\text{FWHM} = 2\sqrt{2\ln 2} \cdot \sigma \approx 2.355\sigma$ | Used to quantify blur severity |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical / Industrial Applications:**
> 1. **CT Reconstruction:** Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) applies a **ramp filter**; pre-blurring with a Gaussian is used to suppress high-frequency noise amplification.
> 2. **MRI Pre-processing:** Gaussian blurring ($sigma$ = 1–2 pixels) is standard before tumor segmentation in brain MRI to suppress Rician noise.
> 3. **Ultrasound Speckle Reduction:** Anisotropic diffusion (a non-linear blur) is preferred over linear Gaussian for preserving edges.
> 4. **PET Imaging:** The intrinsic PSF of PET scanners is approximately Gaussian with FWHM ≈ 4–6 mm, blurring small lesions. Resolution recovery algorithms (e.g., PSF modeling in Siemens TrueX) attempt to deconvolve this blur.
> 5. **Digital Pathology:** Multi-scale Gaussian blurring (scale-space) is used to detect nuclei at different magnifications.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Mandate:** Derivations are high-weightage in Part B (14-mark) questions. The following derivations are exhaustive — every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No shortcut phrases like "it can be shown" are used.

## 3.1 Derivation: 2D Gaussian PSF from the 1D Gaussian

### Step 1 — Start with the 1D Gaussian Probability Density Function

The 1D Gaussian PDF is defined as:

$$g_1(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

This is the standard normal distribution with mean $\mu = 0$ and standard deviation $\sigma$.

### Step 2 — Define the 1D Blur Kernel (Centered, Truncated)

For a discrete image of size $M \times N$, we center the kernel at the origin. A 1D Gaussian PSF (for blurring along the x-direction only) is identical to the 1D Gaussian PDF, normalized to sum to 1:

$$h_1(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right), \quad x \in \mathbb{Z}$$

### Step 3 — Define the Independent 1D Gaussian Along y

Similarly, for the y-direction:

$$h_1(y) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma} \exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right), \quad y \in \mathbb{Z}$$

### Step 4 — Construct the 2D Kernel as an Outer Product

Since the 2D isotropic Gaussian is **separable** into two independent 1D Gaussians, the 2D PSF is the outer product:

$$h_2(x,y) = h_1(x) \cdot h_1(y)$$

### Step 5 — Substitute and Simplify

Substituting the expressions from Steps 2 and 3:

$$h_2(x,y) = \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\right] \cdot \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma} \exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\right]$$

Multiplying the constants:

$$h_2(x,y) = \frac{1}{(\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma)^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2 + y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2 + y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

This is the **2D isotropic Gaussian PSF**, the standard blur model in medical imaging. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation: Optical Transfer Function of the Gaussian PSF

### Step 1 — State the OTF Definition

The OTF is the 2D Fourier Transform of the PSF:

$$H(u,v) = \mathcal{F}\{h(x,y)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(x,y) \, e^{-j2\pi(ux+vy)} \, dx \, dy$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Gaussian PSF

$$H(u,v) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) e^{-j2\pi(ux+vy)} \, dx \, dy$$

### Step 3 — Separate the Integral (Using Separability)

The exponent splits additively:

$$H(u,v) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}} e^{-j2\pi ux} dx \cdot \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma^2}} e^{-j2\pi vy} dy$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the 1D Gaussian Fourier Transform

The Fourier transform of a 1D Gaussian $e^{-ax^2}$ is $\sqrt{\pi/a} \cdot e^{-\pi^2 u^2 / a}$. With $a = \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}$:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}} e^{-j2\pi ux} dx = \sigma\sqrt{2\pi} \cdot e^{-2\pi^2 \sigma^2 u^2}$$

The same applies to the y-integral. Therefore:

$$H(u,v) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} \cdot \left(\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}\, e^{-2\pi^2\sigma^2 u^2}\right) \cdot \left(\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}\, e^{-2\pi^2\sigma^2 v^2}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Simplify

The constants cancel: $\frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} \cdot \sigma^2 \cdot 2\pi = 1$, leaving:

$$H(u,v) = \exp\!\left(-2\pi^2\sigma^2(u^2 + v^2)\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** The OTF of a Gaussian PSF is **also a Gaussian** in the frequency domain. This is a unique and beautiful property — it means Gaussian blurring causes **no ringing artifacts** in the output image, unlike the box or motion blur, whose OTFs have sinc-shaped side lobes.

## 3.3 Derivation: Total Variance of a Box Filtered Image (Noise Suppression)

### Step 1 — Assume Independent Zero-Mean Noise

Let the input be $f(x,y) = s(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$, where $s$ is the noise-free signal and $\eta$ is i.i.d. zero-mean noise with variance $\sigma_\eta^2$.

### Step 2 — Apply the Box Filter (Averaging over a $k \times k$ Window)

$$g(x,y) = \frac{1}{k^2} \sum_{i=-a}^{a}\sum_{j=-a}^{a} f(x+i, y+j), \quad k = 2a+1$$

### Step 3 — Compute Output Variance (Noise Component)

The noise component of $g$ is $\eta_g(x,y) = \frac{1}{k^2}\sum \eta(x+i,y+j)$. Since the noise samples are independent and identically distributed:

$$\text{Var}[\eta_g] = \text{Var}\!\left[\frac{1}{k^2}\sum \eta(x+i,y+j)\right] = \frac{1}{k^4} \cdot k^2 \cdot \sigma_\eta^2 = \frac{\sigma_\eta^2}{k^2}$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

$$\sigma_{\eta,\text{out}}^2 = \frac{\sigma_{\eta,\text{in}}^2}{k^2} \implies \text{SNR improvement} = k$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A $k \times k$ box filter **reduces noise variance by a factor of $k^2$** (and improves SNR by a factor of $k$). For a Gaussian filter, the equivalent noise reduction is $\sigma_{\eta,\text{out}}^2 \approx \sigma_{\eta,\text{in}}^2 \cdot \frac{\pi \sigma^2}{2}$ (for large $\sigma$).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Blur Kernel

| Image Characteristic | Recommended Blur | Reason |
|---------------------|------------------|--------|
| High Gaussian noise, fine details needed | Small $\sigma$ Gaussian ($\sigma \leq 1.5$) | Minimal detail loss, moderate noise reduction |
| Heavy speckle noise (ultrasound) | Anisotropic diffusion / Median filter | Median preserves edges better than linear blur |
| Motion artifact in CT | Estimate motion PSF → Wiener deconvolution | Linear blur must be inverted, not applied |
| Pre-segmentation | Gaussian, $\sigma = 1$–$2$ pixels | Smooths intensity gradients, prevents over-segmentation |
| Pre-edge detection (Canny) | Gaussian, $\sigma = 1.4$ (Canny's recommended) | Matches Canny's optimal smoothing |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Blurring Pipeline

The following block diagram illustrates the complete signal-flow of the medical-image blurring process, from acquisition to blurred output, including both spatial and frequency-domain pathways.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ORIGINAL MEDICAL IMAGE f x y] --> B[ACQUISITION SYSTEM LSI]
    P[POINT SOURCE DELTA FUNCTION] --> B
    B --> C[BLURRED OUTPUT g x y]
    A --> D[SPATIAL DOMAIN PATH]
    B --> D
    D --> E[CONVOLVE WITH PSF h x y]
    E --> F[ADD NOISE ETA x y]
    F --> G[OUTPUT g x y]
    A --> H[FREQUENCY DOMAIN PATH]
    B --> H
    H --> I[COMPUTE 2D FFT]
    I --> J[F u v AND H u v]
    J --> K[POINTWISE MULTIPLY]
    K --> L[ADD NOISE SPECTRUM]
    L --> M[COMPUTE INVERSE FFT]
    M --> N[OUTPUT g x y]
    G --> O[FINAL BLURRED IMAGE]
    N --> O
    C --> O
```

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart: Selecting a Blur Kernel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[START: Need to Blur Image] --> Q1{Is noise Gaussian?}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Need to preserve edges?}
    Q1 -- NO, SPECKLE --> S2[Use Median or Anisotropic Diffusion]
    Q2 -- YES --> S3[Use Small Sigma Gaussian sigma 1 to 1.5]
    Q2 -- NO --> S4[Use Box or Large Sigma Gaussian]
    Q1 -- NO, IMPULSE --> S5[Use Median Filter]
    S2 --> END[APPLY SELECTED KERNEL]
    S3 --> END
    S4 --> END
    S5 --> END
```

## 4.3 Blur Taxonomy Tree (Mermaid Block-Diagram Fallback)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BLURRING IN MEDICAL IMAGING] --> B[LINEAR BLUR]
    A --> C[NON-LINEAR BLUR]
    B --> B1[GAUSSIAN BLUR]
    B --> B2[BOX AVERAGING BLUR]
    B --> B3[MOTION BLUR]
    B --> B4[DEFOCUS DISK BLUR]
    C --> C1[MEDIAN FILTER]
    C --> C2[ANISOTROPIC DIFFUSION]
    C --> C3[BILATERAL FILTER]
    B1 --> B1A[ISOTROPIC, SEPARABLE, SMOOTH OTF]
    B2 --> B2A[FAST, BUT RINGING ARTIFACTS]
    B3 --> B3A[DIRECTIONAL, SINC OTF]
    B4 --> B4A[ROTATIONALLY SYMMETRIC, JINC OTF]
    C1 --> C1A[EDGE PRESERVING, GOOD FOR IMPULSE NOISE]
    C2 --> C2A[EDGE PRESERVING, GOOD FOR SPECKLE]
    C3 --> C3A[EDGE PRESERVING, INTENSITY AWARE]
```

## 4.4 Frequency-Domain Visualization (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT SPECTRUM F u v] --> B[OTF H u v GAUSSIAN SHAPE]
    B --> C[PRODUCT G u v]
    C --> D[NOISE N u v ADDED]
    D --> E[OUTPUT SPECTRUM G u v]
    E --> F[INVERSE FFT]
    F --> G[BLURRED IMAGE g x y]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrammatic Interpretation:** In the frequency domain, the Gaussian OTF is itself a Gaussian function centered at the origin with rapidly decaying tails. It multiplies $F(u,v)$ pointwise, attenuating high frequencies (away from origin) more strongly than low frequencies (near origin). This is the precise mechanism by which blurring "suppresses" fine detail and noise.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the Point Spread Function (PSF) of an imaging system. Why is the Gaussian PSF preferred in medical image processing?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The Point Spread Function (PSF), denoted $h(x,y)$, is the impulse response of a linear, shift-invariant (LSI) imaging system. It is the image produced by the system when the input is a perfect point source (a 2D Dirac delta $\delta(x,y)$). Mathematically, the observed image is $g(x,y) = f(x,y) \ast h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$.
- **(2 marks)** The Gaussian PSF $h(x,y) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ is preferred in medical image processing for three reasons: (i) it is **separable** into two 1D Gaussians, enabling efficient computation; (ii) it is **rotationally symmetric** (isotropic) — no preferred direction matches the nature of most biological tissues; and (iii) its Fourier transform is **also a Gaussian**, so it introduces **no ringing artifacts** in the output, unlike the box or motion blur.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supple Paper]**
*State and explain the convolution theorem. How is it used to perform blurring in the frequency domain?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1.5 marks)** The convolution theorem states that convolution in the spatial domain corresponds to pointwise multiplication in the frequency domain: $\mathcal{F}\{f(x,y) \ast h(x,y)\} = F(u,v) \cdot H(u,v)$.
- **(1.5 marks)** To blur an image in the frequency domain: (i) compute the 2D FFT of the image, $F(u,v)$; (ii) compute the 2D FFT of the blur kernel, $H(u,v)$ (the OTF); (iii) multiply them: $G(u,v) = F(u,v) \cdot H(u,v)$; (iv) compute the inverse 2D FFT of $G(u,v)$ to obtain the blurred image $g(x,y)$. This approach is computationally efficient when the kernel is large because FFT-based convolution has $O(N^2 \log N)$ complexity versus $O(N^2 k^2)$ for direct spatial convolution.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern (Module 3 — Internal Choice):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. Total = 14 marks.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Paper, Module 3]*

**(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for the 2D Gaussian Point Spread Function (PSF) used in medical image blurring. Show that the resulting kernel is normalized. (7 marks)

**(b)** A $5 \times 5$ box filter is applied to a medical image corrupted by zero-mean Gaussian noise with standard deviation $\sigma_\eta = 20$ gray levels. Calculate the standard deviation of the noise in the filtered output. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of the 2D Gaussian PSF (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the 1D Gaussian PDF:
$$h_1(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** State that a 2D rotationally symmetric Gaussian must be separable and depend only on the radial distance $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Form the 2D PSF as the outer product $h_2(x,y) = h_1(x) \cdot h_1(y)$:
$$h_2(x,y) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma}\exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Simplify:
$$h(x,y) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Verify normalization by integrating over the entire real plane (using the standard Gaussian integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-ax^2}dx = \sqrt{\pi/a}$):
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(x,y)\,dx\,dy = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} \cdot \sqrt{2\pi}\sigma \cdot \sqrt{2\pi}\sigma = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} \cdot 2\pi\sigma^2 = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms the kernel preserves the average image intensity (DC gain = 1).

---

**Part (b) — Noise Standard Deviation in the Filtered Output (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** A $5 \times 5$ box filter has kernel size $k = 5$, so $k^2 = 25$ pixels contribute to each output pixel.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The output noise is the average of 25 i.i.d. noise samples:
$$\eta_g(x,y) = \frac{1}{25}\sum_{i,j} \eta(x+i, y+j)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Using the variance formula for a sum of independent variables (each with variance $\sigma_\eta^2 = 400$):
$$\text{Var}[\eta_g] = \frac{1}{25^2} \cdot 25 \cdot \sigma_\eta^2 = \frac{\sigma_\eta^2}{25} = \frac{400}{25} = 16$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Therefore:
$$\sigma_{\eta,\text{out}} = \sqrt{16} = 4 \text{ gray levels}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** **Conclusion:** The box filter reduces the noise standard deviation from **20 → 4 gray levels** (a 5× improvement, matching $k = 5$). The trade-off is a 5× loss of image sharpness, illustrating the **noise-vs-sharpness trade-off** that is central to all blurring operations.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supple Paper, Module 3]*

**(a)** With the help of the general image degradation model, explain the role of the convolution theorem in performing blur in the frequency domain. Derive the OTF of a 2D Gaussian PSF. (7 marks)

**(b)** A motion-blurred CT image has a PSF approximated as $h(x,y) = \frac{1}{10}$ for $0 \leq x \leq 9$ and $y = 0$, and zero elsewhere. Compute the magnitude of the OTF at spatial frequency $u = 0.1$ cycles/pixel along the u-axis (assuming $v = 0$). (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Degradation Model and OTF Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the degradation model:
$$g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** State the convolution theorem:
$$\mathcal{F}\{f \ast h\} = F(u,v) \cdot H(u,v)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Transform the degradation model:
$$G(u,v) = H(u,v) \cdot F(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Explain: blurring = multiplication by a low-pass function $H(u,v)$ in the frequency domain. Noise is additive in both domains.

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Derive OTF of Gaussian: Take the 2D Fourier transform of the Gaussian PSF. Using the separability property and the standard Gaussian FT pair, $H(u,v) = \exp\!\left(-2\pi^2\sigma^2(u^2+v^2)\right)$.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclude: the Gaussian OTF is also Gaussian → no ringing.

---

**Part (b) — OTF of Motion Blur PSF (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The motion PSF is a 1D rectangular pulse of length $L = 10$:
$$h(x,y) = \frac{1}{L} \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{x}{L}\right) \cdot \delta(y)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Its 2D Fourier transform (using the Fourier transform of a rect function) is:
$$H(u,v) = \frac{\sin(\pi u L)}{\pi u L} = \text{sinc}(uL) \quad \text{at } v=0$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute $L = 10$ and $u = 0.1$:
$$H(0.1, 0) = \frac{\sin(\pi \cdot 0.1 \cdot 10)}{\pi \cdot 0.1 \cdot 10} = \frac{\sin(\pi)}{\pi} = \frac{0}{\pi} = 0$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The magnitude is therefore:
$$\vert H(0.1, 0) \vert = 0$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** **Interpretation:** At frequency $u = 0.1$ cycles/pixel, the motion-blur OTF is **exactly zero** (a null). This means the imaging system completely **loses all information** at this spatial frequency — those image frequencies are unrecoverable by direct inverse filtering, which is why Wiener filtering with noise regularization is essential.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the normalization constant** $\frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2}$ in the Gaussian PSF — loses 1–2 marks per question. **Always verify** $\sum h(x,y) = 1$.
> 2. **Confusing the 1D and 2D Gaussian** formulas — the 1D has $\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma$ in the denominator; the 2D has $2\pi\sigma^2$. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Using direct convolution in frequency domain without the convolution theorem** — students write $G = F + H$ instead of $G = F \cdot H$. This is a 3-mark penalty.
> 4. **Sign errors in the motion-blur OTF** — the sinc function uses $\sin(\pi u L)$ in the numerator, **not** $\sin(u L)$. The $\pi$ factor is critical.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert FWHM ↔ $\sigma$** in problems — remember $\text{FWHM} = 2\sqrt{2\ln 2}\,\sigma \approx 2.355\,\sigma$.
> 6. **Conflating spatial-domain convolution with element-wise matrix multiplication** — these are entirely different operations in image processing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this the night before the exam):**
> - **Blurring = Convolution with PSF** + additive noise: $g = f \ast h + \eta$
> - **Convolution Theorem:** Spatial convolution ↔ Frequency multiplication: $G = F \cdot H$
> - **2D Gaussian PSF:** $h(x,y) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$
> - **Gaussian OTF:** $H(u,v) = \exp\!\left(-2\pi^2\sigma^2(u^2+v^2)\right)$ — no ringing!
> - **Box filter** reduces noise variance by factor $k^2$ (where $k$ is the window size)
> - **Motion PSF** is a 1D line; its OTF is a **sinc** function (causes ringing)
> - **Defocus PSF** is a uniform disk; its OTF is a **jinc** function
> - **Separability:** 2D Gaussian = outer product of two 1D Gaussians (huge computational saving)
> - **Isotropy:** Gaussian is rotationally symmetric — ideal for medical images (no anatomical preferred direction)
> - **FWHM ≈ 2.355 σ** (Gaussian)
> - **Blurring is a low-pass filter** — suppresses high frequencies (noise + fine detail) → preserves low frequencies (large structures)
> - **Two contexts:** Intentional blur (pre-processing) vs. unintentional blur (degradation → requires restoration)
> - **In medical imaging,** Gaussian blur is the most commonly assumed PSF model (matches diffraction, PET, MRI point response)
> - **Inverse filtering** of a blurred image amplifies noise — always prefer **Wiener filtering** in practice
> - **Rule of thumb:** $\sigma$ for pre-segmentation Gaussian blur = 1–2 pixels in CT/MRI

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modelling Image Degradation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT833 - MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Modelling Image Degradation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Image Degradation Model (KTU 2024 Standard Formulation):**
> An image is said to be **degraded** when its observed form differs from the true scene because of the imaging system's limitations or unwanted random disturbances. The **general image degradation model** represents this process mathematically as a linear operation on the original image corrupted by an additive noise term.

The fundamental degradation equation in the spatial domain is:

$$g(x,y) = H[f(x,y)] + \eta(x,y)$$

where every term has a strict engineering meaning:
- $f(x,y)$ is the **original, undegraded input image** (the ideal scene radiance reaching the sensor).
- $H[\cdot]$ is the **degradation operator** — a deterministic, often linear, system function that distorts the image (blur, motion, defocus, atmospheric effects).
- $\eta(x,y)$ is the **additive noise term** — a stochastic, zero-mean (or low-mean) random field.
- $g(x,y)$ is the **observed, degraded image** that we actually capture.

If the operator $H$ is assumed **linear and position-invariant (LPI)**, then the convolution description holds in the spatial domain:

$$g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

and equivalently in the **frequency domain** through the Convolution Theorem:

$$G(u,v) = H(u,v) \cdot F(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like taking a photograph through a rain-splattered car windshield at night.**
> - The **original scene** is what your eyes would see on a clear day.
> - The **degradation function $H$** plays the role of the raindrops and smudges on the glass — they systematically blur, smear, and distort the image in a predictable, geometric way.
> - The **noise $\eta$** is like the random flickering of streetlights, the grainy "snow" on a low-quality camera, or the random electronic static — it adds unpredictable random dots on top.
> - The **captured photograph** is the blurry, grainy image you actually save on your phone.

For medical imaging (X-ray, MRI, CT, Ultrasound), degradation arises from:
- **Patient motion** (breathing, heartbeat — motion blur).
- **Sensor limitations** (finite focal spot size — defocus blur).
- **Electronic noise** in detectors (Gaussian, Poisson noise).
- **Quantization noise** from Analog-to-Digital conversion.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The model is **forward** (degradation) when we go from $f \rightarrow g$. It becomes **inverse** (restoration) when we go from $g \rightarrow f$. Restoration is the *hard* problem because $H$ and $N$ are rarely known exactly.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Notations

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $f(x,y)$ | Original image | Intensity (grayscale value) |
| $g(x,y)$ | Degraded image | Intensity (grayscale value) |
| $h(x,y)$ | Degradation Point Spread Function (PSF) | Dimensionless kernel |
| $H(u,v)$ | Optical Transfer Function (OTF) / Modulation Transfer Function (MTF) magnitude | Dimensionless |
| $\eta(x,y)$ | Additive noise random field | Intensity |
| $\mu$ | Mean of noise | Intensity |
| $\sigma^2$ | Variance of noise | Intensity² |
| $\bar{S}_n$ | Periodogram of noise | Intensity² |

The **Point Spread Function (PSF)** and the **Optical Transfer Function (OTF)** are Fourier transform pairs:

$$H(u,v) = \mathcal{F}\{h(x,y)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(x,y)\, e^{-j2\pi(ux+vy)}\, dx\, dy$$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Degradation Process

The overall degradation pipeline can be broken into **four sequential stages**, each contributing a specific kind of distortion:

> **Stage 1 — Illumination & Photon Capture**
> The original scene $f(x,y)$ interacts with the imaging optics. The optics map the scene through a 2D **aperture function**, which determines the depth of field and resolution limit.

> **Stage 2 — Optical Blur (Deterministic)**
> Due to diffraction, defocus, or relative motion between sensor and subject, a **convolution with the PSF** $h(x,y)$ occurs. This is the deterministic $H[\cdot]$ part.

> **Stage 3 — Photon / Electronic Noise (Stochastic)**
> Random fluctuations are added — thermal noise in CCD, shot noise from photon statistics, quantization noise. This is the $\eta(x,y)$ part.

> **Stage 4 — Sampling & Quantization (Digitization)**
> The continuous image is discretized to a finite grid, introducing aliasing and rounding errors. This is often lumped into the noise term.

## 2.2 Three Canonical Degradation Models (KTU High-Yield)

The KTU syllabus emphasizes **three classical analytical PSF/OTF models** that must be memorized for board questions.

### Model 1 — Atmospheric Turbulence
Caused by refractive index variations in long-path imaging (e.g., aerial photographs, telescope images).

$$H(u,v) = e^{-k\left(u^2 + v^2\right)^{5/6}}$$

where $k$ is a turbulence constant depending on atmospheric conditions ($k > 0$).

### Model 2 — Motion Blur (Uniform Linear Motion)
Caused by relative linear motion between camera and subject during exposure time $T$.

$$H(u,v) = \frac{T}{\pi(ua + vb)} \sin\!\big[\pi(ua + vb)\big]\, e^{-j\pi(ua + vb)}$$

where $(a, b)$ is the displacement vector (e.g., $a=1, b=0$ for horizontal motion).

### Model 3 — Out-of-Focus Blur
Modelled as a circular aperture causing an "Airy disk" PSF.

$$H(u,v) = \frac{J_1\!\big(\pi d \sqrt{u^2+v^2}\big)}{\pi d \sqrt{u^2+v^2}}$$

where $J_1(\cdot)$ is the **Bessel function of the first kind, order 1**, and $d$ is the radius of the confusion circle.

## 2.3 The Six Canonical Noise Probability Models

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates memorization of the **six noise PDFs**. Each represents a different physical mechanism.

| # | Noise Model | PDF $p(z)$ | Valid Range | Physical Origin |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Gaussian** | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma} e^{-(z-\mu)^2/2\sigma^2}$ | $-\infty < z < \infty$ | Electronic sensor noise, thermal noise |
| 2 | **Rayleigh** | $\dfrac{2}{b}(z-a)e^{-(z-a)^2/b}$ for $z \geq a$ | $z \geq a$ | Range imaging, ultrasound speckle |
| 3 | **Erlang (Gamma)** | $\dfrac{a^b z^{b-1}}{(b-1)!}e^{-az}$ | $z \geq 0$ | Photon counting, Poisson limit |
| 4 | **Exponential** | $a\, e^{-az}$ | $z \geq 0$ | Laser imaging, special case of Erlang with $b=1$ |
| 5 | **Uniform** | $\dfrac{1}{b-a}$ | $a \leq z \leq b$ | Quantization noise (ideal ADC) |
| 6 | **Impulse (Salt & Pepper)** | $P_a$ for $z=a$, $P_b$ for $z=b$ | Discrete $z \in \{a,b\}$ | Dead pixels, transmission errors |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mean and Variance of Noise** (essential KTU formulae):
> - **Mean:** $\bar{z} = \sum_{i=1}^{L} z_i\, p(z_i)$
> - **Variance:** $\sigma^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{L} (z_i - \bar{z})^2 p(z_i)$

## 2.4 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) Estimation

When the **true noise is not directly available** (which is the common case), SNR is estimated from the degraded image $g(x,y)$ itself:

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{\sum_{(x,y)\in A} g(x,y)^2}{\sum_{(x,y)\in A} \big[g(x,y) - \bar{g}\big]^2}$$

where $A$ is a small homogeneous sub-region, and $\bar{g}$ is the mean intensity in that region.

> [!TIP]
> The numerator represents **signal power** (mean squared value), and the denominator represents **noise variance** about the local mean. A high SNR means a clean image; low SNR means a noisy image.

## 2.5 Periodicity Estimation from Noise Spectrum

To detect **periodic noise** (e.g., 50 Hz power line interference) we compute the **periodogram** of the noise:

$$S_{N}(u,v) = \vert N(u,v)\vert^2$$

A practical estimator (Gonzalez & Woods formulation taught in KTU):

$$N^p = \frac{M \cdot N}{\displaystyle\sum_{(u,v)\in S_{uv}} \frac{S_{uv}}{\text{Number of points in } S_{uv}}}$$

where $S_{uv}$ is a set of "spike" pixels in the spectrum suspected of being periodic noise.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| Concept | Formula | Key Variables | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spatial degradation | $g = H[f] + \eta$ | $f, H, \eta, g$ | Forward modeling of any blur |
| LPI Convolution | $g = h \ast f + \eta$ | $h$ is PSF | Optical systems, motion |
| Frequency model | $G = HF + N$ | $H$ is OTF/MTF | Fast computation via FFT |
| Turbulence OTF | $e^{-k(u^2+v^2)^{5/6}}$ | $k$ turbulence constant | Telescope, aerial imaging |
| Motion OTF | $\dfrac{T\sin[\pi(ua+vb)]}{\pi(ua+vb)}e^{-j\pi(ua+vb)}$ | $T$ exposure, $(a,b)$ velocity | MRI motion correction |
| Defocus OTF | $\dfrac{J_1(\pi d \sqrt{u^2+v^2})}{\pi d \sqrt{u^2+v^2}}$ | $d$ blur circle radius | Microscopy, X-ray |
| Noise mean | $\mu = \sum z_i p(z_i)$ | $p(z_i)$ PDF | Statistical characterization |
| Noise variance | $\sigma^2 = \sum (z_i - \mu)^2 p(z_i)$ | $\mu$ mean | Image quality metric |
| Estimated SNR | $\dfrac{\sum g^2}{\sum (g - \bar{g})^2}$ | homogeneous region $A$ | Quality assessment |
| Periodicity estimate | $N^p = \dfrac{MN}{\sum S_{uv}/K}$ | $S_{uv}$ spectral spikes | Detect power-line noise |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Spatial Model to Frequency Model

**Statement to prove:** Given $g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$, prove that the equivalent frequency-domain relation is $G(u,v) = H(u,v) F(u,v) + N(u,v)$.

**Step 1 — Write the convolution definition explicitly.**
The 2D linear convolution of the PSF with the image is:

$$g(x,y) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(a,b)\, f(x-a, y-b)\, da\, db + \eta(x,y)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 2D Fourier Transform to both sides.**
By definition:

$$G(u,v) = \mathcal{F}\{g(x,y)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(x,y)\, e^{-j2\pi(ux+vy)}\, dx\, dy$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Convolution Theorem of the 2D Fourier Transform.**
The Convolution Theorem states that convolution in the spatial domain corresponds to pointwise multiplication in the frequency domain:

$$\mathcal{F}\{h \ast f\} = H(u,v) \cdot F(u,v)$$

where $H(u,v) = \mathcal{F}\{h(x,y)\}$ and $F(u,v) = \mathcal{F}\{f(x,y)\}$.

**Step 4 — Apply linearity of the Fourier Transform to the additive noise term.**
Fourier Transform is linear, so:

$$\mathcal{F}\{\eta(x,y)\} = N(u,v)$$

**Step 5 — Combine the results.**
Substituting back:

$$\boxed{G(u,v) = H(u,v) F(u,v) + N(u,v)}$$

> **Logical interpretation:** The frequency domain representation is **multiplicative** for the signal and **additive** for the noise. This is the cornerstone of every frequency-domain restoration filter (Wiener, inverse, etc.) and earns a 7-mark KTU question.

## 3.2 Derivation: Motion Blur OTF in the Frequency Domain

**Problem:** Derive $H(u,v)$ for a uniform linear motion during exposure time $T$ in the direction $(a, b)/T$ (normalized displacement).

**Step 1 — Spatial PSF form.**
A point source moving uniformly during exposure smears its energy along a straight line of length $L = T\sqrt{a^2+b^2}$. The PSF is a 1D rectangular pulse:

$$h(x,y) = \frac{1}{L} \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{x}{L_x}, \frac{y}{L_y}\right)$$

The analytical form, written as a line integral over the path:

$$h(x,y) = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \delta\!\big(x - a t,\, y - b t\big)\, dt$$

where $\delta(\cdot)$ is the Dirac delta function (captures the moving point's location at each instant).

**Step 2 — Take the 2D Fourier Transform.**
Using the sifting property of the delta function and the substitution $\tau = t/T$:

$$H(u,v) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \delta(x-at, y-bt)\, e^{-j2\pi(ux+vy)} dt\, dx\, dy$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the inner double integral using the sifting property.**
The delta function picks out the value of the exponential at $x = at, y = bt$:

$$H(u,v) = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} e^{-j2\pi(uat + vbt)}\, dt$$

**Step 4 — Factor and integrate.**

$$H(u,v) = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} e^{-j2\pi(ua+vb)t}\, dt$$

**Step 5 — Perform the elementary integral.**

$$H(u,v) = \frac{1}{T} \left[ \frac{e^{-j2\pi(ua+vb)t}}{-j2\pi(ua+vb)} \right]_{0}^{T}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the boundary evaluation.**

$$H(u,v) = \frac{1}{T} \cdot \frac{e^{-j2\pi(ua+vb)T} - 1}{-j2\pi(ua+vb)}$$

**Step 7 — Factor out the average phase term using the half-angle identity.**
Let $\alpha = \pi(ua+vb)T$. Then $e^{-j2\alpha} - 1 = -2j e^{-j\alpha} \sin(\alpha)$:

$$H(u,v) = \frac{-2j\, e^{-j\alpha}\sin(\alpha)}{-j2\pi(ua+vb)T} = \frac{\sin(\alpha)}{\alpha} \cdot e^{-j\alpha}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute back the value of $\alpha$.**

$$\boxed{H(u,v) = \frac{T}{\pi(ua+vb)} \sin\!\big[\pi T(ua+vb)\big] \cdot e^{-j\pi T(ua+vb)}}$$

When $T$ is normalized such that $a, b$ are integer pixel displacements and $T$ is taken as 1:

$$H(u,v) = \frac{\sin\!\big[\pi(ua+vb)\big]}{\pi(ua+vb)} \cdot e^{-j\pi(ua+vb)}$$

> **Real-world utility:** The $\sin(x)/x$ (sinc) envelope explains the characteristic **"ringing"** in motion-blurred images, and the phase term $e^{-j\pi(ua+vb)}$ represents the **delay** caused by the mid-exposure position of the moving point.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mean and Variance of Selected Noise PDFs

### Case A — Gaussian Noise
**Mean derivation:**

$$\mu = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma} e^{-(z-\mu_0)^2 / 2\sigma^2}\, dz = \mu_0$$

**Variance derivation:**
Using the identity $\int z^2 p(z) dz = \sigma^2 + \mu^2$:

$$\sigma_N^2 = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}(z - \mu_0)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma} e^{-(z-\mu_0)^2/2\sigma^2}\, dz = \sigma^2$$

### Case B — Uniform Noise
**Mean derivation:**

$$\mu = \int_{a}^{b} z \cdot \frac{1}{b-a}\, dz = \frac{1}{b-a} \cdot \frac{z^2}{2}\bigg\vert_{a}^{b} = \frac{a+b}{2}$$

**Variance derivation:**

$$\sigma_N^2 = \int_{a}^{b} \left(z - \frac{a+b}{2}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{b-a}\, dz = \frac{(b-a)^2}{12}$$

### Case C — Rayleigh Noise
**Mean derivation** (using substitution $w = (z-a)^2/b$):

$$\mu = \int_{a}^{\infty} z \cdot \frac{2(z-a)}{b}\, e^{-(z-a)^2/b}\, dz = a + \sqrt{\frac{\pi b}{4}}$$

**Variance derivation** (standard result):

$$\sigma_N^2 = \frac{b(4-\pi)}{4}$$

### Case D — Erlang Noise
**Mean derivation** (using Gamma function identity $\int_0^\infty z^b e^{-az}dz = b!/a^{b+1}$):

$$\mu = \int_{0}^{\infty} z \cdot \frac{a^b z^{b-1}}{(b-1)!}\, e^{-az}\, dz = \frac{a^b}{(b-1)!} \cdot \frac{b!}{a^{b+1}} = \frac{b}{a}$$

**Variance derivation:**

$$\sigma_N^2 = \frac{b}{a^2}$$

## 3.4 Verification: Why the Frequency Model is Indispensable

> **Claim:** Restoration filters in the frequency domain are computationally faster than spatial-domain convolutions for large images.

**Reasoning:**
- A spatial convolution on an $N \times N$ image requires $O(N^4)$ operations.
- Using the FFT-based frequency model, the equivalent operation requires $O(N^2 \log N)$ operations.
- For $N = 1024$, $N^4 \approx 10^{12}$ vs. $N^2 \log N \approx 10^7$ — a **100,000× speedup**.

This is the *engineering reason* the KTU syllabus pushes students to master the $G = HF + N$ form.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Degradation-Restoration Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Image f of x,y] --> B[Atmospheric Effects]
    B --> C[Optical Lens System]
    C --> D[Sensor / Detector]
    D --> E[Analog to Digital Conversion]
    E --> F[Add Noise eta of x,y]
    F --> G[Degraded Image g of x,y]
    G --> H[Noise Estimation Module]
    H --> I[PSF Estimation Module]
    I --> J[Restoration Filter Algorithm]
    J --> K[Restored Image f hat of x,y]
    G -.->|Inverse Path| J
    H -.->|Feedback| J
    I -.->|Feedback| J
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Degradation Model Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Input Original Image f of x,y] --> A2[Convolution with PSF h of x,y]
    A2 --> A3[Spatial Domain Corrupted Image]
    A4[Noise Source eta of x,y] --> A5[Additive Junction Node]
    A3 --> A5
    A5 --> A6[Observed Degraded Image g of x,y]
    A6 --> A7[2D Fourier Transform to Frequency Domain]
    A7 --> A8[Frequency Domain G of u,v]
    A8 --> A9[Apply Restoration Filter in Frequency Domain]
    A9 --> A10[Inverse 2D Fourier Transform]
    A10 --> A11[Restored Image f hat of x,y]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Noise Model Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N0[Noise Source] --> N1[Distribution Driven]
    N0 --> N2[Physical Phenomenon Driven]
    N1 --> N1A[Gaussian]
    N1 --> N1B[Rayleigh]
    N1 --> N1C[Erlang Gamma]
    N1 --> N1D[Exponential]
    N1 --> N1E[Uniform]
    N1 --> N1F[Impulse Salt and Pepper]
    N2 --> N2A[Electronic Thermal Noise equals Gaussian]
    N2 --> N2B[Photon Shot Noise equals Poisson Approx Erlang]
    N2 --> N2C[Quantization Noise equals Uniform]
    N2 --> N2D[Transmission Errors equals Impulse]
```

## 4.4 Signal Flow for Frequency-Domain Restoration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[F of u,v Original Spectrum] --> F2[Multiplier with H of u,v]
    F2 --> F3[Product H times F]
    N1[N of u,v Noise Spectrum] --> F4[Adder Junction]
    F3 --> F4
    F4 --> F5[G of u,v Degraded Spectrum]
    F5 --> F6[Restoration Filter R of u,v]
    F6 --> F7[Estimated Spectrum F hat of u,v]
    F7 --> F8[Inverse FFT]
    F8 --> F9[Restored Image f hat of x,y]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** In the forward direction, $H$ multiplies $F$ and noise $N$ is added to produce $G$. In the inverse (restoration) direction, the filter $R$ (e.g., Wiener filter, inverse filter) operates on $G$ to recover an estimate $\hat{F}$. The quality of $\hat{F}$ depends on how well $H$ and $N$ are estimated.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**State and explain the general image degradation model. Mention the role of each term.**

**Model Answer:**

The general image degradation model is expressed in the spatial domain as:

$$g(x,y) = H[f(x,y)] + \eta(x,y)$$

- $f(x,y)$ — Original undegraded image (input).
- $H[\cdot]$ — Degradation operator (deterministic, often a linear system).
- $\eta(x,y)$ — Additive noise (random stochastic component).
- $g(x,y)$ — Observed degraded image (output).

When the operator is linear and shift-invariant, the model simplifies to $g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$, and in the frequency domain, $G(u,v) = H(u,v) F(u,v) + N(u,v)$. The model is essential for designing restoration filters. **(3 marks)**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**List any four standard noise probability density functions used in image degradation modeling.**

**Model Answer:**

The four standard noise PDFs are:
1. **Gaussian** — Models electronic/sensor noise with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$.
2. **Rayleigh** — Skewed distribution for range imaging and ultrasound.
3. **Erlang (Gamma)** — Models photon-counting statistics in low-light imaging.
4. **Impulse (Salt & Pepper)** — Models transmission errors and dead pixels.

(Other valid answers: Exponential, Uniform.) **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Internal Choice Pattern:** Each question has two parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The student is expected to attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

#### **(a) Derive the general image degradation model in both spatial and frequency domains. Discuss the assumptions made in deriving the frequency-domain form. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the spatial domain model.**
The general degradation model is:

$$g(x,y) = H[f(x,y)] + \eta(x,y) \qquad (1)$$

**Step 2 — Assume linearity and shift-invariance.**
Under the assumptions of linearity and position-invariance, the operator $H$ can be replaced by a convolution:

$$g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y) \qquad (2)$$

where $h(x,y)$ is the Point Spread Function (PSF) of the imaging system.

**Step 3 — Take the 2D Fourier Transform.**
Applying $\mathcal{F}\{\cdot\}$ to both sides of (2):

$$G(u,v) = H(u,v) F(u,v) + N(u,v) \qquad (3)$$

**Step 4 — Discuss the assumptions.**
- **Linearity:** $H[af_1 + bf_2] = aH[f_1] + bH[f_2]$. This enables use of superposition.
- **Shift-Invariance:** $H[f(x-a, y-b)] = g(x-a, y-b)$. This makes the PSF position-independent.
- **Additive noise assumption:** $\eta$ is zero-mean and statistically independent of the signal.

**Conclusion:** Equation (3) is the cornerstone of frequency-domain restoration. (3 + 2 + 2 = 7 marks)

#### **(b) With suitable expressions, explain the atmospheric turbulence and motion-blur degradation models. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Atmospheric Turbulence Model:**

$$H(u,v) = e^{-k\left(u^2 + v^2\right)^{5/6}}$$

- $k$ is a positive constant related to atmospheric conditions.
- Causes long-exposure, long-distance blur (telescope, aerial photography).
- The $5/6$ exponent is empirically derived from Kolmogorov turbulence theory.

**Motion Blur Model:**

$$H(u,v) = \frac{T \sin\!\big[\pi(ua+vb)\big]}{\pi(ua+vb)} \cdot e^{-j\pi(ua+vb)}$$

- $T$ = total exposure time; $(a,b)$ = components of uniform velocity.
- The sinc envelope causes periodic "zero-crossings" → ring artifacts in the spectrum.
- The exponential factor is a phase term that shifts the point spread along the motion direction.

**Comparison Table (for full marks):**

| Feature | Turbulence | Motion Blur |
|---|---|---|
| PSF shape | Gaussian-like | Sinc line |
| Cause | Refractive index fluctuations | Relative linear motion |
| Severity | Increases with path length | Increases with exposure $T$ |
| Medical relevance | Endoscopic/airborne | Cardiac/breath MRI motion |

(3.5 + 3.5 = 7 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

#### **(a) Define and derive the mean and variance for the Gaussian and Rayleigh noise probability density functions. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Gaussian PDF:**

$$p(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma} e^{-(z-\mu)^2 / 2\sigma^2}, \quad -\infty < z < \infty$$

**Mean derivation:**

$$\mu_G = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z \cdot p(z)\, dz = \mu$$

(Standard result; uses symmetry of the Gaussian about $z = \mu$.)

**Variance derivation:**

$$\sigma_G^2 = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (z-\mu)^2 p(z)\, dz = \sigma^2$$

**Rayleigh PDF:**

$$p(z) = \frac{2(z-a)}{b} e^{-(z-a)^2 / b}, \quad z \geq a$$

**Mean derivation:**
Using $w = (z-a)^2/b$, $dw = 2(z-a)/b\, dz$:

$$\mu_R = \int_{a}^{\infty} z \cdot \frac{2(z-a)}{b} e^{-(z-a)^2/b}\, dz = a + \int_{0}^{\infty} \sqrt{bw}\, e^{-w}\, dw = a + \sqrt{\pi b / 4}$$

**Variance derivation:**

$$\sigma_R^2 = \frac{b(4-\pi)}{4}$$

(3.5 marks for Gaussian, 3.5 marks for Rayleigh = 7 marks total)

#### **(b) Explain how the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) of a degraded image is estimated from a homogeneous sub-region. Derive the expression used. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of SNR.**
SNR is the ratio of signal power to noise power in the image.

**Step 2 — Identify a homogeneous sub-region $A$ of the degraded image $g(x,y)$.**
A homogeneous region has approximately constant intensity $\bar{g}$.

**Step 3 — Compute the local mean.**

$$\bar{g} = \frac{1}{|A|} \sum_{(x,y)\in A} g(x,y)$$

**Step 4 — Write the SNR formula.**

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{(x,y)\in A} g(x,y)^2}{\displaystyle\sum_{(x,y)\in A} \big[g(x,y) - \bar{g}\big]^2}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
- **Numerator:** approximates the signal energy (mean squared value).
- **Denominator:** approximates the noise variance about the local mean.
- High SNR ($\gg 1$) → clean image. Low SNR ($\approx 1$) → heavily noisy.

**Step 6 — Practical remark.**
In medical imaging, an SNR $< 2$ in a diagnostic region is generally considered inadequate for clinical decision-making. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in "Modelling Image Degradation" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the noise term** — When writing $g = h \ast f + \eta$, students often drop $\eta$ in the frequency model. **Always retain $N(u,v)$** — it is worth 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Confusing PSF and OTF** — The PSF is in the spatial domain, the OTF is its Fourier transform. Mixing them up in a 7-mark derivation loses 3 marks instantly.
>
> 3. **Using "deconvolution" instead of "restoration"** — Deconvolution is a *technique*; restoration is the *goal*. Examiners deduct marks for imprecise terminology.
>
> 4. **Skipping the $5/6$ exponent in turbulence** — Writing $e^{-k(u^2+v^2)}$ instead of $e^{-k(u^2+v^2)^{5/6}}$ is a full 1-mark loss and shows incomplete understanding.
>
> 5. **Not specifying range of $z$** — For noise PDFs, the valid range (e.g., $z \geq 0$ for Rayleigh) is mandatory. Omitting it costs 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Confusing Gaussian and Erlang** — Erlang requires integer $b$ and a Gamma function form. Don't write it as $e^{-(z-\mu)^2}$ — that is Gaussian!
>
> 7. **SNR formula units** — SNR is dimensionless. If you write $\sum g^2$ without specifying "per pixel" or normalizing, the examiner will not penalize, but writing it as "intensity²" without mentioning the ratio wastes a sentence.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Bullet-Point Density for Last-Minute Review)**

- [x] **Master Formula:** $g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$ and $G(u,v) = H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v)$.
- [x] **Linearity + Shift-Invariance** is the key assumption enabling the convolution form.
- [x] **Three classical OTFs to memorize:** Turbulence ($e^{-k(\cdot)^{5/6}}$), Motion (sinc envelope), Defocus (Bessel $J_1$ ratio).
- [x] **Six noise PDFs:** Gaussian, Rayleigh, Erlang, Exponential, Uniform, Impulse.
- [x] **Rayleigh mean:** $a + \sqrt{\pi b/4}$; **variance:** $b(4-\pi)/4$.
- [x] **Erlang mean:** $b/a$; **variance:** $b/a^2$.
- [x] **Uniform mean:** $(a+b)/2$; **variance:** $(b-a)^2/12$.
- [x] **Gaussian mean:** $\mu$; **variance:** $\sigma^2$.
- [x] **Exponential mean:** $1/a$; **variance:** $1/a^2$.
- [x] **Salt-and-Pepper PDF:** discrete at two values $a, b$ with probabilities $P_a, P_b$.
- [x] **SNR estimator:** use a small homogeneous region; ratio of squared intensity to variance about the local mean.
- [x] **Periodicity estimator** $N^p$ uses the periodogram $\vert N(u,v)\vert^2$ to detect periodic noise spikes.
- [x] **Medical relevance:** motion blur in MRI, Gaussian noise in CT detectors, Poisson/shot noise in X-ray, speckle (Rayleigh-like) in ultrasound.
- [x] **Engineering utility:** the forward model is needed to design inverse (restoration) filters — Wiener, inverse, constrained least squares.
