# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "image feature" with two examples in the context of medical imaging. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

> An *image feature* in medical image processing is a distinctive, locally measurable attribute of a digital medical image (CT, MRI, X-ray, ultrasound) that carries diagnostically meaningful information and enables automated analysis tasks such as segmentation, registration, and classification.
>
> **Two Examples:**
>
> 1. **Edge feature** — a local intensity discontinuity, e.g., the boundary of the liver in a CT slice, typically detected by Sobel or Canny operators.
> 2. **Texture feature** — a statistical descriptor of spatial gray-level variation, e.g., GLCM contrast computed inside a tumor ROI in MRI to grade malignancy.

---

### Question 2: Briefly explain the principle of the Harris corner detector. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

> The Harris corner detector identifies points in an image where intensity changes significantly in *more than one direction*. It uses the **second-moment (structure) tensor** $M$ computed over a local window of gradient products $I_x^2, I_y^2, I_x I_y$. The corner response is:
>
> $$ R = \det(M) - k \, \text{trace}(M)^2 $$
>
> A large positive $R$ indicates a **corner**, a small $R$ indicates a **flat region**, and a large negative $R$ indicates an **edge**. In medical imaging, Harris corners are widely used as landmarks for **multi-modal image registration** (e.g., aligning pre-operative MRI with intra-operative ultrasound).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** In Part B, Module 4 questions, students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are provided below. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks): Edge, Corner, and Texture Features

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Edge Detection with Canny

> **Q:** With a neat block diagram, explain the Canny edge detection algorithm. Discuss why it is preferred over simple gradient thresholding in medical image analysis. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the problem:** Simple gradient thresholding produces thick, broken edges that are sensitive to noise — unacceptable in medical imaging where boundary continuity is critical.

**Step 2 — Canny Block Diagram:** (refer Section 4.5)

**Step 3 — Detailed Stages:**

1. **Gaussian Smoothing** with kernel $G_\sigma$:
   $$ I_s(x,y) = G_\sigma(x,y) \ast I(x,y) $$
   This suppresses noise below the chosen scale $\sigma$.

2. **Gradient Computation** using Sobel kernels:
   $$ G_x, \, G_y \Rightarrow \vert \nabla I \vert = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}, \quad \theta = \arctan(G_y/G_x) $$

3. **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS):** For each pixel, keep the gradient magnitude only if it is a local maximum along the gradient direction. This thins edges to one-pixel width.

4. **Hysteresis Thresholding** with two thresholds $T_{\text{low}}$ and $T_{\text{high}}$:
   - Pixels with $\vert \nabla I \vert > T_{\text{high}}$ → **strong edges**.
   - Pixels with $T_{\text{low}} < \vert \nabla I \vert < T_{\text{high}}$ → **weak edges** (kept only if connected to strong edges).
   - All others → suppressed.

**Step 4 — Why preferred in medical imaging:**

- **Good detection:** Low false-negative rate.
- **Good localization:** Edges lie close to true boundaries.
- **Single response:** Only one edge per true boundary.
- **Noise robustness:** Gaussian pre-smoothing + hysteresis eliminate spurious responses due to imaging noise.

**Step 5 — Medical Example:** Detecting the pleural boundary in chest X-rays or the cortical outline in dental radiographs.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: GLCM Texture Features

> **Q:** Define the Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM). Derive the formulas for **Contrast** and **Homogeneity** features. State two medical imaging applications. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of GLCM:** The GLCM $P(i, j; d, \theta)$ is a square matrix whose $(i,j)$-entry is the number of times a pixel with gray level $i$ is adjacent (at distance $d$, angle $\theta$) to a pixel with gray level $j$, in the given ROI. The matrix is then normalized: $p(i,j) = P(i,j) / \sum P$, so that $\sum p = 1$.

**Step 2 — Contrast derivation:**

$$ \text{Contrast} = \sum_i \sum_j (i - j)^2 \, p(i,j) $$

This penalizes gray-level pairs that differ strongly. A perfectly uniform image has contrast 0; a high-contrast image has large contrast values.

**Step 3 — Homogeneity derivation:**

$$ \text{Homogeneity} = \sum_i \sum_j \frac{p(i,j)}{1 + (i - j)^2} $$

The denominator heavily weights the diagonal ($i = j$), so homogeneity is high when most adjacent pixels share similar gray levels.

**Step 4 — Medical Applications:**

1. **Brain tumor grading in MRI:** Malignant gliomas show higher GLCM contrast and entropy than benign lesions due to heterogeneous cell density.
2. **Lung tissue classification in HRCT:** Emphysema exhibits different GLCM statistics from interstitial lung disease, enabling automated CAD.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks): Blob, SIFT, and Shape Features

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Scale-Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT)

> **Q:** Explain the major stages of the SIFT algorithm. Discuss why SIFT features are useful in medical image registration. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Motivation:** Medical images are acquired at varying scales (e.g., CT slice thickness), orientations, and with intensity distortions. Registration algorithms need features that are invariant to these transformations.

**Step 2 — Stage 1: Scale-Space Extrema Detection**

Construct a Gaussian scale-space $L(x, y, \sigma) = G_\sigma \ast I$. Compute DoG:

$$ D(x, y, \sigma) = L(x, y, k\sigma) - L(x, y, \sigma) \approx (k-1)\sigma^2 \nabla^2 G_\sigma \ast I $$

Detect local extrema in $D(x, y, \sigma)$ across scales by comparing each point to its 26 neighbors (8 in the same scale + 9 above + 9 below).

**Step 3 — Stage 2: Keypoint Localization**

Fit a 3D quadratic to $D$ to refine keypoint location to sub-pixel accuracy. Reject low-contrast keypoints (sensitive to noise) and those lying on edges (using Hessian ratio test, analogous to Harris).

**Step 4 — Stage 3: Orientation Assignment**

For each keypoint, compute a histogram of gradient orientations weighted by magnitude in a neighborhood. The peak gives a canonical orientation, making the descriptor rotation-invariant.

$$ \theta_{\text{keypoint}} = \arg\max_{\theta} \, H(\theta) $$

**Step 5 — Stage 4: Descriptor Construction**

Build a $4 \times 4$ spatial grid of 8-bin orientation histograms around the keypoint, giving a $4 \times 4 \times 8 = 128$-dimensional descriptor, normalized to unit length.

**Step 6 — Use in medical image registration:** SIFT matches are robust to scale, rotation, and illumination differences, enabling landmark-based registration of pre-operative and intra-operative images (e.g., MRI to ultrasound in neurosurgery).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Hu Moment Invariants

> **Q:** Define geometric moments and central moments. Derive the first two Hu moment invariants. Why are they called "invariants"? Give a medical example. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometric moments:**

$$ M_{pq} = \sum_x \sum_y x^p y^q \, I(x,y) $$

$M_{00}$ is the area (sum of pixel intensities for a binary image), $M_{10}/M_{00}$ and $M_{01}/M_{00}$ give the centroid.

**Step 2 — Central moments (translation invariant):**

$$ \mu_{pq} = \sum_x \sum_y (x - \bar{x})^p (y - \bar{y})^q \, I(x,y) $$

These are unchanged when the object is translated, because the centroid $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ is used as the reference.

**Step 3 — Normalized central moments (scale invariant):**

$$ \eta_{pq} = \frac{\mu_{pq}}{\mu_{00}^{\,1 + (p+q)/2}} $$

The denominator $\mu_{00}^{(2+p+q)/2}$ exactly compensates the area scaling under uniform scale changes.

**Step 4 — Hu's first two invariants:**

$$ \phi_1 = \eta_{20} + \eta_{02} $$

$$ \phi_2 = (\eta_{20} - \eta_{02})^2 + 4\eta_{11}^2 $$

**Step 5 — Why "invariants"?** Hu (1962) algebraically constructed seven nonlinear combinations of $\eta_{pq}$ that remain constant under **translation** (because $\eta_{pq}$ uses central moments), **scale** (because of $\mu_{00}$ normalization), and **rotation** (because the combinations cancel the cross-terms introduced by rotation).

**Step 6 — Medical Example:** In **mammographic mass classification**, the seven Hu moments are computed for the segmented lesion contour. Round, lobulated, and spiculated masses produce distinct Hu signatures, enabling a CADx system to flag malignant cases.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in KTU 2024 Module 4 Feature Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing detector vs descriptor** — Harris *detects* keypoints, SIFT *describes* them. Mixing up these two terms in an exam costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting normalization in GLCM** — Many students write $P(i,j)$ in formulas but omit that $p(i,j) = P(i,j)/\sum P$. Examiners explicitly deduct for this.
> 3. **Writing formulas without units / interpretation** — KTU 2024 OBE rewards the "physical interpretation" line. Always end with one sentence linking the feature to a medical application.
> 4. **Skipping derivation steps in Hu moments** — A common 7-mark answer incorrectly jumps from $M_{pq}$ to $\phi_1$ without showing normalization. Show every step.
> 5. **No block diagram for Canny / SIFT** — These are visual questions; an answer without a diagram caps at ~60% marks.
> 6. **Confusing GLCM Contrast with Image Contrast** — Image contrast is dynamic range $I_{\max} - I_{\min}$; GLCM contrast is a *spatial* second-moment statistic. Mixing them up is a frequent error.
> 7. **Writing $kx$ for $|x|$ in GLCM** — Always write $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$. This is a common LaTeX error that examiners interpret as a formula mistake.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Features: Last-Minute Revision Bullets**
>
> ✅ **Feature** = a measurable, locally descriptive image attribute used for detection, classification, segmentation, or registration.
>
> ✅ **Edge features:** Sobel, Prewitt, Laplacian, Canny. Canny = Gaussian smoothing + Sobel + NMS + hysteresis. Best for organ boundaries.
>
> ✅ **Canny optimality criteria:** Good detection, good localization, single response.
>
> ✅ **Corner features:** Harris uses $R = \det(M) - k \cdot \text{trace}(M)^2$; Shi-Tomasi uses $\min(\lambda_1, \lambda_2)$.
>
> ✅ **Scale-space representation:** $L(x, y, \sigma) = G_\sigma \ast I$. As $\sigma$ increases, finer details vanish.
>
> ✅ **Blob features:** LoG (zero-crossings for edges, max for blobs); DoG (efficient LoG approximation) used in SIFT.
>
> ✅ **SIFT stages:** (1) Scale-space extrema → (2) Localization → (3) Orientation → (4) 128-D descriptor.
>
> ✅ **SURF:** Hessian-based detector, 64-D descriptor, faster than SIFT, used in real-time surgical guidance.
>
> ✅ **GLCM** is defined for (distance $d$, angle $\theta$). Always **normalize** before computing features.
>
> ✅ **Haralick features (must memorize):** Energy (ASM), Contrast, Correlation, Homogeneity (IDM), Entropy. Each has a distinct medical interpretation.
>
> ✅ **Moments:** $M_{pq} \rightarrow \mu_{pq}$ (translation invariant) $\rightarrow \eta_{pq}$ (scale invariant) $\rightarrow \phi_1 \ldots \phi_7$ (rotation invariant — Hu moments).
>
> ✅ **LBP$_{P,R}$** is a local texture descriptor; uniform LBP is rotation-invariant. Used in skin lesion and mammography classification.
>
> ✅ **Feature selection matrix:** Boundaries → edges, Points → corners, Regions → blobs, Internal pattern → texture, Whole shape → moments.
>
> ✅ **Pipeline:** Preprocess → Detect → Describe → Classify/Match.
>
> ✅ **Examiner loves:** (1) Block diagrams, (2) one-line medical interpretation, (3) pairing of feature type with clinical task.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Describing the Contents and Shape of Objects; Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT833 - MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Describing the Contents and Shape of Objects; Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Object Description** is the process of extracting a compact, mathematically tractable set of numerical attributes (called **features** or **descriptors**) from a segmented region of interest (ROI) in a medical image so that the object can be uniquely characterized, compared, indexed, and categorized.

In Medical Image Processing, once an object (e.g., a tumor, lesion, organ, or cell) has been **segmented** from the surrounding tissue, we must convert the raw pixel data into a meaningful, low-dimensional representation. This transformation is achieved through two complementary families of descriptors:

1. **Shape Descriptors** — Capture the *geometric*, *morphological*, and *boundary* characteristics of the object (size, roundness, irregularity, elongation).
2. **Content (Texture/Intensity) Descriptors** — Capture the *internal statistical distribution*, *texture patterns*, and *gray-level arrangement* inside the object.

Together, these descriptors form the **feature vector** $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$ that feeds a **Classifier** to assign the object to a diagnostic category (e.g., *Benign* vs *Malignant*).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Describing a Person for an ID Card":**
> Imagine you meet a stranger and must describe them so a friend recognizes them later. You wouldn't say *"there are 2,847,302 pixels of them"* — that is meaningless. Instead, you'd say:
> - **Shape/Form** → "Tall, thin, oval face" (this is a *shape descriptor*).
> - **Contents/Texture** → "Wearing a blue checkered shirt, pale skin" (this is a *content descriptor*).
>
> Now, to *file* this person into a category (Doctor, Engineer, Artist), you'd hand the description to a classifier — the classifier is like a **receptionist at a club** who, based on your description, points the person to the right room.

In medical imaging, a radiologist's report ("a 2 cm irregular hypoechoic mass with heterogeneous internal echoes") is essentially a **verbal feature vector**. Automated CAD (Computer-Aided Diagnosis) systems formalize this same intuition mathematically.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Pixel spacing** → typically **0.5 mm to 1.0 mm** in clinical CT/MRI.
- **Hu Moments (7 invariants)** → dimensionless, scale/rotation/translation invariant shape numbers.
- **Compactness (Circularity)** → ideal circle = **1.0**, all other shapes $\geq \frac{4\pi}{P^2}$.
- **GLCM (Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix)** → second-order statistical texture descriptor.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Shape Descriptors — Two Philosophical Schools

Object shape description splits naturally into two complementary approaches:

#### A. Boundary-Based (Contour) Descriptors

These describe the object by tracing its outer edge. They are highly sensitive to segmentation noise but excel at capturing fine shape irregularities (critical for distinguishing malignant vs benign tumors).

| Technique | Mathematical Object | Strength |
|-----------|--------------------|----------|
| **Chain Codes** | 8-directional encoded sequence | Compact boundary storage |
| **Signature** | $r(\theta)$ distance function | Translation invariant |
| **Fourier Descriptors** | DFT of complex boundary signal | Noise robust, scale/rotation invariant |
| **Curvature** | $\kappa(s)$ along arc length $s$ | Detects sharp boundary turns |

#### B. Region-Based (Global) Descriptors

These describe the object by integrating information over its **entire interior pixel set**.

| Descriptor | Formula (intuitive form) | Diagnostic Meaning |
|-----------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| **Area** | $A = \sum_{(x,y)\in R} 1$ | Lesion size |
| **Perimeter** | $P = \sum$ boundary pixels | Irregularity proxy |
| **Compactness** | $C = \frac{P^2}{A}$ (or $\frac{4\pi A}{P^2}$) | Roundness; **lower = more irregular** |
| **Eccentricity** | Ratio of major to minor axis | Elongation |
| **Moments** | $m_{pq} = \sum_x \sum_y x^p y^q I(x,y)$ | Statistical shape signature |
| **Hu Moments** | 7 invariant combinations of $m_{pq}$ | Robust shape fingerprint |

### 2.2 Content (Texture/Intensity) Descriptors

> [!NOTE]
> **Texture** = spatial arrangement of gray-level intensities. A benign cyst looks **homogeneous** (smooth texture); a malignant mass often looks **heterogeneous** (chaotic texture).

- **First-Order Statistics** — ignore spatial position; treat pixels as a histogram.
- **Second-Order Statistics (GLCM)** — model the joint probability $P(i,j \vert d, \theta)$ of pixel pairs at distance $d$ and angle $\theta$.

#### Standard GLCM Features

$$\text{Contrast} = \sum_{i,j} (i-j)^2 \, P(i,j)$$

$$\text{Energy (Angular Second Moment)} = \sum_{i,j} P(i,j)^2$$

$$\text{Homogeneity} = \sum_{i,j} \frac{P(i,j)}{1 + \vert i-j \vert}$$

$$\text{Entropy} = -\sum_{i,j} P(i,j) \log P(i,j)$$

$$\text{Correlation} = \sum_{i,j} \frac{(i-\mu_i)(j-\mu_j)P(i,j)}{\sigma_i \sigma_j}$$

### 2.3 Classification — The Decision Engine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification** = mapping a feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ to a discrete class label $y \in \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_K\}$ via a learned decision function $f(\mathbf{x}) \rightarrow y$.

#### The Two Paradigms

| Paradigm | How it Learns | Medical Examples |
|----------|---------------|------------------|
| **Supervised** | Uses *labeled* training data $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$ | Tumor benign/malignant diagnosis |
| **Unsupervised** | Finds hidden structure in *unlabeled* data | Discovering disease subtypes via clustering |

#### Canonical Classifiers in Medical Imaging

1. **Minimum Distance Classifier** — assigns $\mathbf{x}$ to the class with closest mean $\mu_k$ using Euclidean distance.
2. **k-Nearest Neighbour (k-NN)** — assigns $\mathbf{x}$ to the majority class of its $k$ closest training samples.
3. **Bayes Classifier (MAP)** — chooses class maximizing the posterior $P(C_k \vert \mathbf{x}) \propto P(\mathbf{x} \vert C_k) P(C_k)$.
4. **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** — finds the maximum-margin hyperplane separating classes.
5. **Artificial Neural Networks (ANN/Deep Learning)** — learns hierarchical non-linear mappings.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use $\vert$ instead of $\vert$ symbol if you rewrite these notes. The system prevented me from using vertical pipes in tables.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Area of ROI | $A = \sum_{(x,y) \in R} 1$ | Pixel count |
| 2 | Compactness (1) | $C_1 = \frac{P^2}{A}$ | $\geq 4\pi$ for non-overlapping boundary |
| 3 | Compactness (2, normalized) | $C_2 = \frac{4\pi A}{P^2}$ | $= 1$ for perfect circle |
| 4 | Eccentricity | $e = \frac{\text{major axis}}{\text{minor axis}}$ | $\geq 1$ |
| 5 | Raw 2-D Moment | $m_{pq} = \sum_x \sum_y x^p y^q I(x,y)$ | Order $= p+q$ |
| 6 | Central Moment | $\mu_{pq} = \sum_x \sum_y (x-\bar{x})^p (y-\bar{y})^q I(x,y)$ | Translation invariant |
| 7 | Normalized Central Moment | $\eta_{pq} = \frac{\mu_{pq}}{\mu_{00}^{\gamma}}$ where $\gamma = \frac{p+q}{2}+1$ | Scale invariant |
| 8 | GLCM Contrast | $\sum_{i,j} (i-j)^2 P(i,j)$ | High for heterogeneous textures |
| 9 | GLCM Energy | $\sum_{i,j} P(i,j)^2$ | High for uniform textures |
| 10 | Bayes Decision | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_k \, P(C_k) P(\mathbf{x} \vert C_k)$ | MAP rule |
| 11 | Euclidean Distance | $d(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_i (x_i - y_i)^2}$ | k-NN, Min-Distance |
| 12 | SVM Hyperplane | $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | Maximum margin separator |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Radiomics**: Extracts hundreds of shape and texture descriptors from CT/MRI for cancer prognosis.
- **Histopathology CAD**: Classifies cells as normal/benign/malignant using Haralick texture features.
- **Brain MRI Analysis**: Uses Hu moments + SVM to detect Alzheimer's disease from hippocampal atrophy.
- **Cell Biology**: Quantifies cell morphology for drug-response studies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Compactness / Circularity Measure

We want a **shape ratio** that equals **1** for a perfect circle and is **greater than 1** for every other closed shape. The classical derivation proceeds from the **isoperimetric inequality**.

**Step 1 — Start from the known isoperimetric inequality.**
For any closed planar region with perimeter $P$ and area $A$:

$$4\pi A \leq P^2$$

Equality holds **only** for a circle.

**Step 2 — Solve the inequality for the dimensionless ratio.**
Rearranging gives the *non-normalized* compactness:

$$C_1 = \frac{P^2}{A} \geq 4\pi$$

For a circle of radius $r$: $P = 2\pi r$, $A = \pi r^2$, so $C_1 = \frac{(2\pi r)^2}{\pi r^2} = 4\pi$.

**Step 3 — Normalize so the perfect circle equals 1.**
Dividing $4\pi A$ by $P^2$ yields the **inverse compactness** (also called *circularity* or *roundness*):

$$C_2 = \frac{4\pi A}{P^2} \leq 1$$

For a perfect circle: $C_2 = 1$. For a line (degenerate region): $C_2 \to 0$.

**Step 4 — Discrete pixel-domain implementation.**

$$A = \sum_{(x,y) \in R} 1$$

$$P \approx \sum_{(x,y) \in \text{boundary}(R)} 1$$

**Resulting engineering formula:**

$$\boxed{C_2 = \frac{4\pi \cdot \text{(number of pixels inside ROI)}}{(\text{number of boundary pixels})^2}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation — Hu's Seven Invariant Moments

Hu (1962) showed that seven non-linear combinations of central moments are invariant to translation, scale, and rotation. The derivation proceeds in three stages.

**Stage 1 — Raw moments (geometric moments).**

$$m_{pq} = \sum_{x} \sum_{y} x^p y^q I(x,y) \quad \text{for } p,q = 0,1,2,\dots$$

**Stage 2 — Central moments (translation invariant).**
First, compute the centroid $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{m_{10}}{m_{00}}, \qquad \bar{y} = \frac{m_{01}}{m_{00}}$$

Then:

$$\mu_{pq} = \sum_{x} \sum_{y} (x - \bar{x})^p (y - \bar{y})^q I(x,y)$$

**Stage 3 — Normalized central moments (scale invariant).**

$$\eta_{pq} = \frac{\mu_{pq}}{\mu_{00}^{\gamma}}, \qquad \gamma = \frac{p+q}{2} + 1$$

**Stage 4 — Hu's seven invariants.**

$$\phi_1 = \eta_{20} + \eta_{02}$$

$$\phi_2 = (\eta_{20} - \eta_{02})^2 + 4\eta_{11}^2$$

$$\phi_3 = (\eta_{30} - 3\eta_{12})^2 + (3\eta_{21} - \eta_{03})^2$$

$$\phi_4 = (\eta_{30} + \eta_{12})^2 + (\eta_{21} + \eta_{03})^2$$

$$\phi_5 = (\eta_{30} - 3\eta_{12})(\eta_{30}+\eta_{12})\left[(\eta_{30}+\eta_{12})^2 - 3(\eta_{21}+\eta_{03})^2\right] + (3\eta_{21}-\eta_{03})(\eta_{21}+\eta_{03})\left[3(\eta_{30}+\eta_{12})^2 - (\eta_{21}+\eta_{03})^2\right]$$

$$\phi_6 = (\eta_{20}-\eta_{02})\left[(\eta_{30}+\eta_{12})^2 - (\eta_{21}+\eta_{03})^2\right] + 4\eta_{11}(\eta_{30}+\eta_{12})(\eta_{21}+\eta_{03})$$

$$\phi_7 = (3\eta_{21}-\eta_{03})(\eta_{30}+\eta_{12})\left[(\eta_{30}+\eta_{12})^2 - 3(\eta_{21}+\eta_{03})^2\right] - (\eta_{30}-3\eta_{12})(\eta_{21}+\eta_{03})\left[3(\eta_{30}+\eta_{12})^2 - (\eta_{21}+\eta_{03})^2\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Hu moments matter in medical imaging:** A tumor imaged at different scanner angles or resolutions still yields the *same* $\phi_1 \dots \phi_7$ values (in log-scale). This enables *radiomic fingerprinting* of lesions across hospitals.

### 3.3 Derivation — Bayes Minimum-Error Classifier

We seek the decision rule $f(\mathbf{x})$ that minimizes the probability of misclassification $P(\text{error})$.

**Step 1 — Define the conditional error given observation $\mathbf{x}$.**

$$P(\text{error} \vert \mathbf{x}) = 1 - P(C_k \vert \mathbf{x}) \quad \text{where } C_k = \arg\max_k P(C_k \vert \mathbf{x})$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes theorem.**

$$P(C_k \vert \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \vert C_k) P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

**Step 3 — Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ is constant across classes, drop it.**

$$\hat{y}(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_k \, P(\mathbf{x} \vert C_k) P(C_k)$$

**Step 4 — If Gaussian assumption is added ($\mathbf{x} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_k, \Sigma_k)$), the log-posterior becomes quadratic in $\mathbf{x}$, producing a *quadratic discriminant*:**

$$g_k(\mathbf{x}) = -\tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\mu_k)^T \Sigma_k^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\mu_k) - \tfrac{1}{2}\log \vert \Sigma_k \vert + \log P(C_k)$$

Choosing $\hat{y} = \arg\max_k g_k(\mathbf{x})$ minimizes the probability of classification error under the Gaussian model.

### 3.4 Derivation — k-NN Decision Rule

Given training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ and query $\mathbf{x}_q$:

**Step 1 — Compute distances to every training point.**

$$d_i = \lVert \mathbf{x}_q - \mathbf{x}_i \rVert_2$$

**Step 2 — Identify the $k$ smallest distances; let $\mathcal{N}_k$ be the set of their indices.**

**Step 3 — Vote by majority.**

$$\hat{y}(\mathbf{x}_q) = \arg\max_{c} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k} \mathbb{1}\{y_i = c\}$$

where $\mathbb{1}\{\cdot\}$ is the indicator function.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Feature Extraction & Classification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Medical Image CT or MRI] --> B[Pre-processing Noise Reduction Intensity Normalization]
    B --> C[Segmentation Thresholding or Region Growing or Deep Learning]
    C --> D[Object Region ROI Extracted]
    D --> E[Shape Descriptors Area Perimeter Compactness Hu Moments]
    D --> F[Content Descriptors First Order Stats GLCM Texture Features]
    E --> G[Feature Vector x with n dimensions]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Classifier Type}
    H --> I1[Supervised SVM or k-NN or ANN]
    H --> I2[Unsupervised K Means or Fuzzy C Means]
    I1 --> J[Diagnostic Label Benign or Malignant]
    I2 --> K[Cluster ID Subtype Discovery]
    J --> L[Clinical Report Decision Support]
    K --> L
```

### 4.2 Shape vs Content Descriptor Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Descriptor Family] --> B{What aspect of object matters most}
    B -- Boundary Geometry --> C[Shape Boundary Methods]
    B -- Internal Pixel Statistics --> D[Content Texture Methods]
    B -- Both --> E[Hybrid Feature Vector]
    C --> C1[Chain Codes]
    C --> C2[Fourier Descriptors]
    C --> C3[Curvature Functions]
    C --> C4[Hu Invariant Moments]
    D --> D1[Histogram Mean Variance Skewness]
    D --> D2[GLCM Contrast Energy Homogeneity]
    D --> D3[Run Length Matrix]
    D --> D4[Gabor Wavelets]
    E --> F[Concatenate into x then Classify]
```

### 4.3 Supervised vs Unsupervised Classification Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Labeled Training Data] --> S[Supervised Path]
    U[Unlabeled Data] --> N[Unsupervised Path]
    S --> S1[Learn Decision Boundary]
    S1 --> S2[SVM k-NN ANN Bayes]
    S2 --> S3[Predict Class Label]
    N --> N1[Discover Hidden Groups]
    N1 --> N2[K Means Hierarchical DBSCAN]
    N2 --> N3[Assign Cluster ID]
    S3 --> Out[Clinical Decision]
    N3 --> Out
```

### 4.4 GLCM Computation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input ROI Image] --> B[Quantize Gray Levels to Ng bins]
    B --> C[Define Offset d and theta]
    C --> D[Slide Pair Window across ROI]
    D --> E[Build Co occurrence Matrix P i j]
    E --> F1[Contrast]
    E --> F2[Energy]
    E --> F3[Homogeneity]
    E --> F4[Correlation]
    E --> F5[Entropy]
    F1 --> G[Feature Vector]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define object descriptors in medical image processing. Differentiate between shape descriptors and texture descriptors with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer:**

Object descriptors are numerical features extracted from a segmented region of interest to characterize it for comparison and classification.

**Shape descriptors** capture geometric/morphological properties of the object boundary or region. Examples include area, perimeter, compactness, eccentricity, and Hu's invariant moments. They answer the question *"What does the object look like?"*

**Texture (content) descriptors** capture the spatial distribution of gray-level intensities within the object. Examples include first-order statistics (mean, variance) and second-order GLCM features (contrast, energy, homogeneity, entropy). They answer *"What is inside the object?"*

> [!NOTE]
> Shape descriptors answer *form*; texture descriptors answer *content*.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is a Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM)? List any four features computed from it.*

**Model Answer:**

A GLCM is a **second-order statistical texture descriptor** that tabulates the joint frequency $P(i,j \vert d, \theta)$ of pixel pairs $(i, j)$ occurring at a specified spatial offset (distance $d$, angle $\theta$).

Four GLCM features:

1. **Contrast** — $\sum (i-j)^2 P(i,j)$; high for heterogeneous textures.
2. **Energy / Angular Second Moment** — $\sum P(i,j)^2$; high for uniform textures.
3. **Homogeneity / Inverse Difference Moment** — $\sum \frac{P(i,j)}{1+\vert i-j \vert}$; high when similar gray levels co-occur.
4. **Entropy** — $-\sum P(i,j) \log P(i,j)$; measures randomness; high for chaotic textures.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the procedure to compute Hu's seven invariant moments from a binary segmented medical image. Why are they called *invariant*? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the Bayes minimum-error classifier for medical image classification with mathematical formulation. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Hu's Seven Invariant Moments**

**Step 1 — Compute raw geometric moments.**
For a binary ROI image $I(x,y)$:

$$m_{pq} = \sum_x \sum_y x^p y^q I(x,y)$$

**Step 2 — Find the centroid $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ for translation invariance.**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{m_{10}}{m_{00}}, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{m_{01}}{m_{00}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute central moments (translation invariant).**

$$\mu_{pq} = \sum_x \sum_y (x - \bar{x})^p (y - \bar{y})^q I(x,y)$$

**Step 4 — Normalize by $\mu_{00}^{\gamma}$ to achieve scale invariance.**

$$\eta_{pq} = \frac{\mu_{pq}}{\mu_{00}^{\gamma}}, \quad \gamma = \frac{p+q}{2} + 1$$

**Step 5 — Form Hu's seven non-linear combinations** $\phi_1, \phi_2, \dots, \phi_7$ as derived in Section 3.2 above. These are invariant to translation, scale, and rotation.

**Why they are called *invariant***: Because each $\phi_i$ produces the same numerical value (up to a magnitude scaling for scale) regardless of where the object is positioned (translation), how big it is (scale), or at what angle it has been rotated.

**(b) Bayes Minimum-Error Classifier**

The classifier chooses the class $C_k$ that **maximizes the posterior probability** $P(C_k \vert \mathbf{x})$ of the class given the observed feature vector $\mathbf{x}$.

**Bayes theorem:**

$$P(C_k \vert \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \vert C_k) P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

**Decision rule:**

$$\hat{y}(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_k \, P(\mathbf{x} \vert C_k) P(C_k)$$

where $P(C_k)$ is the prior class probability (estimated from training data) and $P(\mathbf{x} \vert C_k)$ is the class-conditional density (likelihood).

For Gaussian classes $\mathbf{x} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_k, \Sigma_k)$, the decision function becomes:

$$g_k(\mathbf{x}) = -\tfrac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma_k \vert - \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\mu_k)^T \Sigma_k^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\mu_k) + \ln P(C_k)$$

The Bayes classifier is *optimal* in the sense of minimizing total probability of misclassification.

**Advantages in medical imaging**: probabilistic output (not just a label), handles asymmetric class costs (e.g., false negative cost $>$ false positive cost in cancer screening).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the various shape descriptors used in medical image processing. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the k-Nearest Neighbour (k-NN) classifier. Explain its working with an example suitable for medical image classification. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Shape Descriptors — Block Diagram and Explanation**

Shape descriptors fall into two categories:

**Boundary-based (Contour) Descriptors:**

1. **Chain Codes** — Encode the 8-directional transition between successive boundary pixels as a sequence of digits 0–7. Compact but rotation-sensitive.
2. **Boundary Segments** — Decompose the contour into straight segments via polygonal approximation.
3. **Signature** — Plot the distance $r(\theta)$ from centroid to boundary as a function of angle; translation-invariant.
4. **Fourier Descriptors** — Apply 1-D Discrete Fourier Transform to the complex boundary signal $z(k) = x(k) + jy(k)$; the resulting DFT coefficients $a(u)$ are descriptors. Magnitudes $\vert a(u) \vert$ are rotation/scale invariant.
5. **Curvature** — First and second derivatives of the boundary parametric form; identifies corners and bends.

**Region-based Descriptors:**

1. **Area $A$** — Number of pixels inside the ROI.
2. **Perimeter $P$** — Number of boundary pixels (4- or 8-connected).
3. **Compactness / Circularity** — $C = \frac{4\pi A}{P^2}$; $=1$ for a circle.
4. **Eccentricity** — Major axis / Minor axis of the best-fit ellipse.
5. **Solidity** — $\frac{A}{\text{Convex Hull Area}}$; quantifies boundary concavity.
6. **Moments** — $m_{pq}$, $\mu_{pq}$, $\eta_{pq}$, and Hu's invariants.

**Block Diagram** (see Mermaid Section 4.2 above).

**(b) k-Nearest Neighbour (k-NN) Classifier**

The k-NN classifier is a **lazy, non-parametric** supervised learner that classifies a query point $\mathbf{x}_q$ based on the majority class of its $k$ closest training samples in feature space.

**Algorithm**:

1. Compute Euclidean distance $d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i)$ to every training sample $\mathbf{x}_i$.
2. Sort distances and select the $k$ smallest.
3. Take a majority vote among the labels of these $k$ neighbours.
4. Assign $\mathbf{x}_q$ to the winning class.

**Mathematical form:**

$$\hat{y}(\mathbf{x}_q) = \arg\max_c \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k} \mathbb{1}\{y_i = c\}$$

**Example — Breast Cancer Classification**:
Given a feature vector $\mathbf{x} = [\text{area}, \text{compactness}, \text{contrast}]^T$ from a segmented mammogram mass, k-NN with $k=5$ would find the 5 most similar masses from a labeled training database and assign the new mass to whichever diagnosis (benign/malignant) is in the majority.

**Pros**: simple, no training phase, naturally multi-class. **Cons**: slow at inference, sensitive to feature scaling, sensitive to curse-of-dimensionality.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting normalization in moments** — computing $\mu_{pq}$ without dividing by $\mu_{00}^{\gamma}$ loses scale invariance → lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing compactness formula direction** — students often write $C = \frac{4\pi A}{P^2}$ and $\geq 1$; the correct form is $\leq 1$.
> 3. **GLCM is NOT symmetric by default** — must explicitly symmetrize by adding its transpose.
> 4. **In Bayes classifier**, do not drop the denominator $P(\mathbf{x})$ carelessly — note it is *constant across classes*, hence removable.
> 5. **k-NN majority tie-breaking** — for $k$ even and a tie, KTU expects you to mention using odd $k$ or distance-weighted voting.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Shape descriptors** quantify *geometry*; **content/texture descriptors** quantify *internal pixel patterns*.
- **Area = pixel count inside ROI**; **Perimeter = boundary pixel count**.
- **Compactness** $C = \frac{4\pi A}{P^2} \leq 1$ — equals 1 only for a perfect circle.
- **Eccentricity** = ratio of major to minor axis of best-fit ellipse ($\geq 1$).
- **Hu's 7 invariant moments** are translation, scale, and rotation invariant shape fingerprints built from normalized central moments.
- **GLCM features** (Contrast, Energy, Homogeneity, Correlation, Entropy) capture second-order texture statistics.
- **First-order stats** (mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis) ignore spatial arrangement; GLCM captures pairwise spatial relationships.
- **Classifiers** are either *supervised* (need labels — SVM, k-NN, ANN, Bayes) or *unsupervised* (no labels — k-means, hierarchical).
- **Bayes classifier** is optimal under known distributions; chooses class maximizing $P(C_k) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \vert C_k)$.
- **k-NN** is a lazy learner using Euclidean distance + majority vote; sensitive to feature scaling.
- **Hu moments are usually log-transformed** $\log \vert \phi_i \vert$ before classification to handle their huge dynamic range.
- **Feature vector concatenation** of shape + texture features improves diagnostic accuracy in radiomics pipelines.
- **Radiomics** is the modern clinical application: extracts hundreds of descriptors and feeds them to ML classifiers for prognosis prediction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — PATTERN RECOGNITION FUNDAMENTALS FOR MEDICAL IMAGING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Supervised Learning

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Supervised Learning* is a **paradigm of machine learning** in which a classification or regression model is trained using a **labelled dataset** — i.e., each input vector $\mathbf{x}_i$ is paired with a known target output (class label $y_i$ or continuous value). The objective is to learn a mapping function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ that **generalises** to unseen data by minimising a predefined loss function.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners):**
Imagine a medical student learning to identify a *benign* vs *malignant* tumour from mammogram images. The professor shows her **10,000 mammograms** and explicitly tells her — *"this one is benign, this one is malignant, this one is benign…"*. After seeing enough labelled examples, she builds an internal mental rule. When shown a *new* mammogram (without any label), she can classify it. That is **supervised learning** — learning *under the supervision* of pre-assigned ground-truth labels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlights:**
> - Requires **labelled training data** $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$
> - Two main task families: **Classification** (discrete labels) and **Regression** (continuous outputs)
> - In medical imaging: tumour detection, organ segmentation, disease grading, cell-type classification
> - Examples of algorithms: LDA, k-NN, SVM, Random Forest, CNNs

---

### 1.2 Unsupervised Learning

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Unsupervised Learning* is a machine learning paradigm wherein the algorithm is given **unlabelled data** and must discover **intrinsic structure, clusters, or low-dimensional representations** on its own — without any explicit supervisory signal. The objective is to model the underlying probability distribution $P(x)$ of the input space.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
The same medical student is now given **10,000 mammograms with NO labels** at all. No professor, no answers. She starts grouping them based on visual similarity — one pile of round, smooth masses, another pile of spiculated, irregular masses, another pile of microcalcifications. **She has discovered clusters** without any teacher. That is **unsupervised learning** — finding hidden structure in raw, unlabelled data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlights:**
> - Operates on **unlabelled** data $\{x_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$
> - Primary tasks: **Clustering**, **Dimensionality Reduction**, **Density Estimation**
> - In medical imaging: tissue segmentation, anomaly/novelty detection, feature extraction, patient stratification
> - Examples: K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical Clustering, PCA, Autoencoders

---

### 1.3 Discriminant Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Discriminant Analysis* is a **statistical pattern classification technique** that derives **linear (or quadratic) combinations of features** to maximally separate two or more classes. The most common form is **Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)**, introduced by **R. A. Fisher (1936)**, which projects the data onto a lower-dimensional space such that the ratio of *between-class variance* to *within-class variance* is maximised.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Suppose you have MRI scans of *healthy* and *Alzheimer's* brains. Each scan has 100 numerical features (intensity, volume, texture metrics, etc.). It is impossible to visualise a 100-dimensional space. **Discriminant Analysis** finds a *single direction* (a line) in this 100-D space such that when you project all brains onto this line, the *healthy* ones clump together on one side and the *Alzheimer's* ones clump on the other side, with maximum separation. You have converted 100 features into 1 super-feature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Fisher's LDA:**
> - Maximises the **Fisher Criterion**: $J(w) = \dfrac{w^T S_B w}{w^T S_W w}$
> - Solution: $w = S_W^{-1}(\mu_1 - \mu_2)$ (for 2-class case)
> - Reduces dimensionality while preserving class separability

---

### 1.4 k-Nearest Neighbour (k-NN) Classifier

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The *k-Nearest Neighbour (k-NN) classifier* is a **non-parametric, instance-based supervised learning algorithm** that classifies a query sample $\mathbf{x}_q$ by a **majority vote** of its *k* closest training samples in feature space, where "closeness" is defined by a distance metric (typically Euclidean).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A new patient walks into a hospital with symptoms $\{$fever, cough, fatigue$\}$. The doctor recalls the 5 most similar past patients in the hospital database (k = 5). Of those 5, **3 had flu, 1 had COVID, 1 had pneumonia**. The doctor diagnoses **flu** (the majority class). That is **k-NN** in action — *no model is built; the entire training set is memorised*, and prediction happens by looking at "neighbours" in feature space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Properties:**
> - **Lazy learner** — defers computation until query time
> - **Non-parametric** — no assumption about underlying data distribution
> - Sensitive to: choice of $k$, distance metric, feature scaling
> - In medical imaging: widely used in CAD (Computer-Aided Diagnosis) systems for lesion classification
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Supervised vs Unsupervised — Conceptual Comparison

| Aspect | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Type** | Labelled $(x_i, y_i)$ | Unlabelled $(x_i)$ |
| **Goal** | Learn $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ | Learn $P(x)$ or hidden structure |
| **Output** | Predicted label or value | Clusters / reduced dimensions |
| **Feedback** | Explicit error signal (loss) | No feedback / intrinsic only |
| **Computational Phase** | Train + Predict | Single phase (model discovery) |
| **Medical Example** | Tumour classification (benign/malignant) | Grouping patients by gene expression |
| **Algorithms** | LDA, k-NN, SVM, CNN | K-Means, PCA, Autoencoder |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, AUC-ROC | Silhouette Score, Davies-Bouldin Index |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this distinction matters in KTU exams:**
> Examiners frequently ask students to **contrast** these two paradigms and provide **medical-imaging examples** for each. Memorise the table above — it appears in nearly every Board paper.

---

## 2.2 Discriminant Analysis — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.2.1 Bayes Decision Theory (The Probabilistic Root)

For a $C$-class problem with class-conditional densities $P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i)$ and priors $P(\omega_i)$:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \ln P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

**Decision Rule:** Assign $\mathbf{x}$ to class $\omega_k$ if $g_k(\mathbf{x}) > g_j(\mathbf{x}) \quad \forall j \neq k$.

When $P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i)$ is **multivariate Gaussian** with class mean $\mu_i$ and **common covariance** $\Sigma$, the discriminant becomes **linear** in $\mathbf{x}$:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}_i^T \mathbf{x} + w_{i0}$$

This linear function gives the algorithm its name — **Linear Discriminant Analysis**.

### 2.2.2 Fisher's Criterion (Geometric View)

Fisher formulated the problem *geometrically* — find a projection direction $\mathbf{w}$ that maximises class separability:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T S_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^T S_W \mathbf{w}}$$

where:
- $S_B$ = **Between-class scatter matrix** (variance of class means around global mean)
- $S_W$ = **Within-class scatter matrix** (variance of samples around their own class mean)

The optimal $\mathbf{w}$ that maximises $J(\mathbf{w})$ is found by solving the **generalised eigenvalue problem**:

$$S_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda S_W \mathbf{w} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mathbf{w} = S_W^{-1} S_B$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Formula / Value** |
|---|---|---|
| $S_W$ | Within-class scatter matrix | $\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{C} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in \omega_i} (\mathbf{x} - \mu_i)(\mathbf{x} - \mu_i)^T$ |
| $S_B$ | Between-class scatter matrix | $\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{C} N_i (\mu_i - \bar{\mu})(\mu_i - \bar{\mu})^T$ |
| $S_T$ | Total scatter matrix | $S_T = S_W + S_B$ |
| $\mu_i$ | Mean of class $\omega_i$ | $\displaystyle\frac{1}{N_i} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in \omega_i} \mathbf{x}$ |
| $\bar{\mu}$ | Global (overall) mean | $\displaystyle\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{x}_i$ |
| $J(\mathbf{w})$ | Fisher's criterion | $\dfrac{\mathbf{w}^T S_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^T S_W \mathbf{w}}$ |
| $\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}}$ | Optimal LDA projection (2-class) | $S_W^{-1}(\mu_1 - \mu_2)$ |
| $d_E(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ | Euclidean distance | $\sqrt{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{D} (x_j - y_j)^2}$ |
| $d_M(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ | Manhattan distance | $\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{D} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ |
| $d_{\text{Mink}}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ | Minkowski distance (order $p$) | $\left(\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{D} \vert x_j - y_j \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ |
| $d_C(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ | Cosine distance | $1 - \dfrac{\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{y}}{\vert\vert\mathbf{x}\vert\vert \, \vert\vert\mathbf{y}\vert\vert}$ |
| $P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x})$ | Posterior probability | $\dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **LDA** is the backbone of classical *face recognition* (Belhumeur et al., 1997 — "Eigenfaces vs Fisherfaces") and is extensively used in **brain MRI classification** (e.g., Alzheimer's vs MCI vs HC).
> - **k-NN** is the simplest viable CAD system for *breast cancer Wisconsin dataset* classification, and serves as a **clinical decision-support baseline** in radiology.
> - Both algorithms appear in the FDA-approved pipeline for **diabetic retinopathy screening**.

---

## 2.4 k-NN Algorithm — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Choose $k$** — number of neighbours to consider (typically odd to avoid ties: $k = 1, 3, 5, 7, \ldots$).
2. **Choose distance metric** $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ — Euclidean is most common.
3. **For a query sample** $\mathbf{x}_q$:
   - Compute distance $d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i)$ to **every** training sample $\mathbf{x}_i$.
   - Sort distances in ascending order.
   - Identify the $k$ training samples with the **smallest** distances.
4. **Majority Voting:** Assign $\mathbf{x}_q$ to the class most frequently represented among the $k$ neighbours.
5. **Output:** Predicted class label $\hat{y}_q$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why odd values of $k$?**
> An odd $k$ guarantees a *unique* majority class in a 2-class problem, eliminating tie-breaking ambiguity. In multi-class problems, $k$ is often chosen as a multiple of the number of classes plus 1 (e.g., $k = 5$ for 3 classes).

### 2.4.1 Special Case: k = 1 (Nearest Neighbour)

When $k = 1$, the algorithm assigns $\mathbf{x}_q$ to the class of its **single closest** training sample. This produces a **Voronoi tessellation** of the feature space — each cell belongs to the class of its enclosing training point.

### 2.4.2 Weighted k-NN (Distance-Weighted Voting)

To mitigate the influence of distant neighbours:

$$\hat{y}_q = \arg\max_{c} \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot \mathbb{1}(y_i = c), \quad w_i = \frac{1}{d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i)^2}$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fisher Linear Discriminant

### 3.1.1 Setup: 2-Class Problem

We have $C = 2$ classes, $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$, with $N_1$ and $N_2$ samples respectively. Each sample is a $D$-dimensional feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^D$. We seek a projection vector $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^D$ such that the scalar projection $y = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}$ yields maximum class separation.

### 3.1.2 Defining the Class Means in Projected 1-D Space

The projected mean of class $\omega_i$ is:

$$\tilde{\mu}_i = \frac{1}{N_i} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in \omega_i} \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{w}^T \left( \frac{1}{N_i} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in \omega_i} \mathbf{x} \right) = \mathbf{w}^T \mu_i$$

The distance between projected means is:

$$\vert \tilde{\mu}_1 - \tilde{\mu}_2 \vert = \vert \mathbf{w}^T (\mu_1 - \mu_2) \vert$$

### 3.1.3 Defining the Within-Class Scatter in Projected Space

The within-class scatter (variance) of class $\omega_i$ after projection:

$$\tilde{s}_i^2 = \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in \omega_i} (y - \tilde{\mu}_i)^2 = \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in \omega_i} (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}^T \mu_i)^2$$

Expanding:

$$\tilde{s}_i^2 = \mathbf{w}^T \left[ \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in \omega_i} (\mathbf{x} - \mu_i)(\mathbf{x} - \mu_i)^T \right] \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^T S_i \mathbf{w}$$

Total within-class scatter (sum over both classes):

$$\tilde{S}_W = \tilde{s}_1^2 + \tilde{s}_2^2 = \mathbf{w}^T (S_1 + S_2) \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^T S_W \mathbf{w}$$

### 3.1.4 Constructing Fisher's Criterion

Fisher defined class separability as the **ratio** of *between-class separation* to *within-class scatter*:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\vert \tilde{\mu}_1 - \tilde{\mu}_2 \vert^2}{\tilde{S}_W}$$

Substituting our derived forms:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T S_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^T S_W \mathbf{w}}$$

where $S_B = (\mu_1 - \mu_2)(\mu_1 - \mu_2)^T$ for the 2-class case.

### 3.1.5 Maximising $J(\mathbf{w})$ — Lagrangian Approach

The numerator and denominator are both **quadratic** in $\mathbf{w}$, hence $J(\mathbf{w})$ is invariant to scaling of $\mathbf{w}$. We constrain $\mathbf{w}^T S_W \mathbf{w} = c$ (constant) and maximise the numerator.

Using the method of Lagrange multipliers with constraint $\mathbf{w}^T S_W \mathbf{w} = c$:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^T S_B \mathbf{w} - \lambda(\mathbf{w}^T S_W \mathbf{w} - c)$$

Differentiating with respect to $\mathbf{w}$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 2 S_B \mathbf{w} - 2 \lambda S_W \mathbf{w} = 0$$

$$S_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda S_W \mathbf{w}$$

This is the **generalised eigenvalue problem**.

### 3.1.6 Solving the Generalised Eigenvalue Problem

Substituting $S_B = (\mu_1 - \mu_2)(\mu_1 - \mu_2)^T$:

$$(\mu_1 - \mu_2)(\mu_1 - \mu_2)^T \mathbf{w} = \lambda S_W \mathbf{w}$$

Since $(\mu_1 - \mu_2)^T \mathbf{w}$ is a scalar, the term $(\mu_1 - \mu_2)(\mu_1 - \mu_2)^T \mathbf{w}$ collapses to:

$$(\mu_1 - \mu_2) \cdot \alpha = \lambda S_W \mathbf{w} \quad \text{where } \alpha = (\mu_1 - \mu_2)^T \mathbf{w}$$

Rearranging:

$$\mathbf{w} = \frac{\alpha}{\lambda} S_W^{-1}(\mu_1 - \mu_2)$$

Since $\alpha/\lambda$ is just an arbitrary non-zero scalar, we conclude:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{w} = S_W^{-1}(\mu_1 - \mu_2)}$$

This is the **optimal LDA projection direction** for the 2-class case. For $C > 2$ classes, LDA yields $C - 1$ such projection directions, forming the columns of a projection matrix $W \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times (C-1)}$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — k-NN Classification

### Problem Setup

A hospital dataset contains **5 training patients** characterised by **2 features** (e.g., *lesion density* in HU and *lesion size* in mm). Labels: **0 = Benign, 1 = Malignant**.

| Patient | $x_1$ (Density) | $x_2$ (Size) | Class $y$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 1.0 | 1.0 | 0 |
| P2 | 1.5 | 1.5 | 0 |
| P3 | 2.0 | 2.0 | 0 |
| P4 | 3.0 | 3.5 | 1 |
| P5 | 3.5 | 4.0 | 1 |

**Query Patient Q:** $\mathbf{x}_q = (2.5, 2.5)$, **$k = 3$**, **Distance metric: Euclidean**.

### Step 1 — Compute Euclidean Distances

$$d(Q, P1) = \sqrt{(2.5 - 1.0)^2 + (2.5 - 1.0)^2} = \sqrt{2.25 + 2.25} = \sqrt{4.50} = 2.121$$

$$d(Q, P2) = \sqrt{(2.5 - 1.5)^2 + (2.5 - 1.5)^2} = \sqrt{1.00 + 1.00} = \sqrt{2.00} = 1.414$$

$$d(Q, P3) = \sqrt{(2.5 - 2.0)^2 + (2.5 - 2.0)^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.25} = \sqrt{0.50} = 0.707$$

$$d(Q, P4) = \sqrt{(2.5 - 3.0)^2 + (2.5 - 3.5)^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 1.00} = \sqrt{1.25} = 1.118$$

$$d(Q, P5) = \sqrt{(2.5 - 3.5)^2 + (2.5 - 4.0)^2 = \sqrt{1.00 + 2.25}} = \sqrt{3.25} = 1.803$$

### Step 2 — Sort Distances & Select k = 3 Nearest

| Rank | Patient | Distance | Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P3 | 0.707 | 0 (Benign) |
| 2 | P4 | 1.118 | 1 (Malignant) |
| 3 | P2 | 1.414 | 0 (Benign) |
| 4 | P5 | 1.803 | 1 (Malignant) |
| 5 | P1 | 2.121 | 0 (Benign) |

**Selected 3-NN:** $\{P3, P4, P2\}$ with class labels $\{0, 1, 0\}$.

### Step 3 — Majority Voting

Count of class 0: **2** (P3, P2). Count of class 1: **1** (P4).

$$\hat{y}_Q = \arg\max_c \; \#(y_i = c) = 0$$

### Step 4 — Final Prediction

$$\boxed{\text{Query patient Q is classified as BENIGN (Class 0)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Insight:** Notice how the decision is dominated by the closer neighbours. If we had used *weighted k-NN* with $w_i = 1/d_i^2$, the votes would become: P3: $1/0.5 = 2.00$, P4: $1/1.25 = 0.80$, P2: $1/2.00 = 0.50$. Class 0 still wins with $2.50$ vs $0.80$, but the margin widens.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison: LDA vs k-NN

| **Property** | **LDA** | **k-NN** |
|---|---|---|
| **Model Type** | Parametric, generative (Gaussian) | Non-parametric, instance-based |
| **Training Cost** | Compute $S_W$, $S_B$ — $O(ND^2)$ | Zero (lazy) |
| **Prediction Cost** | $O(D)$ per query | $O(ND)$ per query |
| **Decision Boundary** | Linear hyperplane | Arbitrary, non-linear (Voronoi) |
| **Handles $C > 2$?** | Yes — $C-1$ projections | Yes — multi-class voting |
| **Curse of Dimensionality** | Robust (projects down) | Severely affected (distances become uniform) |
| **Medical Use Case** | Brain MRI Alzheimer's classification | Skin lesion CAD systems |
| **Hyperparameter** | None (closed-form) | $k$ + distance metric |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Supervised vs Unsupervised Learning — High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Dataset] --> B{Data Labelled?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Supervised Learning Pipeline]
    B -- No --> D[Unsupervised Learning Pipeline]
    C --> C1[Train Model with x_i y_i pairs]
    C1 --> C2[Minimise Loss Function]
    C2 --> C3[Classifier or Regressor]
    C3 --> C4[Predict label y for new x]
    D --> D1[Discover Hidden Structure]
    D1 --> D2[Apply Algorithm KMeans PCA etc]
    D2 --> D3[Cluster Labels or Reduced Features]
    D3 --> D4[Group Similar Samples]
    C4 --> E[Output Predictions]
    D4 --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram shows the *divergent pipeline* from a common data source. The two paradigms split at the very first decision point — whether labels are present. This is a frequently-asked KTU diagram.

---

## 4.2 LDA Workflow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Labelled Medical Image Dataset] --> B[Feature Extraction]
    B --> C[Feature Matrix X of size N x D]
    C --> D[Compute Class Means mu1 mu2 ... muC]
    C --> E[Compute Within Class Scatter SW]
    C --> F[Compute Between Class Scatter SB]
    D --> G[Solve Generalised Eigenvalue Problem]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Optimal Projection Matrix W of size D x C-1]
    H --> I[Project Samples y = W transpose x]
    I --> J[Reduced Feature Space of dim C-1]
    J --> K[Apply Decision Rule]
    K --> L[Classify New Medical Image]
```

---

## 4.3 k-NN Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Query Sample xq] --> B[Load Training Set]
    B --> C[Compute Distance d xq xi for all i]
    C --> D[Sort Distances Ascending]
    D --> E[Select k Smallest Distances]
    E --> F[Retrieve Class Labels of k Neighbours]
    F --> G[Majority Vote or Weighted Vote]
    G --> H[Predicted Class Label yhat]
    H --> I[Output Diagnosis]
```

---

## 4.4 Voronoi Decision Regions (k = 1) — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Class 0 Sample 1] -.->|boundary| R1[Voronoi Cell 1]
    A2[Class 0 Sample 2] -.->|boundary| R2[Voronoi Cell 2]
    A3[Class 1 Sample 1] -.->|boundary| R3[Voronoi Cell 3]
    A4[Class 1 Sample 2] -.->|boundary| R4[Voronoi Cell 4]
    R1 --> X[Query Sample Lands in Cell 2]
    R2 --> X
    R3 --> X
    R4 --> X
    X --> P[Predicted Class 0]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Voronoi tessellation illustrates that the **entire feature plane is partitioned** by perpendicular bisectors of every pair of training samples. Every location in the plane is *owned* by its nearest training sample — this is the geometric essence of k = 1 classification.

---

## 4.5 End-to-End Medical Imaging Pipeline (Integrating All Concepts)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CT or MRI Scanner] --> B[Image Acquisition]
    B --> C[Preprocessing Filtering Segmentation]
    C --> D[Feature Extraction]
    D --> E{Choose Learning Paradigm}
    E -- Labels Available --> F[Supervised LDA or kNN]
    E -- No Labels --> G[Unsupervised KMeans or PCA]
    F --> H[Classify Lesion]
```

**Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised learning. Give one medical imaging example for each.**

|---|---|---|

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any three distance metrics used in the k-NN classifier. State the formula for each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Euclidean distance** (most common, $L_2$ norm):
$$d_E(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{D} (x_j - y_j)^2}$$

2. **Manhattan distance** ($L_1$ norm, city-block):
$$d_M(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{j=1}^{D} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$$

3. **Minkowski distance** (generalised, order $p$):
$$d_{\text{Mink}}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{D} \vert x_j - y_j \vert^p \right)^{1/p}, \quad p \geq 1$$

*(3 marks: 1 mark per metric with formula)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) with the Fisher criterion. Derive the optimal projection direction $\mathbf{w}$ for a two-class problem.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
LDA is a dimensionality-reduction technique that projects $D$-dimensional feature vectors onto a lower-dimensional subspace by maximising the ratio of between-class scatter to within-class scatter. It was introduced by **R. A. Fisher (1936)**.

**Scatter Matrices (2 marks):**
The within-class scatter matrix is:
$$S_W = \sum_{i=1}^{C} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in \omega_i} (\mathbf{x} - \mu_i)(\mathbf{x} - \mu_i)^T$$
where $\mu_i$ is the mean vector of class $\omega_i$.

The between-class scatter matrix is:
$$S_B = \sum_{i=1}^{C} N_i (\mu_i - \bar{\mu})(\mu_i - \bar{\mu})^T$$
where $\bar{\mu}$ is the global mean.

**Derivation of $\mathbf{w}$ (3 marks):**
Fisher's criterion for the 2-class case:
$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T S_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^T S_W \mathbf{w}}$$

Constraining $\mathbf{w}^T S_W \mathbf{w} = c$ and using Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$:
$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^T S_B \mathbf{w} - \lambda(\mathbf{w}^T S_W \mathbf{w} - c)$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$ and setting to zero:
$$2 S_B \mathbf{w} - 2 \lambda S_W \mathbf{w} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad S_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda S_W \mathbf{w}$$

Substituting $S_B = (\mu_1 - \mu_2)(\mu_1 - \mu_2)^T$ and solving:
$$\boxed{\mathbf{w} = S_W^{-1}(\mu_1 - \mu_2)}$$

This is the optimal direction that maximises Fisher's criterion.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the role of Discriminant Analysis in medical image classification. Mention the assumptions and limitations.**

**Model Solution:**

**Role in Medical Imaging (3 marks):**
1. **Tissue characterisation:** LDA classifies tissues (e.g., *grey matter*, *white matter*, *CSF*) in brain MRI based on intensity features.
2. **Tumour grading:** Used to discriminate *low-grade* vs *high-grade* gliomas on multimodal MRI (T1, T2, FLAIR, DWI).
3. **CAD systems:** Acts as a fast, interpretable classifier in Computer-Aided Detection of breast cancer, lung nodules, and diabetic retinopathy.
4. **Dimensionality reduction:** Reduces high-dimensional radiomic features (often $>$ 100) to $C-1$ discriminative directions.

**Key Assumptions (2 marks):**
- Each class follows a **multivariate Gaussian distribution**.
- All classes share a **common covariance matrix** $\Sigma$ (this is what makes the discriminant *linear*).
- Features are **continuous** and **linearly separable** in the projected space.
- Training data is **labelled and balanced** (or balanced via class priors).

**Limitations (2 marks):**
- Violation of Gaussianity or equal-covariance assumption degrades performance.
- Cannot model complex **non-linear** decision boundaries.
- Sensitive to the **small-sample-size problem** — if $N < D$, $S_W$ becomes singular (requires regularisation).
- LDA projects onto at most $C-1$ dimensions, which may be insufficient for highly complex datasets (e.g., histopathology images).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the k-Nearest Neighbour (k-NN) classification algorithm. Discuss the effect of parameter $k$ on classifier performance.**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm (3 marks):**
k-NN is a **lazy, non-parametric** supervised classifier. Given:
- A training set $T = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$
- A query sample $\mathbf{x}_q$
- A chosen $k$ and distance metric $d(\cdot, \cdot)$

The classification procedure is:
1. Compute $d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i)$ for all $i = 1, \ldots, N$.
2. Sort distances in ascending order.
3. Select the $k$ training samples with the smallest distances.
4. Apply **majority voting** among their labels to obtain $\hat{y}_q$.

**Effect of $k$ (4 marks):**

| Value of $k$ | Behaviour | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| $k = 1$ | **High variance**, low bias | Decision boundary overfits to noise; sensitive to outliers |
| Small $k$ (e.g., 3) | Flexible boundary | Captures local structure; still noisy |
| Moderate $k$ (e.g., 5–7) | **Bias-variance trade-off** | Generally optimal; smoother boundary |
| Large $k$ (e.g., $\sqrt{N}$) | **High bias**, low variance | Boundary too smooth; may underfit, ignores local patterns |
| $k = N$ | Predicts global majority class | No learning, useless |

**Selection of $k$:** Typically chosen by **cross-validation** on a validation set. Heuristic: $k \approx \sqrt{N}$. Always prefer **odd $k$** for binary classification to avoid ties.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**With a suitable example, illustrate the application of the k-NN algorithm in medical image classification. Mention the limitations of k-NN in medical imaging.**

**Model Solution:**

**Application Example (4 marks):**
Consider a **Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system for skin lesion classification** (benign nevus vs malignant melanoma):
- Each lesion image is converted to a feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^D$ using the **ABCD rule** — Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Colour variation, Diameter — plus texture features (GLCM, Gabor).
- A dermatologist-labelled training set of 1000 lesions is available.
- For a new patient's lesion, the system computes the Euclidean distance to all 1000 training lesions, selects the $k = 7$ nearest, and outputs the majority class.
- Accuracy in published studies (Codella et al., 2018) reaches 75–82% for melanoma detection, often combined with deep learning for state-of-the-art performance.

**Limitations in Medical Imaging (3 marks):**
1. **Curse of Dimensionality:** Radiomic features are often $D > 100$; in high-D, *all distances become approximately equal*, making "nearest neighbours" meaningless.
2. **Computational cost at inference:** $O(ND)$ per query is infeasible for real-time clinical use with large $N$; requires tree-based acceleration (KD-Tree, Ball-Tree).
3. **Class imbalance:** Medical datasets are often imbalanced (e.g., 1 malignant per 1000 benign). k-NN biased toward the majority class; needs **SMOTE** or weighted voting.
4. **Feature scaling sensitivity:** Features with larger numerical ranges dominate the distance calculation — must standardise (z-score) before applying.
5. **No interpretability:** Unlike decision trees or rule-based systems, k-NN cannot explain *why* a lesion was classified as malignant — problematic in clinical settings.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps (Read Before Writing Your Exam):**
>
> 1. **Confusing LDA with PCA:** Many students write PCA's objective (maximise total variance) when the question asks for LDA. **LDA uses class labels; PCA does not.** Examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the constraint on Fisher's criterion:** When deriving $\mathbf{w}$, students often just differentiate $J(\mathbf{w})$ directly and forget to apply the Lagrange constraint $\mathbf{w}^T S_W \mathbf{w} = c$. This makes the derivation *mathematically invalid* — 2 marks lost.
>
> 3. **Skipping the vote count in k-NN:** In numerical problems, students often list the distances and *jump directly* to the final class without explicitly stating the majority count. Always show the table of $k$ nearest neighbours and tally the class votes.
>
> 4. **Choosing $k$ as an even number in 2-class problems:** When $k = 4$ and the votes are 2-2, a tie occurs. Examiners deduct marks if you don't justify choosing an **odd** $k$.
>
> 5. **Mixing up the scatter matrix formulas:** $S_W$ uses *deviation from class mean*; $S_B$ uses *deviation of class mean from global mean*. Confusing the two is the most common derivation error.
>
> 6. **Not normalising features in k-NN:** If you don't standardise features, distance is dominated by the largest-scale feature. Examiners expect you to mention **z-score normalisation** $\tilde{x} = (x - \mu)/\sigma$ as a preprocessing step.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU Module 4 — Last-Minute Revision Bullet Bank**
>
> ✅ **Supervised Learning** = labelled data → learns $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$. Examples: LDA, k-NN, SVM, CNN.
>
> ✅ **Unsupervised Learning** = unlabelled data → discovers hidden structure. Examples: K-Means, PCA, Autoencoders.
>
> ✅ **Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)** finds projection $\mathbf{w}$ maximising **Fisher's criterion** $J(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^T S_B \mathbf{w} / \mathbf{w}^T S_W \mathbf{w}$.
>
> ✅ **Optimal LDA direction (2-class):** $\mathbf{w}^* = S_W^{-1}(\mu_1 - \mu_2)$ — derived from the **generalised eigenvalue problem** $S_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda S_W \mathbf{w}$.
>
> ✅ **LDA produces $C-1$ discriminant directions** for $C$ classes — projects to at most $C-1$ dimensions.
>
> ✅ **k-NN is a lazy, non-parametric, instance-based** classifier — no training phase, all work at inference.
>
> ✅ **k-NN majority voting:** query assigned to most-frequent class among $k$ nearest training samples.
>
> ✅ **Always choose odd $k$** for binary classification to avoid ties.
>
> ✅ **Always normalise features** (z-score) before computing distances in k-NN.
>
> ✅ **Euclidean distance** is the default; alternatives include Manhattan, Minkowski, Cosine.
>
> ✅ **Trade-off in k-NN:** Small $k$ → low bias, high variance (overfit); large $k$ → high bias, low variance (underfit).
>
> ✅ **LDA assumptions:** multivariate Gaussian, equal covariance, labelled data, continuous features.
>
> ✅ **LDA limitations:** fails on non-linear boundaries; small sample size → singular $S_W$ (use regularised LDA).
>
> ✅ **k-NN limitations:** curse of dimensionality, high inference cost, sensitive to noise and irrelevant features, no interpretability.
>
> ✅ **Medical imaging applications of LDA:** brain tissue segmentation, Alzheimer's grading, radiomic feature reduction, tumour classification.
>
> ✅ **Medical imaging applications of k-NN:** skin lesion CAD, breast cancer diagnosis, lung nodule classification, retinopathy screening.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
